title: Physics-guided Convolutional Neural Network for Domain Growth Prediction in Systems with Conserved Kinetics
summary: The spatiotemporal evolution of many physical, chemical, and biological systems is described by nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs). Recently, deep neural network-based surrogate models have gained increasing interest as efficient alternatives to computationally expensive traditional numerical solvers. In this work, we propose an attention-based, physics-guided convolutional neural network as a surrogate model to learn the microstructural evolution of such systems. We train the model to accurately predict the full time-evolution of phase separation in binary mixtures governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation. We show that predictions from our trained surrogate model remain stable and accurate over long-time rollouts for both critical and off-critical mixtures and preserve the mixture composition throughout evolution. We also show that our model accurately captures the growth of domain size and is consistent with the Lifshitz-Slyozov domain-growth law. The prediction results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework for modeling systems with conserved kinetics and can be extended to other complex dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an attention-based, physics-guided convolutional neural network as a surrogate model to predict the microstructural evolution of phase separation in binary mixtures governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation. The model accurately predicts long-time evolution, preserves mixture composition, and is consistent with the Lifshitz-Slyozov domain-growth law.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear PDEs describing spatiotemporal evolution in physical, chemical, and biological systems are computationally expensive to solve with traditional numerical solvers, motivating the need for efficient deep neural network-based surrogate models.
AI.method: An attention-based, physics-guided convolutional neural network trained as a surrogate model to learn and predict the full time-evolution of phase separation in binary mixtures governed by the Cahn-Hilliard equation.
AI.result: Predictions remain stable and accurate over long-time rollouts for both critical and off-critical mixtures, preserve mixture composition, and accurately capture domain growth consistent with the Lifshitz-Slyozov domain-growth law.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively models systems with conserved kinetics and can be extended to other complex dynamical systems.

title: \chisao{}: A GPU-Native Parallel Optimizer for Multimodal Black-Box Functions via Convergence-Anticonvergence Oscillation
summary: Finding all modes of a multimodal black-box function is a fundamental challenge in optimization, Bayesian inference, and scientific computing. Existing approaches -- basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent -- operate sequentially and cannot exploit the massive parallelism of modern GPU hardware. We introduce \chisao{} (\textbf{C}onvergence-\textbf{H}alt-\textbf{I}nvert-\textbf{S}tick-\textbf{A}nd-\textbf{O}scillate), a GPU-native population optimizer that runs an entire sample batch simultaneously and exploits a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle to escape local traps while freezing confirmed modes. The structural move is asymmetric: samples that reach true peaks are frozen (``stuck'') and preserved, while the rest keep exploring via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via two complementary strategies (Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster) maintains population diversity throughout. On all 42 functions of the Simon Fraser University optimization benchmark suite across dimensions $d \in \{2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$, \chisao{} achieves \textbf{100\%} mode recovery where all CPU baselines collapse at $d \geq 8$ on the hardest multimodal functions, at up to \textbf{$34\times$} speedup over basin-hopping on functions where all methods succeed (Michalewicz $d=64$) and up to \textbf{$39\times$} on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid $d=64$, pure GPU dividend). All benchmarks evaluate the objective by value alone -- gradients come from finite differences -- so the reported speedups are a derivative-free worst case. Under substantial likelihood noise ($σ_{\mathrm{noise}}$ up to 1.0), mode detection remains 100\% reliable. The algorithm is available as a standalone open-source Python package on PyPI.

AI.tldr: CHISAO is a GPU-native population optimizer that achieves 100% mode recovery on all 42 SFU benchmark functions (d=2..64) with up to 39× speedup over CPU baselines, using a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle to escape local traps while freezing confirmed modes.
AI.motivation: Finding all modes of multimodal black-box functions is fundamental but existing methods (basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent) operate sequentially and cannot leverage GPU parallelism, limiting scalability for modern hardware.
AI.method: CHISAO runs an entire sample batch simultaneously on GPU using an asymmetric convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle: samples reaching true peaks are frozen ("stuck") and preserved while others explore via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster strategies maintains diversity.
AI.result: 100% mode recovery on all 42 SFU benchmark functions (d=2..64), including functions where CPU baselines collapse at d≥8. Up to 34× speedup over basin-hopping on multimodal functions (Michalewicz d=64) and up to 39× on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid d=64). 100% reliable mode detection under likelihood noise σ_noise up to 1.0.
AI.conclusion: CHISAO demonstrates that deliberate convergence-anticonvergence cycling on GPU enables derivative-free multimodal optimization with superior mode recovery and massive speedups, available as open-source Python package.

title: Implementation of reinforcement learning in chemical reaction networks: application to phototaxis as curiosity-driven exploration
comment: This paper is accepted as talk at ALIFE 2026 (Waterloo, Canada)
summary: Living systems navigate environments using noisy and incomplete sensory signals. In unicellular algae, phototaxis is often modeled as a mechanistic run--tumble process driven by stimulus--response rules. However, such descriptions overlook how organisms actively sample their environment to reduce sensory ambiguity. From a minimal cognition perspective, we reframe this navigation as a subjective, information-driven sensorimotor process. To this end, we propose a framework linking a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with biochemical reaction dynamics. Environmental variables are hidden, while the cell updates a minimal internal state from each observation through a memoryless Bayesian step. These internal dynamics balance orienting toward light with exploratory reorientation and can be implemented through Chemical-Reaction-Network Ordinary Differential Equations (CRN--ODEs). Our model includes a biophysical observation process for photoreception and a chemically computable polynomial bound on information gain. Using Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) on 30 experimentally recorded Chlamydomonas trajectories, we infer the behavioral objective consistent with observed phototactic motion and benchmark the resulting dynamics with standard Stochastic Simulation Algorithm (SSA) baselines. Our model reproduces the empirical alignment-to-light distribution, comparable to objective SSA baselines on this dataset. Within this framework, run--tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy: tumbling reorients the cell to sample new sensory configurations and resolve sensor ambiguity, demonstrating how intracellular biochemical networks can support adaptive information-seeking behavior in cellular navigation.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes phototaxis in unicellular algae as an information-driven sensorimotor process using a POMDP framework with biochemical reaction networks, showing that run-tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy to resolve sensory ambiguity.
AI.motivation: Existing mechanistic models of phototaxis (e.g., run-tumble processes with stimulus-response rules) overlook how organisms actively sample their environment to reduce sensory ambiguity. The authors aim to reframe navigation from a minimal cognition perspective as a subjective, information-driven process.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework linking a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with biochemical reaction dynamics (CRN-ODEs). The cell updates a minimal internal state via memoryless Bayesian updates. They include a biophysical observation process for photoreception and a chemically computable polynomial bound on information gain. Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) is applied to 30 experimentally recorded Chlamydomonas trajectories to infer the behavioral objective. Benchmarking is done with Stochastic Simulation Algorithm (SSA) baselines.
AI.result: The model reproduces the empirical alignment-to-light distribution, comparable to objective SSA baselines on the dataset. Run-tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy: tumbling reorients the cell to sample new sensory configurations and resolve sensor ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Intracellular biochemical networks can support adaptive information-seeking behavior in cellular navigation, demonstrating that seemingly random tumbling is an active strategy to reduce sensory uncertainty rather than mere noise.

title: Neural Speaker Diarization via Multilingual Training: Evaluation on Low-Resource Nepali-Hindi Speech
summary:
Speaker diarization, the task of determining "who spoke when" in a multi-speaker recording, is a critical component in applications such as meeting transcription, accessibility tools, and multilingual information retrieval. While end-to-end neural diarization systems have achieved strong performance for English and other high-resource languages, their effectiveness degrades substantially for underrepresented languages where annotated speech data is scarce.
  This paper investigates speaker diarization for low-resource Nepali-Hindi speech through a multilingual training approach, comparing two modern architectures: EEND with encoder-decoder attractors (EEND-EDA) and EEND with Perceiver-based attractors (DiaPer). Both models are trained on a multilingual corpus combining English speech from LibriSpeech, diverse speaker recordings from VoxCeleb, and separately collected Nepali and Hindi audio, a setup designed to reduce language bias and encourage cross-lingual generalization. We evaluate both models across 2-speaker, 3-speaker, 4-speaker, and mixed-speaker scenarios on LibriSpeech, VoxCeleb, and Nepali-Hindi (NeHi) test sets. DiaPer achieves stronger overall performance than EEND-EDA, particularly in more challenging multi-speaker conditions, obtaining DERs of 3.28%, 2.02%, 4.05%, and 4.76% on NeHi 2-speaker, 3-speaker, 4-speaker, and mixed-speaker settings, respectively, compared to 1.50%, 9.68%, 16.17%, and 11.19% for EEND-EDA. These results demonstrate the viability of Perceiver-based end-to-end neural diarization for low-resource multilingual speech processing.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates multilingual speaker diarization for low-resource Nepali-Hindi speech by comparing two end-to-end neural architectures—EEND-EDA and DiaPer (Perceiver-based)—trained on a multilingual corpus. DiaPer significantly outperforms EEND-EDA, especially in challenging multi-speaker scenarios, achieving substantially lower Diarization Error Rates (DERs) on Nepali-Hindi test sets.
AI.motivation: End-to-end neural diarization systems perform well for high-resource languages like English but degrade substantially for underrepresented languages due to scarce annotated speech data. The paper aims to address this gap by investigating speaker diarization for low-resource Nepali-Hindi speech.
AI.method: Two architectures are compared: EEND with encoder-decoder attractors (EEND-EDA) and EEND with Perceiver-based attractors (DiaPer). Both models are trained on a multilingual corpus combining LibriSpeech (English), VoxCeleb (diverse speakers), and separately collected Nepali-Hindi audio. Evaluation is conducted across 2-speaker, 3-speaker, 4-speaker, and mixed-speaker scenarios.
AI.result: DiaPer achieves DERs of 3.28%, 2.02%, 4.05%, and 4.76% on NeHi 2/3/4/mixed-speaker settings, versus EEND-EDA's 1.50%, 9.68%, 16.17%, and 11.19%. DiaPer outperforms EEND-EDA overall, particularly in more challenging multi-speaker conditions.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate the viability of Perceiver-based end-to-end neural diarization for low-resource multilingual speech processing.

title: Neural Architecture Search for Generative Adversarial Networks: A Comprehensive Review and Critical Analysis
summary: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) has emerged as a pivotal technique in optimizing the design of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), automating the search for effective architectures while addressing the challenges inherent in manual design. This paper provides a comprehensive review of NAS methods applied to GANs, categorizing and comparing various approaches based on criteria such as search strategies, evaluation metrics, and performance outcomes. The review highlights the benefits of NAS in improving GAN performance, stability, and efficiency, while also identifying limitations and areas for future research. Key findings include the superiority of evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based methods in certain contexts, the importance of robust evaluation metrics beyond traditional scores like Inception Score (IS) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and the need for diverse datasets in assessing GAN performance. By presenting a structured comparison of existing NAS-GAN techniques, this paper aims to guide researchers in developing more effective NAS methods and advancing the field of GANs.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive review of Neural Architecture Search methods applied to Generative Adversarial Networks, categorizing approaches by search strategies, evaluation metrics, and performance outcomes.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of manual GAN design by systematically reviewing NAS methods that automate architecture search for GANs, and to provide guidance for developing more effective NAS-GAN techniques.
AI.method: Categorization and comparison of NAS methods for GANs based on search strategies (e.g., evolutionary algorithms, gradient-based methods), evaluation metrics (IS, FID, and more robust alternatives), and performance outcomes.
AI.result: Evolutionary algorithms and gradient-based methods show superiority in certain contexts; conventional metrics like IS and FID have limitations, highlighting the need for more robust evaluation metrics and diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: NAS significantly improves GAN performance, stability, and efficiency, but further research is needed on evaluation metrics, dataset diversity, and addressing current limitations to advance the field.

title: Soroll-IA: A Weakly Labeled Audio Dataset for Real-World Industrial Port Monitoring
comment: Paper being under review at Journal on Audio, Speech, and Music Processing
summary: Soroll-IA is a weakly labeled environmental audio dataset recorded in a real-world industrial port environment in Valencia (Spain) using two fixed sensing nodes. The dataset comprises approximately 22 hours of audio segmented into 7,396 clips and covers 26 sound event classes representative of industrial port acoustic activity commonly observed in such environments, such as crane sirens, train movements, traffic, and other logistical and industrial sounds. Recordings were captured under highly challenging acoustic conditions, including strong background noise, long-distance sources, and frequent event overlap. All audio clips were annotated by domain experts following a weak labeling strategy, where tags indicate the presence of sound events within a clip without temporal localization. To account for inter-annotator variability, two ground-truth versions are released: one without cross-validation, where a class is considered present if annotated by at least one expert, and a second, more conservative version based on cross-validation, where agreement by at least two-thirds of the annotators is required. The dataset is intended to support research in audio tagging, weakly supervised sound event detection, and machine learning under realistic industrial acoustic conditions. Benchmark results are provided using two complementary architectures: CNN14 representing high-capacity convolutional models for audio tagging, and MobileNetV2, selected for its suitability in real-time classification on low-resource edge devices. To the best of current knowledge, Soroll-IA constitutes an available dataset dedicated exclusively to industrial port acoustic environments, aiming to foster advances in robust environmental sound analysis for safety-critical and operational monitoring applications. The dataset is available online and collected under Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International license.

AI.tldr: Soroll-IA is a ~22-hour, 7,396-clip weakly labeled environmental audio dataset from an industrial port in Valencia, Spain, comprising 26 sound event classes relevant to port operations. Recorded under challenging acoustic conditions, it provides two ground-truth versions (permissive and cross-validated) and benchmark results using CNN14 and MobileNetV2, aiming to support audio tagging, weakly supervised sound event detection, and robust machine learning in industrial settings.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of publicly available, weakly labeled environmental audio datasets dedicated to industrial port acoustic environments, enabling research in robust sound analysis for safety-critical and operational monitoring applications under realistic, challenging acoustic conditions.
AI.method: Audio was captured via two fixed sensing nodes in a real-world port environment. 7,396 clips (~22 hours) were weakly annotated by domain experts for 26 sound event classes. Two ground-truth versions are released: a permissive version (class present if ≥1 annotator marks it) and a conservative cross-validated version (≥2/3 agreement). Benchmarking was performed with CNN14 (high-capacity audio tagging) and MobileNetV2 (real-time, low-resource edge inference).
AI.result: Benchmark results using CNN14 and MobileNetV2 are provided, establishing baselines for audio tagging and weakly supervised sound event detection on this dataset. The dataset is released under an Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International license.
AI.conclusion: Soroll-IA serves as the first publicly available dataset exclusively targeting industrial port acoustic environments, offering realistic recording conditions, weak labels with inter-annotator variability handling, and baselines to foster progress in robust environmental sound analysis for monitoring and safety applications.

title: HiPR: Hierarchical Progressive Rendering for Immediate Feedback
summary: Hierarchical Progressive Rendering (HiPR) is a dynamic render-scheduling algorithm that makes interactive path tracing finally feel real-time. While most renderers recompute the entire frame after any change to the scene, our method updates some of the pixels based on a priority order while keeping the others unchanged. Rather than relying on error-driven or temporal reuse heuristics, it amortizes rendering costs by organizing pixels into a hierarchy of light-path dependencies from changed elements outward, prioritizing by perceptual impact and delivering instant visual feedback, while eventually converging to an unbiased result.

AI.tldr: HiPR is a dynamic render-scheduling algorithm for interactive path tracing that prioritizes pixel updates based on a hierarchy of light-path dependencies and perceptual impact, delivering instant visual feedback while converging to an unbiased result.
AI.motivation: Interactive path tracing typically recomputes the entire frame after any scene change, which is computationally expensive and prevents real-time feel. The motivation is to avoid full-frame recomputation and instead provide immediate visual feedback by selectively updating pixels.
AI.method: HiPR organizes pixels into a hierarchy of light-path dependencies originating from changed scene elements, prioritizes updates by perceptual impact, and keeps unchanged pixels intact, amortizing rendering costs over time without relying on error-driven or temporal reuse heuristics.
AI.result: The method delivers instant visual feedback during interactive editing, making path tracing feel real-time, while eventually converging to an unbiased, correct result.
AI.conclusion: HiPR demonstrates that a priority-based, hierarchical scheduling approach can achieve real-time responsiveness in interactive path tracing without sacrificing final image quality or unbiased convergence.

title: Orchestrating Black-Box Schema Converters: An Empirical Study of Automated, Quality-Ranked Conversion Across Heterogeneous Schema Languages
summary: Modern software systems routinely need the same data model in several schema languages: a model may exist as JSON Schema for a web API, as XSD for data exchange, and as SHACL for a knowledge graph. Keeping these representations consistent as the model evolves is a recurring construction and maintenance burden, because converters between schema languages are hard to find, scattered across ecosystems, of uneven quality, and frequently lossy. We study, empirically, to what extent such imperfect, heterogeneous converters can be orchestrated into automated, reproducible, and quality-ranked conversions, and where the current converter landscape reaches its limits. Our approach models schema languages as nodes and converters, treated as black boxes, as directed edges, so that conversions become paths that are discovered, executed, ranked, and reported with full per-step provenance, with failures handled by trying alternatives. We realize it as the open-source Schema Conversion Orchestrator, integrate it into MetaConfigurator, and evaluate it on 60 conversion tasks built from real-world schemas across five schema languages, using agent-assisted, human-reviewed quality annotations. Orchestration surfaces a usable result for 43 of 60 tasks; the remaining failures localize concrete gaps in the converter landscape. We discuss implications for tool builders and for measuring conversion quality.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an orchestration framework that treats schema language converters as black-box edges in a graph, enabling automated, reproducible, and quality-ranked conversions across schema languages (JSON Schema, XSD, SHACL, etc.). Evaluated on 60 real-world tasks, it yields usable results for 43 tasks and identifies concrete gaps in the converter landscape.
AI.motivation: Modern software systems need the same data model represented in multiple schema languages (e.g., JSON Schema, XSD, SHACL), but existing converters are heterogeneous, lossy, scattered across ecosystems, and hard to find or reuse, creating recurring maintenance burden.
AI.method: The approach models schema languages as nodes and converters as directed edges (black boxes), treating conversions as paths to be discovered, executed, ranked, and reported with per-step provenance; failures trigger alternative path exploration. Realized as the open-source Schema Conversion Orchestrator, integrated into MetaConfigurator, and evaluated with agent-assisted, human-reviewed quality annotations on 60 conversion tasks across five schema languages.
AI.result: Orchestration yields a usable result for 43 out of 60 conversion tasks; the remaining 17 failures pinpoint concrete gaps in the current converter landscape.
AI.conclusion: The orchestration approach effectively surfaces conversions despite imperfect converters, but the landscape has significant gaps. Implications for tool builders and measuring conversion quality are discussed.

title: DocArena: Turning Raw Documents into Controllable Training Environments for Document Search Agents
summary: Recent methods train search agents via reinforcement learning from (question, answer, evidence) tuples without requiring expert trajectories. The tuples serve as the training environment, and whose properties directly shape what search strategies and generalization abilities the agent can develop. While prior works have made encouraging progress in improving training data quality, existing environments remain predominantly text-based and existing approaches can struggle to construct training environments that are controllable, scalable, and account for multimodal data. Given this, we propose DocArena, a fully automated data curation pipeline building on the practical need for multimodal document search and question-answering. It transforms raw document collections into training environments for search agents without any human annotation. The pipeline first structures and indexes documents through MLLM-based visual perception, then profiles and leverage the cross-page information distribution to construct reasoning-intensive QA pairs, as well as performs cascaded quality assurance operations via MLLM. We introduce DocArena-79K with QA pairs from 8,336 documents spanning 16 domains and 49 languages. We further design a Doc-Search agent infrastructure that decouples visual perception from the policy model, allowing text-based LLMs to serve as the reasoning backbone for multimodal document retrieval and QA. Under a unified evaluation framework where only the policy model differs, experiments on six multimodal document scenarios and seven text-based QA benchmarks show that agents trained on DocArena data achieve the best performance on both retrieval accuracy and QA quality. Further analysis on agent search behaviors confirms the effectiveness and controllability of the constructed training environment.

AI.tldr: DocArena is a fully automated pipeline that transforms raw multimodal documents into training environments for search agents via MLLM-based visual perception, cross-page QA construction, and cascaded quality assurance. It produces DocArena-79K (8,336 documents, 16 domains, 49 languages) and a Doc-Search agent that decouples visual perception from policy models, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multimodal and text-based QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing training environments for search agents are predominantly text-based, lacking controllability, scalability, and multimodal support. Prior approaches struggle to construct training environments that fully account for multimodal data without human annotation.
AI.method: DocArena pipeline: (1) MLLM-based visual perception for structuring/indexing documents, (2) cross-page information distribution profiling for reasoning-intensive QA pair construction, (3) cascaded quality assurance via MLLM. The Doc-Search agent decouples visual perception from the policy model, enabling text-based LLMs as the reasoning backbone for multimodal retrieval and QA.
AI.result: Under a unified evaluation framework, agents trained on DocArena data achieve best performance on both retrieval accuracy and QA quality across six multimodal document scenarios and seven text-based QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DocArena effectively constructs controllable, scalable multimodal training environments for search agents without human annotation, and the proposed agent architecture successfully decouples visual perception from policy reasoning.

title: Detecting and Controlling Sycophancy with Cascading Linear Features
summary: Interpreting and controlling model behaviors through activation steering methods requires many pairs of contrastive samples that clearly exhibit desired or undesired behavior. These data pairs determine the degree to which interpretability frameworks can reliably detect model features responsible for a behavior, and therefore the ability to steer models toward or away from such behavior. In this work, we present an iterative data generation pipeline that isolates cascading linear features responsible for a behavior. Specifically, we show how moving beyond simple binary pairs of samples, and instead isolating samples that show degrees of features that scale linearly with behavior, allows for better disentanglement of features. We focus on detecting and steering away from sycophancy -- the tendency of language models to prioritize user validation. We demonstrate that sycophancy features discovered through cascading samples form linearly separable subspaces, and allow for selection of model activations that more clearly correspond to the desired behavior than baseline approaches. We also evaluate their ability to enable detection, deterministic scoring, and robust steering, and see that they either match or outperform LLM-as-a-judge and system prompting baselines while providing lower computational demand and more interpretability guarantees. Code & Data: https://cascading-feats.github.io/

AI.tldr: This paper presents an iterative data generation pipeline that isolates cascading linear features for better activation steering, demonstrating improved detection, scoring, and steering of sycophancy in language models compared to baseline approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering methods rely on binary contrastive sample pairs, which are insufficient for reliably detecting and disentangling features responsible for specific model behaviors.
AI.method: An iterative data generation pipeline that isolates cascading linear features by moving beyond binary pairs to samples showing degrees of features that scale linearly with behavior, applied to detect and steer away from sycophancy.
AI.result: Sycophancy features discovered through cascading samples form linearly separable subspaces, enable better selection of model activations, and match or outperform LLM-as-a-judge and system prompting baselines with lower computational demand and more interpretability guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Using cascading, linearly-scaled contrastive samples improves feature disentanglement for activation steering, offering a more efficient and interpretable alternative to standard baselines for controlling model behaviors like sycophancy.

title: An Evaluation of Decentralized Group Formation Techniques for Flying Light Specks
comment: Appeared in ACM Multimedia Asia 2023 (MMAsia '23), December 06-08, 2023, Tainan, Taiwan. ACM, New York, NY, USA, 7 pages
summary: Group formation is fundamental for 3D displays that use Flying Light Specks, FLSs, to illuminate shapes and provide haptic interactions. An FLS is a drone with light sources that illuminates a shape. Groups of G FLSs may implement reliability techniques to tolerate FLS failures, provide kinesthetic haptic feedback in response to a user's touch, and facilitate a divide and conquer approach to challenges such as localizing FLSs to render a shape. This paper evaluates four decentralized techniques to form groups. An FLS implements a technique autonomously using asynchronous communication and without a global clock. We evaluate these techniques using synthetic point clouds with known optimal solutions and real point clouds. Obtained results show a technique named Random Subset (RS) is superior when constructing small groups (G $\leq$ 5) while a different technique named Closest Available Neighbor First (CANF) is superior when constructing large groups (G $\geq$ 10).

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates four decentralized group formation techniques for Flying Light Specks (FLSs) in 3D displays, finding Random Subset (RS) best for small groups (G ≤ 5) and Closest Available Neighbor First (CANF) best for large groups (G ≥ 10).
AI.motivation: Group formation is essential for 3D displays using Flying Light Specks (FLSs) to enable reliability, haptic feedback, and divide-and-conquer localization. Decentralized autonomous group formation without global clocks is needed.
AI.method: Four decentralized group formation techniques are evaluated using synthetic point clouds with known optimal solutions and real point clouds. FLSs operate autonomously using asynchronous communication without a global clock.
AI.result: Random Subset (RS) performs best for small groups (G ≤ 5), while Closest Available Neighbor First (CANF) performs best for large groups (G ≥ 10).
AI.conclusion: Different decentralized group formation techniques are optimal depending on group size, with RS for small groups and CANF for large groups.

title: KG-TRACE: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Mechanistic Grounding in Antimicrobial Resistance Prediction
summary:
While WGS-based AMR prediction has reached high accuracy, existing models lack a mechanism to ground neural attributions in established biological pathways. We present KG-TRACE, a novel neuro-symbolic framework that integrates the WHO mutation knowledge graph (KG) as a structured biological constraint on a neural genomic model. Unlike existing methods that learn statistical patterns in isolation, KG-TRACE fuses genomic features and RotatE-based KG embeddings through a learned epistemic trust gate, dynamically weighting neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge.
  Evaluated on the CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort, KG-TRACE achieves an AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid, achieving competitive accuracy while its primary value lies in symbolic grounding, not predictive uplift. More importantly, we introduce the Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR), a dataset-level metric that quantifies alignment between neural attributions and established biology. Our framework achieves a 92.5% symbolic coverage of isoniazid-resistant predictions and effectively identifies MDR co-occurrence artifacts by issuing laboratory follow-up flags for 'UNCERTAIN' cases. We demonstrate that neuro-symbolic grounding provides a verifiable audit trail for clinicians, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and clinical trust.

AI.tldr: KG-TRACE is a neuro-symbolic framework that integrates WHO mutation knowledge graphs with neural genomic models via a learned epistemic trust gate, achieving high AUROC (0.9760 for isoniazid) on M. tuberculosis AMR prediction while introducing the Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR) metric to quantify alignment between neural attributions and established biology (92.5% symbolic coverage).
AI.motivation: Existing WGS-based AMR models achieve high accuracy but lack mechanisms to ground neural attributions in established biological pathways, limiting clinical trust and verifiability.
AI.method: KG-TRACE integrates WHO mutation knowledge graph embeddings (via RotatE) with neural genomic features through a learned epistemic trust gate that dynamically weights neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge.
AI.result: AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid on CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort; 92.5% symbolic coverage of isoniazid-resistant predictions; effective identification of MDR co-occurrence artifacts with laboratory follow-up flags for 'UNCERTAIN' cases.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-symbolic grounding provides a verifiable audit trail for clinicians, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and clinical trust; primary value lies in symbolic grounding rather than predictive uplift.

title: Listening Like a Judge: A Music-Aware Framework for Automatic Singing Performance Evaluation
comment: Accepted at Interspeech 2026. Supplementary material: https://neelam472.github.io/MusicJudge/Supp.pdf (backup mirror: https://sourav-ghosh.github.io/MusicJudge/Supp.pdf )
summary: Automatic singing quality assessment (SQA) requires evaluating lyrical correctness and musical fidelity while handling expressive variations. However, existing systems largely rely on either acoustic cues or lyric transcriptions exclusively, limiting holistic performance evaluation. Furthermore, their integration is non-trivial due to challenges in robust singing transcription amid melisma, vibrato, and tempo elasticity. To this end, we propose MusicJudge, a modality-guided framework for automated SQA that performs block-aligned multimodal analysis by coupling lyric correctness with pitch-rhythm fidelity. It detects semantically meaningful lyric blocks using multi-signal matching that integrates semantic embeddings, lexical similarity, and phonetic alignment. To improve singing audio transcription, we introduce Modality-Guided LoRA for ASR fine-tuning. Experiments across datasets demonstrate strong agreement with human expert judgments and validate the generalizability of MusicJudge.

AI.tldr: MusicJudge is a modality-guided framework for automatic singing quality assessment that performs block-aligned multimodal analysis coupling lyric correctness with pitch-rhythm fidelity, using multi-signal matching for lyric block detection and Modality-Guided LoRA for improved ASR fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing SQA systems rely solely on either acoustic cues or lyric transcriptions, limiting holistic evaluation, and their integration is difficult due to challenges in robust singing transcription caused by melisma, vibrato, and tempo elasticity.
AI.method: Proposes MusicJudge, a modality-guided framework that performs block-aligned multimodal analysis by: (1) detecting semantically meaningful lyric blocks via multi-signal matching integrating semantic embeddings, lexical similarity, and phonetic alignment; (2) introducing Modality-Guided LoRA for ASR fine-tuning to improve singing audio transcription.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong agreement with human expert judgments and validates generalizability across datasets.
AI.conclusion: MusicJudge effectively addresses the limitations of unimodal SQA systems by integrating lyric correctness with pitch-rhythm fidelity through modality-guided multimodal analysis, achieving human-level assessment agreement.

title: Appearance-Preserving Refinement of Generated 3D Assets for Monochromatic Fabrication
summary: Recent advances in 3D mesh generation have enabled the creation of visually realistic assets. However, much of their visual fidelity is encoded in textures rather than geometry. When such assets are fabricated using monochromatic materials, texture information is largely lost, causing visually important details to disappear even when the original geometry is faithfully preserved. A key challenge is that the geometric perturbations required to recover texture-dependent appearance cues often introduce sharp local features and high-frequency surface structures, which may increase stress concentration and fabrication risk. In this paper, we present GenMF, an appearance-oriented geometry refinement framework for monochromatic fabrication. GenMF transforms texture-dependent visual cues into geometry-induced shading effects and formulates geometry refinement as a balance between appearance preservation and fabrication-oriented robustness. To discourage structurally and narrow the gap between simulation and physical manufacturing, we further introduce a differentiable stress-aware regularization based on a learned thermal-stress predictor. Experimental results demonstrate that GenMF significantly improves appearance preservation under monochromatic rendering while reducing stress concentration under a consistent thermo-mechanical simulation setting. Physical 3D printing examples further show that the refined geometries preserve more recognizable visual details while remaining suitable for fabrication. These results suggest that appearance-aware geometry refinement provides an effective bridge between generated 3D assets and fabrication-ready monochromatic objects.

AI.tldr: GenMF is a geometry refinement framework that transforms texture-based visual cues into geometry-induced shading for monochromatic 3D printing, balancing appearance preservation with fabrication robustness via a differentiable stress-aware regularization.
AI.motivation: Textures encode critical visual fidelity in 3D assets, but when fabricated with monochromatic materials, texture information is lost even if geometry is preserved. Geometric perturbations to recover appearance cues introduce sharp features that increase stress concentration and fabrication risk.
AI.method: GenMF uses an appearance-oriented geometry refinement approach that converts texture-dependent visual cues into geometry-induced shading effects, and formulates refinement as balancing appearance preservation with fabrication robustness. It introduces a differentiable stress-aware regularization based on a learned thermal-stress predictor to discourage structurally weak features and bridge simulation-to-manufacturing gap.
AI.result: GenMF significantly improves appearance preservation under monochromatic rendering while reducing stress concentration in thermo-mechanical simulation. Physical 3D printing examples confirm refined geometries preserve more recognizable visual details while remaining fabricable.
AI.conclusion: Appearance-aware geometry refinement effectively bridges the gap between generated 3D assets and fabrication-ready monochromatic objects.

title: Augmentation with Dilution: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of Human Contributor Ecosystems After AI Coding Agent Adoption
summary: AI coding agents are penetrating open-source software development at an unprecedented pace, yet existing research predominantly treats human contributors as a static backdrop rather than as the subject of inquiry. This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study that takes the human contributor ecosystem as its dependent variable, examining how the number, composition, and behavior of human participants change following AI coding agent adoption in open-source projects. Using a staggered difference-in-differences design on a dataset of 11,097 GitHub repositories spanning January 2023 to May 2026, we provide causal evidence via the Sun and Abraham estimator. Our results show that AI agent adoption does not significantly change the absolute number of human contributors (ATT = 0.014, p = 0.224), but significantly reduces human contributor density (ATT = -0.019, p = 0.002), indicating that the relative share of human participation declines as AI-generated pull requests accumulate. The relative participation share of newcomers declines significantly by 3.7 percentage points (ATT = -0.037, p < 0.001), with the effect emerging immediately after adoption and remaining stable throughout the observation window. Review depth increases significantly by 5.3% (ATT = +0.0168, p < 0.001), indicating that AI agents shift burden from the code production stage to the review stage. Moderator analysis reveals that these effects vary systematically with project size, programming language, and project maturity. Together, these findings present a pattern of augmentation with dilution: AI agents are not displacing human contributors, but are systematically reshaping the participation structure of open-source ecosystems.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how AI coding agents affect human contributor ecosystems in open-source software, finding that AI adoption reduces human contributor density and newcomer participation share while increasing review depth, suggesting augmentation with dilution rather than displacement.
AI.motivation: Existing research treats human contributors as a static backdrop rather than as the subject of inquiry when studying AI coding agents. This paper aims to examine how the number, composition, and behavior of human participants change following AI coding agent adoption.
AI.method: A staggered difference-in-differences design on a dataset of 11,097 GitHub repositories from January 2023 to May 2026, using the Sun and Abraham estimator for causal evidence.
AI.result: AI agent adoption does not significantly change the absolute number of human contributors (ATT = 0.014, p = 0.224), but significantly reduces human contributor density (ATT = -0.019, p = 0.002). Newcomer participation share declines by 3.7 percentage points (ATT = -0.037, p < 0.001). Review depth increases by 5.3% (ATT = +0.0168, p < 0.001). Effects vary with project size, language, and maturity.
AI.conclusion: AI agents are not displacing human contributors but are systematically reshaping the participation structure of open-source ecosystems, presenting a pattern of augmentation with dilution.

title: Predicting Fruit Quality with a Hybrid Machine Learning and Image Processing Approach
summary: Fruit spoilage is a significant issue in agriculture, leading to substantial economic losses. Addressing this, our study introduces a hybrid approach combining image processing and deep learning to assess fruit freshness. We developed an image processing algorithm that quantifies spoilage on a scale from 0 (fully fresh) to 100 (fully rotten). Alongside, we trained a convolutional neural network (CNN) to perform binary classification (fresh or rotten) using a large dataset of fruit images. The outcomes of both methods were synthesized using logistic regression to enhance the accuracy of freshness predictions. Subsequently, this logistic regression model was utilized to enable the image processing algorithm to provide binary classification based on its percentage output, thus eliminating the need for the CNN in real-time applications. Our approach, which does not require high computational resources, achieved real-time performance and was validated with over 90% accuracy on a dataset comprising apples and oranges. The primary limitation lies in the requirement for fruits to be isolated on a background that must be either white or transparent, suggesting future improvements could include advanced segmentation models to automate background removal. This study's results highlight the potential of integrating simple image processing techniques with machine learning to provide practical solutions in the agricultural sector.

AI.tldr: A hybrid image processing + deep learning approach quantifies fruit spoilage (0-100 scale) and binary classification (fresh/rotten), using logistic regression to fuse methods, achieving >90% accuracy in real-time with low computational resources.
AI.motivation: Fruit spoilage causes substantial economic losses in agriculture, motivating the need for an accurate, low-cost, real-time freshness assessment solution.
AI.method: An image processing algorithm quantifies spoilage on a 0-100 scale; a CNN performs binary classification (fresh/rotten); logistic regression synthesizes both outputs to enhance accuracy and enables the image processing algorithm to output binary decisions, removing CNN dependency in deployment.
AI.result: Achieved over 90% accuracy on an apple and orange dataset with real-time performance and low computational requirements.
AI.conclusion: Integrating simple image processing with machine learning provides practical, efficient agricultural solutions; future work requires advanced segmentation models for automated background removal (currently limited to white/transparent backgrounds).

title: Life After Benchmark Saturation: A Case Study of CORE-Bench
summary: When a benchmark's accuracy saturates, it is often retired and replaced with a more challenging version. We show that this approach privileges accuracy and misses the opportunity to study six other key dimensions of agent performance: construct validity issues such as shortcuts, out-of-distribution generalizability, efficiency, reliability, the relative importance of the model versus the scaffold, and uplift from human-agent collaboration. We use CORE-Bench Hard, a benchmark for computational reproducibility of scientific code, as a case study to demonstrate that measuring agents along these dimensions yields meaningful insights into agent performance even after accuracy saturates. First, we surface threats to construct validity in CORE-Bench Hard that are difficult to anticipate with less capable agents. We introduce an improved benchmark, CORE-Bench v1.1, and an out-of-distribution task suite, CORE-Bench OOD. Second, we find that despite accuracy saturation, CORE-Bench v1.1 remains useful for measuring efficiency, reliability, model performance, and scaffold performance. Finally, we conduct a small-scale randomized experiment to measure uplift from human-agent collaboration on real-world computational reproducibility tasks. We find a statistically significant speedup by about a factor of two -- likely underestimated due to one-fifth of human-only reproductions reaching the time limit before completing -- and describe various other findings. Together, our contributions present a more rigorous alternative to the dominant accuracy-centric evaluation paradigm.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that benchmark evaluation should move beyond accuracy saturation to measure six additional dimensions of agent performance: construct validity, out-of-distribution generalizability, efficiency, reliability, model vs. scaffold contribution, and human-agent collaboration uplift, using CORE-Bench as a case study.
AI.motivation: Current practice retires benchmarks when accuracy saturates, missing opportunities to evaluate agent performance along six other key dimensions beyond accuracy.
AI.method: Using CORE-Bench Hard as a case study, the authors surface construct validity issues, introduce CORE-Bench v1.1 and CORE-Bench OOD, measure efficiency/reliability/model-scaffold contributions, and conduct a small-scale randomized experiment on human-agent collaboration uplift.
AI.result: CORE-Bench v1.1 remains useful for measuring efficiency, reliability, model performance, and scaffold performance despite accuracy saturation. Human-agent collaboration yields a statistically significant ~2x speedup (likely underestimated).
AI.conclusion: The work presents a more rigorous alternative to accuracy-centric evaluation by proposing multi-dimensional assessment that remains informative even after accuracy saturates.

title: WQ-Fusion: Dynamic Gated Attention for Cross-Domain Audio Representation
summary: While pre-trained models excel in specialized tasks, learning universal representations across diverse acoustic domains remains challenging. To address this, we propose WQ-Fusion, a robust dual-encoder framework for cross-domain audio representation learning. Overcoming the limitations of static concatenation, WQ-Fusion integrates whisper and qwen via an Adaptive Feature Modulation module and a novel element-wise gated attention mechanism. This design enables dynamic feature selection, allowing the model to selectively emphasize relevant acoustic and semantic dimensions. Extensive experiments on the Interspeech 2026 Audio Encoder Capability Challenge (Track A) benchmark demonstrate that by effectively routing heterogeneous information, WQ-Fusion achieves a superior overall score of 0.836, significantly outperforming the strongest single-encoder baseline.

AI.tldr: WQ-Fusion is a dual-encoder framework that integrates whisper and qwen via adaptive feature modulation and element-wise gated attention, achieving state-of-the-art cross-domain audio representation learning with a 0.836 score on the Interspeech 2026 benchmark.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained models struggle to learn universal representations across diverse acoustic domains, and static concatenation of features is insufficient for cross-domain audio representation learning.
AI.method: A dual-encoder framework (WQ-Fusion) combining whisper and qwen through an Adaptive Feature Modulation module and an element-wise gated attention mechanism for dynamic feature selection.
AI.result: Achieved a superior overall score of 0.836 on the Interspeech 2026 Audio Encoder Capability Challenge (Track A) benchmark, significantly outperforming the strongest single-encoder baseline.
AI.conclusion: WQ-Fusion demonstrates that effective routing of heterogeneous information via adaptive gating significantly improves cross-domain audio representation learning over single-encoder approaches.

title: Necessary but Not Sufficient: Temperature Control and Reproducibility in LLM-as-Judge Safety Evaluations
summary: LLM-as-judge ("grader") components are now standard in evaluation harnesses, including safety evaluations where a pass/fail verdict may gate downstream deployment decisions. A widespread assumption is that setting the grader's sampling temperature to 0 makes grading deterministic. We test this assumption against a real safety-evaluation codebase (Japan AISI's open-source aisev) and show it fails on two levels. First, the harness invokes its grader without setting temperature or seed; the underlying provider silently applies its default of 1.0, so items near the decision boundary flip pass/fail across identical runs (per-item disagreement up to ~50% over 20 runs). Second, pinning temperature=0 reduces but does not eliminate flips: across 690 API calls spanning two providers, three model tiers, and five sampling configurations, 1-2 of 7 borderline items remain non-reproducible even under forced greedy decoding (top_k=1). Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 has since deprecated temperature entirely, rendering the primary mitigation inapplicable to newer model generations. These findings expose a structural gap: evaluation harnesses that report single-run verdicts without variance or grader-disagreement metrics can present noise as a safety property. We release a reproduction harness (690 calls, 7 conditions) and recommend that harnesses treat grader disagreement as a first-class health metric alongside the scores themselves.

AI.tldr: LLM-as-judge evaluations are assumed deterministic at temperature=0, but testing shows this fails: (1) defaults silently apply temperature=1.0 causing ~50% per-item disagreement, and (2) even forced greedy decoding leaves 1-2 of 7 borderline items non-reproducible. Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 deprecated temperature entirely, making the mitigation inapplicable. Recommendations include reporting variance and grader-disagreement metrics.
AI.motivation: To test the widespread assumption that setting LLM grader sampling temperature to 0 yields deterministic grading in safety evaluation harnesses, where pass/fail verdicts may gate deployment decisions.
AI.method: Empirical testing against Japan AISI's open-source aisev codebase across 690 API calls spanning two providers, three model tiers, and five sampling configurations, including forced greedy decoding (top_k=1).
AI.result: The assumption fails on two levels: (1) harness defaults silently apply temperature=1.0 causing ~50% per-item disagreement across runs; (2) pinning temperature=0 reduces but does not eliminate flips—1-2 of 7 borderline items remain non-reproducible under greedy decoding. Claude Opus 4.7/4.8 deprecated temperature entirely.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation harnesses that report single-run verdicts without variance or grader-disagreement metrics can present noise as a safety property. The paper releases a reproduction harness and recommends treating grader disagreement as a first-class health metric.

title: VoiceTTA: Enhancing Zero-Shot Text-to-Speech via Reinforcement Learning-Based Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Recently, zero-shot text-to-speech (TTS) has enabled high-fidelity and expressive speech synthesis, but it often fails to imitate unseen speaking styles from uncommon scenarios (e.g., crosstalk, dialects). Moreover, fine-tuning pretrained models requires large, high-quality datasets, limiting rapid personalization. We propose VoiceTTA, a reinforcement learning-based test-time adaptation (TTA) method that improves voice imitation of pretrained zero-shot TTS models. VoiceTTA introduces two style rewards based on coefficient-of-variation differences of F0 and energy, combined with speaker similarity and intelligibility (WER from a pretrained Whisper model), and optimizes learnable prefixes via group relative preference optimization (GRPO) in a flow matching-based model at inference time. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial improvements on uncommon speech prompts, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Audio samples are available at https://voicetta.pages.dev/

AI.tldr: VoiceTTA is a reinforcement learning-based test-time adaptation method that improves zero-shot TTS voice imitation for unseen styles by optimizing learnable prefixes via GRPO using style rewards (F0/energy CV differences), speaker similarity, and WER intelligibility.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot TTS fails to imitate unseen speaking styles from uncommon scenarios (e.g., crosstalk, dialects), and fine-tuning pretrained models requires large, high-quality datasets, limiting rapid personalization.
AI.method: VoiceTTA uses reinforcement learning-based test-time adaptation with two style rewards based on coefficient-of-variation differences of F0 and energy, speaker similarity, and intelligibility (WER from Whisper). It optimizes learnable prefixes via group relative preference optimization (GRPO) in a flow matching-based model at inference time.
AI.result: Substantial improvements on uncommon speech prompts, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: RL-based TTA effectively enables zero-shot TTS models to adapt to unseen speaking styles without large fine-tuning datasets.

title: Neural Texture Compression using Hypernetworks
summary: Recent work on neural texture compression has demonstrated that it is possible to learn small, per-material texture representations (composed of latent textures and a small Multi-Layer Perceptron decoder) that can be decoded in real-time during shading to reproduce the input to a physically based shading model. However, existing methods require performing gradient-descent optimization per material for a given MLP and latent configuration. In this work, we train a single hypernetwork that outputs both the latent features and the MLP's weights and biases. Though the solution space is high-dimensional, this approach produces results comparable in quality to the current reference neural texture compressors. We further extend this approach to infer multiple decoders at once or even produce decoders that learn super-resolution.

AI.tldr: A hypernetwork predicts latent features and MLP weights for neural texture compression, matching per-material optimization quality, with extensions to multi-decoder and super-resolution inference.
AI.motivation: Existing neural texture compression requires per-material gradient-descent optimization for each MLP and latent configuration, which is computationally expensive and lacks generalization.
AI.method: Train a single hypernetwork that directly outputs both latent texture features and the MLP decoder's weights and biases, eliminating per-material optimization.
AI.result: The hypernetwork approach produces results comparable in quality to the current reference per-material optimized neural texture compressors.
AI.conclusion: A single hypernetwork can effectively compress neural textures without per-material optimization, and can be extended to infer multiple decoders or learn super-resolution.

title: Towards Safety-Aware Mutation Testing for Autonomous Driving Systems
summary:
Simulation-based testing is essential for ensuring the safety of Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS), yet the community lacks a systematic criterion for determining when we can safely stop additional test scenario generation. Existing coverage metrics typically focus on individual component reliability or treat the ADS as a black box, failing to capture certain component interactions that cause most ADS accidents. While traditional mutation testing provides a falsifiable measure of test adequacy, directly porting code- and deep learning model-level mutations to the corresponding modules of ADS is insufficient.
  In this vision paper, we propose a paradigm shift toward Safety-Aware Mutation Testing (SAMT). Unlike traditional mutation testing, which creates mutants (i.e., faulty versions of the software under test) by injecting artificial faults into individual components, SAMT systematically injects temporally bounded faults into the messages exchanged between ADS modules to simulate realistic interaction failures. To ensure these mutants represent genuine hazards, we propose deriving mutant generation rules directly from top-down safety engineering frameworks, such as System-Theoretic Process Analysis (STPA). By embedding systems thinking into the mutation testing pipeline, SAMT provides a rigorous mechanism for evaluating test adequacy, enabling automated scenario generation, and guiding ADS repair. We also outline critical open challenges.

AI.tldr: This vision paper proposes Safety-Aware Mutation Testing (SAMT), a new approach that injects temporally bounded faults into inter-module messages of Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS) based on safety engineering frameworks like STPA, aiming to provide a systematic criterion for test adequacy in simulation-based testing.
AI.motivation: The community lacks a systematic criterion for determining when to stop generating additional test scenarios for ADS simulation-based testing. Existing coverage metrics fail to capture component interactions that cause most ADS accidents.
AI.method: SAMT injects temporally bounded faults into messages exchanged between ADS modules (rather than individual components) and derives mutant generation rules from top-down safety engineering frameworks like System-Theoretic Process Analysis (STPA).
AI.result: SAMT provides a rigorous mechanism for evaluating test adequacy, enabling automated scenario generation, and guiding ADS repair.
AI.conclusion: SAMT represents a paradigm shift from traditional mutation testing by embedding systems thinking into the pipeline, addressing component interaction failures through safety-aware mutant generation. Critical open challenges are also outlined.

title: LCG: Long-Context Consistent Image Generation with Sparse Relational Attention
summary: Recent image generation models achieve impressive quality in single-image synthesis, but often fail to maintain consistency across sequential outputs, as required in comics, storyboards, and visual narratives. We propose Long-Context Generation (LCG), a framework for long-context multi-image text-to-image generation, to improve consistency and scalability in long-context multi-image generation. LCG employs the Sparse Relational Attention (SRA) mechanism to selectively attend to core features across extended visual contexts, ensuring that the propagation of semantic and layout information remains computationally tractable. To enforce semantic alignment, we introduce the Routing Consistency Constraint (RCC), which leverages identity-aware masks to align structural patterns across generation branches, effectively mitigating drift in appearance even in complex multi-character scenes. To support training and evaluation in this setting, we construct the Long-Context Consistency Dataset (LCCD), a large-scale synthetic dataset comprising character-centric multi-image sequences spanning varied situational contexts. LCCD contains 600K training sequences and a separate 1K test set, with each sequence containing 6 to 20 images. The experiments demonstrate that LCG outperforms the compared baselines in prompt alignment and character consistency for long-context image generation, including multi-character scenes.

AI.tldr: LCG is a framework for long-context multi-image text-to-image generation that uses Sparse Relational Attention (SRA) and Routing Consistency Constraint (RCC) to improve consistency across sequential images, evaluated on the new Long-Context Consistency Dataset (LCCD) containing 600K training sequences and 1K test sequences with 6-20 images each.
AI.motivation: Existing image generation models produce high-quality single images but fail to maintain consistency across sequential outputs needed for comics, storyboards, and visual narratives.
AI.method: Proposes LCG framework with two key components: (1) Sparse Relational Attention (SRA) for selectively attending to core features across extended visual contexts, and (2) Routing Consistency Constraint (RCC) using identity-aware masks to align structural patterns and mitigate appearance drift. Also introduces LCCD dataset for training/evaluation.
AI.result: LCG outperforms compared baselines in prompt alignment and character consistency for long-context image generation, including multi-character scenes, evaluated on LCCD (600K training sequences, 1K test set, 6-20 images per sequence).
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses consistency and scalability challenges in long-context multi-image generation, showing superior performance over existing methods.

title: Refusal Lives Downstream of Persona in Chat Models
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 Mechanistic Interpretability workshop
summary: Linear directions in activation space have been identified for both refusal and persona traits in instruction-tuned chat models, but the two have been studied as separate mechanisms. We show they interact: a compliant persona gates refusal. In Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, we extract a compliant model-persona direction and a refusal direction and intervene on both. Compliant persona steering suppresses refusal -- in Llama, the refusal rate falls from 97% to 2%. Reintroducing the refusal direction partially restores refusal at late layers but not at early ones. Projecting out the persona direction in a late-layer window restores it to baseline; projecting out a random direction does not. Refusal is therefore gated at the late-layer expression stage, downstream of where it is computed. Treating refusal as a single isolated direction misses its dependence on persona.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that in instruction-tuned chat models, a compliant persona direction gates refusal expression at late layers, meaning refusal is not an isolated mechanism but depends on persona; intervening on both directions reveals that compliant persona steering suppresses refusal, while reintroducing refusal direction partially restores it only at late layers.
AI.motivation: Prior work identified separate linear directions for refusal and persona traits but treated them as independent mechanisms; this paper investigates how they interact.
AI.method: Extracted compliant model-persona and refusal directions from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, then performed intervention (steering and projection) on both directions across different layers.
AI.result: Compliant persona steering suppressed refusal (e.g., Llama refusal rate dropped from 97% to 2%); reintroducing refusal direction at late but not early layers partially restored refusal; projecting out persona direction at late layers restored refusal to baseline, while random direction did not.
AI.conclusion: Refusal is gated at the late-layer expression stage, downstream of where it is computed, and depends on persona; treating refusal as an isolated direction misses this dependency.

title: NaviCache: Test-Time Self-Calibration Caching for Video Generation
comment: Published at ICML 2026: Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning, Seoul, South Korea. PMLR 306, 2026
summary: Video Diffusion Models (VDMs) is constrained by immense computational costs. While offline calibration-based acceleration suffers from calibration data dependency, prohibitive calibration duration, and susceptibility to distribution shifts, offline calibration-free methods eliminate these hurdles. However, since they rely on instantaneous zero-order approximations where the mapping between input and output differences varies in real-time, they are susceptible to observational noise and ignore the intrinsic momentum within the diffusion trajectory. In this paper, we propose NaviCache, a plug-and-play test-time self-calibration method re-conceptualizing feature evolution as an Inertial Navigation System (INS) problem. NaviCache bridges the fundamental domain gap and the non-stationary nature of diffusion by modeling the relative coupling between input and output variations. We introduce a dual-state estimation architecture that adaptively tracks the feature change ratio and its latent drift, initialized via a specialized Initial Alignment phase. By integrating a time-dependent noise schedule with an uncertainty-aware Measurement Update mechanism, NaviCache provides a theoretically grounded mechanism for error-bounded computation skipping. Extensive experiments on the HunyuanVideo, Wan, and Open-Sora series demonstrate that NaviCache exhibits more accurate error judgment for computation skipping and achieves outstanding comprehensive performance.

AI.tldr: NaviCache is a plug-and-play test-time self-calibration method for accelerating Video Diffusion Models, re-conceptualized as an Inertial Navigation System problem with dual-state estimation and uncertainty-aware computation skipping.
AI.motivation: Existing VDM acceleration methods: offline calibration-based approaches suffer from calibration data dependency, prohibitive duration, and distribution shift susceptibility; offline calibration-free methods are vulnerable to observational noise and ignore intrinsic momentum in diffusion trajectories due to reliance on instantaneous zero-order approximations.
AI.method: NaviCache bridges domain gap and non-stationary diffusion by modeling relative coupling between input/output variations via a dual-state estimation architecture that adaptively tracks feature change ratio and latent drift, initialized via an Initial Alignment phase, with time-dependent noise schedule and uncertainty-aware Measurement Update.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on HunyuanVideo, Wan, and Open-Sora series demonstrate more accurate error judgment for computation skipping and outstanding comprehensive performance.
AI.conclusion: NaviCache provides a theoretically grounded mechanism for error-bounded computation skipping in VDM acceleration.

title: Clue-Guided Money Laundering Group Discovery
summary: Money Laundering Group Discovery (MLGD) aims to identify hidden criminal groups and recover their complete structures in large-scale financial networks. Existing graph anomaly detection methods mainly produce node-level risk alerts, while global group discovery methods passively search for suspicious groups over the whole network. Both are mismatched with real Anti-money-laundering (AML) investigations, where analysts usually start from a concrete clue and gradually expand the investigation to recover the responsible group. To address this gap, we propose Clue-Guided Group Discovery (CGGD), where a laundering group is progressively recovered from an initial clue set through analyst interaction. We further propose Clue2Group, a framework that first constructs a compact local investigation context to reduce noise and preserve chain-like and cycle-like laundering structures. It then estimates a clue-conditioned local risk field with a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN, and finally integrates risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover a coherent laundering group. Experiments on two large-scale AML benchmarks show that Clue2Group provides a practical clue-driven analysis framework for AML investigations, offering a feasible step toward bridging the gap between graph-based AML research and real investigation workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Clue2Group, a clue-guided framework for Money Laundering Group Discovery that progressively recovers criminal groups from initial clues, using compact local contexts, multi-semantic local-temporal GNNs, and integration of risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to align with real AML investigation workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing graph anomaly detection methods produce node-level alerts and global group discovery methods passively search over whole networks, both mismatched with real AML investigations where analysts start from a concrete clue and expand investigation to recover the responsible group.
AI.method: Proposes Clue2Group framework that: (1) constructs a compact local investigation context to reduce noise and preserve chain-like/cycle-like laundering structures; (2) estimates a clue-conditioned local risk field with a multi-semantic local-temporal GNN; (3) integrates risk, structural, and prior-pattern evidence to recover a coherent laundering group.
AI.result: Experiments on two large-scale AML benchmarks demonstrate that Clue2Group provides a practical clue-driven analysis framework for AML investigations.
AI.conclusion: Clue2Group offers a feasible step toward bridging the gap between graph-based AML research and real investigation workflows.

title: Vis4GS: A Visual Analytic Tool for 3D Gaussian Splatting Reconstruction
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) supports fast training and real-time rendering, but its optimization process remains difficult to interpret. Existing viewers mainly expose the final reconstructed scene and offer limited support for explaining how Gaussian properties contribute to visible artifacts or evolve during training. We present Vis4GS, a multi-view visual analytics tool for primitive-level diagnosis of 3DGS reconstruction artifacts. Built on the original 3DGS viewer and training framework, Vis4GS links rendered artifacts to Gaussian properties, View Coverage, training progress, and Gaussian genealogy through four linked views: an interactive Gaussian analysis view, a property timeline view, a Gaussian densification tree view, and a log and control panel. The system supports Gaussian selection, blur and needle-like artifact scoring, View Coverage analysis, and multiscale genealogy exploration of clone, split, prune, and clone-split events. By connecting scene-level artifacts with primitive-level evidence and optimization history, Vis4GS enables a structured workflow for diagnosing reconstruction failures beyond final-image inspection and global metrics. A user study also shows that Vis4GS provides stronger support for usability and artifact understanding than the original 3DGS viewer.

AI.tldr: Vis4GS is a multi-view visual analytics tool that extends the 3DGS viewer to enable primitive-level diagnosis of reconstruction artifacts by linking rendered artifacts to Gaussian properties, View Coverage, training progress, and Gaussian genealogy through four linked views.
AI.motivation: 3DGS supports fast training and real-time rendering, but its optimization process is difficult to interpret; existing viewers expose only the final scene and offer limited support for explaining how Gaussian properties contribute to artifacts or evolve during training.
AI.method: Vis4GS provides four linked views: an interactive Gaussian analysis view, a property timeline view, a Gaussian densification tree view, and a log and control panel. It supports Gaussian selection, blur/needle-like artifact scoring, View Coverage analysis, and multiscale genealogy exploration of clone, split, prune, and clone-split events.
AI.result: A user study shows Vis4GS provides stronger support for usability and artifact understanding than the original 3DGS viewer.
AI.conclusion: By connecting scene-level artifacts with primitive-level evidence and optimization history, Vis4GS enables a structured workflow for diagnosing reconstruction failures beyond final-image inspection and global metrics.

title: An Empirical Study of LLM-Generated Specifications for VeriFast
summary: Static verification tools can assure industrial scale software, but require significant human labor to write specifications. This is particularly true of static verifiers based on separation logic (SL verifiers), which excel at verifying heapmanipulating programs, but require many complex auxiliary specifications to reason about heap structure. Recent work applies large language models (LLMs) to generate code, tests, and proofs, including specifications for verifiers, but mostly targeting non-SL verifiers. To address this gap, this paper thoroughly evaluates how well LLMs perform when prompted to generate specifications for verifying 303 C functions with the SL verifier VeriFast. We explored eight prompting approaches, ten LLMs, and three input types in two stages. Quantitative and qualitative analyses are used to assess the LLM-generated code and specifications for functional behavior, verifiability and errors. The results show that LLMs preserve functional behavior in source code and specifications (both over 91%), but achieve modest verification success (31.4%). Using Gemini 2.5 Pro and providing formal contracts lead to higher success rates in our setting. Moreover, most errors (94%) come from LLMs' mistakes in the domainspecific knowledge of SL verifiers such as VeriFast. These findings provide guidance for optimizing LLM-generated specifications for SL verifiers.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates LLMs' ability to generate specifications for the separation logic verifier VeriFast across 303 C functions, finding that LLMs preserve functional behavior well (over 91%) but achieve only 31.4% verification success, with 94% of errors stemming from LLMs' lack of domain-specific knowledge about SL verifiers.
AI.motivation: Static verification tools, particularly separation logic (SL) verifiers, require significant human effort to write complex auxiliary specifications. While LLMs have been applied to generate specifications for non-SL verifiers, there is a gap in understanding how well LLMs can generate specifications specifically for SL-based verifiers like VeriFast.
AI.method: The authors evaluate eight prompting approaches, ten LLMs, and three input types across two stages to generate specifications for 303 C functions using the SL verifier VeriFast. Both quantitative and qualitative analyses are conducted on the LLM-generated code and specifications.
AI.result: LLMs preserve functional behavior in source code and specifications (over 91%), but achieve only 31.4% verification success. Gemini 2.5 Pro and providing formal contracts lead to higher success rates. 94% of errors stem from LLMs' mistakes in domain-specific knowledge of SL verifiers like VeriFast.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide guidance for optimizing LLM-generated specifications for SL verifiers, highlighting the need to address domain-specific knowledge gaps in LLMs for separation logic verification.

title: Self-Supervised Tree-level Biomass Estimation in Urban Environments From Airborne LiDAR and Optical Observations
summary: Urban tree biomass remains less spatially explicitly quantified than biomass in managed forests because many estimates rely on inventories or coarse products that cannot resolve individual crowns or fine-scale heterogeneity. We present a crown-level above-ground biomass (AGB) framework for an 810~km$^2$ landscape in Ontario, Canada, using leaf-off airborne LiDAR (8--10~pulses~m$^{-2}$) and near-infrared RGB orthophotography (0.16--0.20~m) from 2018 and 2023. A dual-stream cross-attention network trained on rule-based pseudo-labels produced semantic marks for buildings, needleleaf trees, and deciduous trees, supporting crown delineation and functional-type assignment. On independently annotated withheld tiles, global/mean precision, recall, and Dice scores were 0.86, 0.83, and 0.84. Crowns were delineated with multiscale watershed segmentation in mapped tree areas, and AGB was estimated from a crown area--height power-law proxy calibrated to species-specific allometry (Lambert et al., 2005) for 21,921 inventory trees.
