For 18,713 inventory--segment matched pairs from a 90,726-tree held-out test set, AGB prediction achieved $R^2=0.609$ using inventory crown geometry and $R^2=0.570$ under operational segmentation, identifying crown delineation as the remaining uncertainty source. Aggregated to 30~m, estimates yielded total AGB stocks of 1.73~Tg in 2018 and 1.81~Tg in 2023 (811--850~Gg~C), local densities up to ${\sim}140$~Mg~ha$^{-1}$ along the Niagara Escarpment, and a net carbon gain of 39~Gg~C over five years. Deep-ensemble uncertainty maps highlighted high-epistemic-uncertainty areas linked to underrepresented land covers and guided assignment of uncertain crowns to a pooled allometric equation. The framework uses standard provincial data, requires no manual annotation, and produces a public bitemporal crown-level AGB database for trees outside forests at management-relevant resolution.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a crown-level above-ground biomass (AGB) estimation framework for urban trees across 810 km² in Ontario, Canada, using leaf-off airborne LiDAR and RGB orthophotography. A dual-stream cross-attention network with rule-based pseudo-labels classifies trees as needleleaf or deciduous, followed by multiscale watershed segmentation and power-law AGB modeling calibrated to species-specific allometry. Validation shows R²=0.570–0.609 for AGB prediction, with total stocks of 1.73 Tg (2018) and 1.81 Tg (2023) and a net carbon gain of 39 Gg C over five years.
AI.motivation: Urban tree biomass is less spatially explicitly quantified than managed forest biomass because most estimates rely on inventories or coarse products that cannot resolve individual crowns or fine-scale heterogeneity.
AI.method: A dual-stream cross-attention network trained on rule-based pseudo-labels produces semantic maps for buildings, needleleaf, and deciduous trees; multiscale watershed segmentation delineates crowns; AGB is estimated via a crown area–height power-law proxy calibrated to Lambert et al. (2005) species-specific allometry using 21,921 inventory trees; deep-ensemble uncertainty mapping guides uncertain crowns to pooled allometry.
AI.result: Global mean precision 0.86, recall 0.83, Dice 0.84; AGB prediction R²=0.609 (inventory geometry) and R²=0.570 (operational segmentation); total AGB 1.73 Tg (2018) and 1.81 Tg (2023), net carbon gain 39 Gg C over five years.
AI.conclusion: The framework uses standard provincial data, requires no manual annotation, and produces a public bitemporal crown-level AGB database for trees outside forests at management-relevant resolution.

title: AlgoEvolve: LLM-driven Meta-evolution of Algorithmic Trading Programs
summary: Recent work shows that Large Language Models (LLMs) can act as semantic mutation operators for the evolutionary discovery of programs and proofs. Most current applications focus on static coding benchmarks. We extend this paradigm to algorithmic trading. This domain is uniquely challenging because it is noisy, non-stationary, and highly discontinuous. We present AlgoEvolve, an LLM-driven evolutionary framework that generates, evaluates, and iteratively improves executable trading strategies. These strategies are expressed as Python code and evaluated through a rigorous testing protocol. Across multiple experiments, the system exhibits emergent regime-adaptive strategy logic, including autonomous shifts in trading rules. We further introduce a meta-evolutionary outer loop that evolves the prompts guiding program synthesis in the inner loop. This outer loop discovers improved search heuristics. These heuristics balance exploration and exploitation while reducing zero-trade failures. They consistently outperform initial human-designed instructions. The results demonstrate that LLM-based semantic evolution provides a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex environments.

AI.tldr: AlgoEvolve is an LLM-driven evolutionary framework that generates, evaluates, and iteratively improves executable trading strategies in Python, exhibiting emergent regime-adaptive logic and using a meta-evolutionary outer loop to evolve prompts for improved search heuristics in algorithmic trading.
AI.motivation: To extend LLM-driven semantic mutation operators from static coding benchmarks to the challenging domain of algorithmic trading, which is noisy, non-stationary, and highly discontinuous.
AI.method: An evolutionary framework (AlgoEvolve) using LLMs as semantic mutation operators to generate and iteratively improve Python-based trading strategies, with a meta-evolutionary outer loop that evolves prompts guiding the inner-loop program synthesis.
AI.result: The system exhibits emergent regime-adaptive strategy logic with autonomous shifts in trading rules. The meta-evolutionary outer loop discovers improved search heuristics that balance exploration and exploitation, reduce zero-trade failures, and consistently outperform initial human-designed instructions.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based semantic evolution provides a viable approach for continual program synthesis in complex, noisy, and non-stationary environments like algorithmic trading.

title: Federated Hash Projected Latent Factor Learning
summary: Hash Learning (HL) is an efficient representation learning approach that maps real-valued data into compact binary representations. Traditional HL methods typically require users to upload personal data to a central server, which is incompatible with increasingly stringent data security regulations. Federated Learning (FL) provides a decentralized paradigm for learning globally optimal models without centralizing private data. However, most FL methods rely on transmitting large-scale real-valued gradient information, leading to high communication overhead and potential privacy risks. Integrating HL into FL is a promising solution. Nevertheless, existing HL methods suffer from limited representational capacity of binary codes, which may degrade model accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a Federated Hash Projected Latent Factor (FHPLF) model. FHPLF introduces three key innovations: (a) replacing real-valued gradient matrices with binary gradient-like matrices, significantly reducing computation, storage, and communication costs while enhancing privacy protection; (b) leveraging Projected Hamming Distance for similarity modeling, which captures the importance of individual binary bits to improve representation capability; and (c) proposing a Secure Binary Gradient Reassembly and Privacy-Enhanced Upload (SBG-PEU) strategy to further reduce the risk of user interaction leakage during transmission. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that FHPLF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art HL and FL methods, achieving a favorable trade-off among accuracy, efficiency, and privacy preservation.

AI.tldr: FHPLF integrates Hash Learning into Federated Learning by replacing real-valued gradients with binary gradient-like matrices, using Projected Hamming Distance for better similarity modeling, and employing a secure binary gradient reassembly strategy to improve accuracy, efficiency, and privacy in decentralized binary representation learning.
AI.motivation: Traditional Hash Learning requires centralized data uploads violating privacy regulations, while Federated Learning suffers from high communication overhead from real-valued gradient transmission. Integrating HL into FL faces degraded accuracy due to limited binary code representation capacity.
AI.method: FHPLF introduces three innovations: (a) binary gradient-like matrices replace real-valued gradients to reduce costs and enhance privacy; (b) Projected Hamming Distance captures bit-level importance for better representation; (c) Secure Binary Gradient Reassembly and Privacy-Enhanced Upload (SBG-PEU) strategy minimizes leakage risk during transmission.
AI.result: On four real-world datasets, FHPLF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art HL and FL methods, achieving a favorable trade-off among accuracy, efficiency, and privacy preservation.
AI.conclusion: FHPLF effectively addresses the accuracy degradation problem in federated hash learning by improving binary code representation capability while maintaining communication efficiency and privacy protection.

title: wav2tok 2.0: Scalable Audio Tokenization Maintaining Explicit Pairwise Token Alignment for Efficient Audio Retrieval
summary: Learning discrete speech representations that preserve similarity across variable-length utterances is central to query-by-example spoken term detection (QbE-STD). While wav2tok introduced CTC-based sequence alignment to enforce token consistency, its tightly coupled clustering and alignment training recipe limits scalability. We propose wav2tok 2.0, a scalable alignment-aware speech tokenizer built on the BEST-STD backbone. wav2tok 2.0 employs staged training, first learning discriminative, speaker-invariant representations via contrastive learning and vector quantization, and then enforcing pairwise token consistency using a CTC alignment loss and a novel DTW-aligned framewise prediction objective with adaptive weighting. Experiments show that wav2tok 2.0 consistently outperforms BEST-STD and general-purpose tokenizers on QbE-STD while remaining efficient and scalable.

AI.tldr: wav2tok 2.0 improves upon wav2tok by using a staged training approach with contrastive learning, vector quantization, and CTC/DTW alignment losses for better query-by-example spoken term detection.
AI.motivation: wav2tok's tightly coupled clustering and alignment training limits scalability; the goal is a scalable alignment-aware speech tokenizer that preserves similarity across variable-length utterances for QbE-STD.
AI.method: Staged training: (1) discriminative, speaker-invariant representations via contrastive learning and vector quantization; (2) pairwise token consistency via CTC alignment loss and DTW-aligned framewise prediction objective with adaptive weighting, built on the BEST-STD backbone.
AI.result: wav2tok 2.0 consistently outperforms BEST-STD and general-purpose tokenizers on QbE-STD while remaining efficient and scalable.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling the training pipeline into stages improves scalability and performance for discrete speech tokenization in QbE-STD.

title: SpaceRipple: Lightweight Semantic Delivery for Mission-Oriented LEO Earth Observation Satellite Networks
summary: Earth observation satellite networks generate massive volumes of high-resolution imagery, whereas inter-satellite and downlink resources remain limited. In many time-sensitive missions, ground users require mission-relevant semantic information rather than a full raw-image downlink. This paper proposes SpaceRipple, a lightweight framework for mission-oriented semantic delivery and on-board processing in Earth observation satellite networks. A sensing satellite performs adaptive compression and metadata generation to reduce inter-satellite traffic, while an edge computing satellite restores the received representation and extracts task-relevant semantic information. Unlike fidelity-driven image transmission, SpaceRipple coordinates compression, forwarding, restoration, and semantic inference within a collaborative pipeline, enabling semantic-oriented delivery instead of pixel-level image delivery. A compression-aware MoE enhancement module is further introduced to improve robustness under degraded visual inputs. Experimental results show that SpaceRipple achieves favorable reconstruction quality, improved semantic detection performance, and substantial bandwidth savings, demonstrating its potential for efficient and reliable Earth observation under constrained satellite-network resources.

AI.tldr: SpaceRipple is a lightweight framework for semantic-oriented delivery and on-board processing in Earth observation satellite networks, using adaptive compression, metadata generation, and MoE enhancement to reduce bandwidth while preserving task-relevant semantic information.
AI.motivation: Earth observation satellite networks generate massive high-resolution imagery but have limited inter-satellite and downlink resources. Time-sensitive missions require semantic information rather than full raw-image downlinks.
AI.method: A sensing satellite performs adaptive compression and metadata generation; an edge computing satellite restores representations and extracts task-relevant semantics. A compression-aware Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) enhancement module improves robustness under degraded inputs. The pipeline coordinates compression, forwarding, restoration, and semantic inference collaboratively.
AI.result: Achieves favorable reconstruction quality, improved semantic detection performance, and substantial bandwidth savings compared to fidelity-driven image transmission.
AI.conclusion: SpaceRipple demonstrates potential for efficient and reliable Earth observation under constrained satellite-network resources by shifting from pixel-level to semantic-oriented delivery.

title: Evaluation-Strategy Gap in Fault Diagnosis of Deep Learning Programs
comment: Accepted research track paper in the 42nd IEEE International Conference on Software Maintenance and Evolution (ICSME 2026)
summary: Deep Learning (DL) programs can fail during training for many reasons, and diagnosing the cause is a costly and time-consuming maintenance task. Techniques for diagnosing such failures are commonly assessed using within-program cross-validation, which may be inadequate for deployment settings involving previously unseen programs. It is therefore necessary to assess how performance differs across these settings and to identify the causes of any performance gap in established fault diagnosis techniques for DL. We investigate this gap using DynFault, a corpus of 5,542 fault-injected training traces from 38 real-world DL programs. We found a gap of 0.190 in balanced accuracy for existing fault diagnosis techniques between within-program evaluation and holding out whole programs. We also found the gap comes from program-level structure in the features, which led us to examine two runtime feature sets, curvature features and optimizer features, and their behavior on unseen programs. We found that curvature features are useful for instability detection on unseen programs, while optimizer and activation features help only on programs seen during training.

AI.tldr: Existing fault diagnosis techniques for DL programs show a 0.190 balanced accuracy gap between within-program cross-validation and held-out program evaluation, primarily due to program-level feature structure. Curvature features generalize to unseen programs, while optimizer and activation features only help on seen programs.
AI.motivation: Techniques for diagnosing DL training failures are typically evaluated using within-program cross-validation, which may not reflect performance on unseen programs in deployment settings. The paper aims to quantify this performance gap and identify its causes.
AI.method: Used DynFault corpus (5,542 fault-injected training traces from 38 real-world DL programs); compared within-program evaluation vs. holding out whole programs; analyzed curvature, optimizer, and activation features to understand generalization behavior.
AI.result: Found a 0.190 balanced accuracy gap between within-program and held-out program evaluation. The gap stems from program-level feature structure. Curvature features generalize to unseen programs; optimizer and activation features only help on seen programs.
AI.conclusion: Current evaluation practices overestimate fault diagnosis performance; curvature features are promising for cross-program generalization, while optimizer and activation features require program-specific training data.

title: A multi-task spatiotemporal deep neural network for predicting penetration depth and morphology in laser welding
summary: In laser penetration welding, the assessment of penetration state and weld seam morphology plays a crucial role in determining the weld quality. This paper presents a comprehensive introduction of the innovative muti-task deep learning model that has the capability to predict penetration state, depth, and weld seam morphology with high accuracy. The monitoring platform relies on weld pool images captured during the laser welding process using a complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor camera. The proposed model integrates spatiotemporal features extracted from top weld pool images along with welding parameters, establishing a deep learning framework based on convolutional neural networks and state space models for more efficient extraction and processing of spatial-temporal information. Furthermore, a reliable method for constructing the dataset is proposed to enhance both robustness and generalization capability of the developed model. Validation results on the test set demonstrate that prediction accuracy for penetration state can reach 99.35%, while prediction error for penetration depth is 1.79 millimeter, and accuracy of reconstructing the weld cross-section is 95.65%. This study provides new insights and methodologies for in-situ quality control strategies in laser penetration welding systems.

AI.tldr: A multi-task deep learning model (CNN + state space model) predicts penetration state (99.35% accuracy), penetration depth (1.79 mm error), and weld cross-section morphology (95.65% accuracy) from CMOS-captured weld pool images and welding parameters in laser penetration welding.
AI.motivation: Accurate assessment of penetration state and weld seam morphology is essential for determining weld quality in laser penetration welding, motivating the need for a predictive model.
AI.method: A multi-task deep learning model integrating CNNs and state space models extracts spatiotemporal features from top weld pool images (captured by CMOS camera) combined with welding parameters. A robust dataset construction method enhances model generalization.
AI.result: Penetration state prediction accuracy: 99.35%; penetration depth prediction error: 1.79 mm; weld cross-section reconstruction accuracy: 95.65%.
AI.conclusion: The study provides new insights and methodologies for in-situ quality control in laser penetration welding systems.

title: Agentic Analysis for Agentic Infrastructure: An LLM-Powered Pipeline for Comparative Governance of DAO and Corporate AI Protocols
summary: As AI agent protocols proliferate, the governance structures shaping their interoperability standards remain empirically underexamined. We introduce an LLM-powered comparative pipeline for large-scale governance discourse analysis, integrating automated annotation, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to study socio-technical power structures at scale. We validate it on two contrasting standards for agent interoperability: ERC-8004 (permissionless, on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led). Analyzing 4,323 governance participation records, we combine LLM-assisted coding, topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis to examine how institutional design shapes thematic priorities and community structure. We find that while governance form influences substantive focus, both regimes exhibit comparable levels of participation inequality and community fragmentation. Discourse alignment is denser in the permissionless setting, suggesting that open governance may foster greater thematic convergence despite decentralized participation. These findings illustrate how LLM-assisted methods can advance the empirical study of technology governance, with implications for designing more equitable agentic AI standards. All data and code are openly available.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an LLM-powered comparative pipeline for analyzing governance discourses in AI agent interoperability standards, comparing ERC-8004 (permissionless, on-chain) and Google A2A (corporate-led) across 4,323 participation records, finding that while governance form influences thematic focus, both regimes exhibit similar participation inequality and community fragmentation, though open governance fosters greater thematic convergence.
AI.motivation: The governance structures shaping AI agent interoperability standards remain empirically underexamined despite the proliferation of agent protocols.
AI.method: LLM-assisted coding, neural topic modeling, and multi-layer network analysis applied to 4,323 governance participation records comparing ERC-8004 (permissionless) and Google A2A (corporate-led) standards.
AI.result: Governance form influences substantive focus, but both regimes show comparable participation inequality and community fragmentation. Open governance fosters denser discourse alignment and greater thematic convergence despite decentralized participation.
AI.conclusion: LLM-assisted methods can advance empirical study of technology governance, with implications for designing more equitable agentic AI standards.

title: Statistical and Structural Approaches to Algorithmic Fairness
summary: Modern machine learning systems have outgrown their origins as isolated predictive constructs, evolving into complex socio-technical architectures that actively mediate human opportunity. As algorithms increasingly determine access to economic and social opportunities, it has become widely recognized that these systems are deeply embedded with the structural inequalities and prejudices of their environments. The field of algorithmic fairness emerged in response to the growing recognition that models optimized for predictive accuracy can systematically disadvantage marginalized groups. Early mitigation strategies, however, rested on fragile simplifications that limited their effectiveness in complex socio-technical environments. This thesis identifies and addresses two fundamental limitations of contemporary fairness paradigms: the reliance on deterministic point estimates for auditing and the treatment of individuals as isolated entities devoid of structural context.

AI.tldr: This thesis identifies and addresses two fundamental limitations of algorithmic fairness: reliance on deterministic point estimates for auditing and treating individuals as isolated entities without structural context.
AI.motivation: Machine learning systems have become socio-technical architectures that mediate human opportunity, yet they embed structural inequalities and prejudices. Existing fairness mitigation strategies rely on fragile simplifications that limit their effectiveness.
AI.method: The thesis identifies two core limitations of current fairness paradigms: (1) deterministic point estimates for auditing, and (2) treating individuals as isolated entities devoid of structural context, and proposes addressing these issues.
AI.result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract; the paper identifies limitations and proposes to address them.
AI.conclusion: The two identified limitations (deterministic point estimates and isolated individual treatment) are fundamental barriers to effective fairness in complex socio-technical environments.

title: Elastic Time: Dynamic Frame Rate Bottlenecks for Neural Audio Coding
summary: Neural audio autoencoders have become a core component of compression, feature extraction, and generation. However, while existing systems support variable bitrate, the vast majority of models still operate at a fixed latent frame-rate, allocating equal temporal budget to regions with very different information density, which can result in unnecessarily long sequences. We introduce Elastic Time, a dynamic frame-rate bottleneck that converts fixed-frame-rate autoencoders to dynamic ones. Our method learns a lightweight latent predictor used to decide which frames can be skipped and later reconstructed, enabling efficient greedy boundary selection at inference. Experiments show our method enables deployment-time rate control while improving efficiency-quality tradeoffs relative to baselines. Overall, we provide a flexible mechanism for adjusting temporal resolution in audio autoencoders, potentially facilitating more efficient downstream modeling for generation and long-context tasks.

AI.tldr: Elastic Time introduces a dynamic frame-rate bottleneck that converts fixed-frame-rate neural audio autoencoders into dynamic ones, using a lightweight latent predictor to skip and reconstruct frames, enabling deployment-time rate control and improved efficiency-quality tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Existing neural audio autoencoders operate at a fixed latent frame-rate, allocating equal temporal budget to regions with varying information density, leading to unnecessarily long sequences and inefficient resource usage.
AI.method: A lightweight latent predictor is learned to decide which frames can be skipped and later reconstructed, enabling greedy boundary selection at inference for dynamic frame-rate control.
AI.result: Enables deployment-time rate control with improved efficiency-quality tradeoffs compared to fixed-frame-rate baselines.
AI.conclusion: Provides a flexible mechanism for adjusting temporal resolution in audio autoencoders, facilitating more efficient downstream modeling for generation and long-context tasks.

title: Extracting Neural Materials from Multi-view Images
summary: Neural materials can represent complex specular reflections and scattering effects in a compact, universal basis. However, acquiring and authoring such materials remains challenging. We present NeuMatEx, a differentiable inverse rendering method for extracting spatially varying neural materials from images. The nonlinear structure of neural material latent spaces makes optimization with naive inverse rendering infeasible. To address this, we train a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) that directly predicts initialbase color, neural material latents, and aleatoric uncertainty guides from images. This material prior provides a good initialization and better constrains our subsequent optimization using inverse path tracing. The predicted uncertainty further helps by anchoring high-confidence regions more tightly to the LMRM prediction, preventing lighting and complex specular effects from being baked into materials. Experiments on synthetic and real assets show that NeuMatEx extracts complex materials with better visual quality and material decomposition than PBR-based methods.

AI.tldr: NeuMatEx is a differentiable inverse rendering method that extracts spatially varying neural materials from images by using a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) as an initialization prior and uncertainty-guided optimization with inverse path tracing.
AI.motivation: Neural materials can represent complex specular reflections and scattering effects, but acquiring and authoring such materials from images remains challenging due to the nonlinear structure of neural material latent spaces making naive inverse rendering optimization infeasible.
AI.method: NeuMatEx trains a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) to predict initial base color, neural material latents, and aleatoric uncertainty guides from images, providing a good initialization and constraints for subsequent optimization using inverse path tracing. The predicted uncertainty anchors high-confidence regions more tightly to LMRM predictions.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real assets show that NeuMatEx extracts complex materials with better visual quality and material decomposition compared to PBR-based methods.
AI.conclusion: NeuMatEx effectively addresses the challenge of neural material extraction by combining a learned material prior with uncertainty-guided optimization, outperforming traditional PBR-based approaches in both visual quality and material decomposition.

title: Same Scrutiny, More Time: Eye Tracking Insights into Reviewing LLM-Labelled Code
comment: Accepted at the 41st IEEE/ACM International Conference on Automated Software Engineering (ASE 2026)
summary: Modern software development increasingly involves the use of large language models (LLMs) to generate code. Despite their rapid advancement, LLMs remain prone to errors and hallucinations, emphasizing the importance of careful code inspection. However, in practice, developers' trust in LLM-generated code and their willingness to review it thoroughly may differ from these recommendations. How developers actually behave when reviewing LLM-generated code remains largely unexplored. In this study, we conduct a Wizard-of-Oz experiment to examine how software engineers behave when code is explicitly labeled as LLM-generated during a code review task. We collect both behavioral data and participant feedback through eye-tracking and exit interviews. Combining Bayesian data analysis with qualitative analysis, we found that while the thoroughness of code review did not change for participants, they spent more time fixating on LLM-labelled code, indicating that the label itself influences attention. Practitioners also adapted their review strategy for LLM-labelled code by assessing the code based on specific criteria (e.g., logical correctness), or using the prompt to guide their review. These findings inform LLM-based tool design on labelling while incorporating the prompt as a software artifact. Our study reveals a gap between reviewers' intentions and actual reviewing behaviour, highlighting the need for software companies to revisit their AI policies (particularly regarding LLM-assisted development) to better support developers in reviewing LLM-generated code.

AI.tldr: This study uses a Wizard-of-Oz experiment with eye-tracking and interviews to investigate how labeling code as LLM-generated affects developers' code review behavior. Results show the label does not change review thoroughness but increases fixation time; developers adapt strategies using criteria like logical correctness and prompts to guide review.
AI.motivation: Despite LLMs being widely used for code generation, they remain error-prone. While careful inspection is recommended, it's unknown how developers actually behave when reviewing code explicitly labeled as LLM-generated—a gap this study aims to fill.
AI.method: Wizard-of-Oz experiment with code review tasks where code was explicitly labeled as LLM-generated. Data collected via eye-tracking (behavioral) and exit interviews (qualitative feedback). Analysis combined Bayesian data analysis and qualitative analysis.
AI.result: Review thoroughness did not change with LLM labeling, but participants spent more time fixating on LLM-labeled code. Developers adapted review strategies (e.g., evaluating logical correctness, using prompts as guidance). A gap between reviewers' intentions and actual behavior was identified.
AI.conclusion: Labeling code as LLM-generated influences attention and strategy adaptation but not thoroughness. Findings inform LLM-based tool design around labeling and prompt integration, and highlight the need for companies to update AI policies to better support LLM-generated code review.

title: Beyond Single-Source Cognitive Taskonomy:Multi-Source Task Relations through fMRI Transfer Learning
summary: Cognitive tasks are organized by shared and specialized neural processes. Masked fMRI reconstruction provides a common self-supervised objective for quantifying transfer relations among task states, but existing reconstruction-based taskonomies mainly study one-to-one transfer from a single source task to a target. Here, we extend an fMRI cognitive taskonomy from single-source to multi-source transfer across 23 Human Connectome Project task states and use Boolean Integer Programming (BIP) to analyze budget-constrained task allocation. We train 1,127 task-specific and transfer models. Single-source transfer is directional and paradigm structured: motor states transfer well within the motor paradigm but provide limited support to most non-motor targets, consistent with a shared sensorimotor execution system and effector-specific representations. Multi-source transfer depends on the composition of the source set, suggesting that many-to-one task relations are not fully captured by pairwise taskonomy alone. Across supervision budgets, BIP repeatedly allocates direct supervision to several 0-back and 2-back working-memory states, although these states are not consistently the strongest individual sources. This pattern may reflect the integration of perceptual, attentional, and executive processes in working-memory tasks. Together, these findings reveal a cross-paradigm-limited motor cluster and working-memory states with high priority under the specified global allocation objective. Our study extends reconstruction-based fMRI taskonomy from one-to-one transfer to many-to-one task relations and budget-constrained task dependencies.

AI.tldr: This study extends fMRI cognitive taskonomy from single-source to multi-source transfer across 23 HCP task states using Boolean Integer Programming, finding that single-source transfer is directional and paradigm-structured (motor transfers well within-paradigm but poorly to non-motor targets), while multi-source transfer reveals working-memory states as consistently high-priority sources under budget constraints.
AI.motivation: To extend existing reconstruction-based fMRI taskonomies from one-to-one transfer to many-to-one task relations and budget-constrained task dependencies, and to quantify transfer relations among task states using masked fMRI reconstruction.
AI.method: Trained 1,127 task-specific and transfer models across 23 Human Connectome Project task states; used masked fMRI reconstruction as a self-supervised objective; applied Boolean Integer Programming (BIP) for budget-constrained task allocation analysis.
AI.result: Single-source transfer is directional and paradigm-structured: motor states transfer well within motor paradigm but limited to non-motor targets. Multi-source transfer depends on source composition, not fully captured by pairwise taskonomy. BIP consistently allocates direct supervision to working-memory states (0-back and 2-back) across budgets.
AI.conclusion: Findings reveal a cross-paradigm-limited motor cluster and working-memory states with high priority under global allocation objectives, extending taskonomy to many-to-one transfer relations.

title: Knowledge-augmented Agentic AI for Mental Health Medication Information Seeking
summary: Patients increasingly seek medication information online, yet safety knowledge for psychiatric drugs is split between regulatory adverse-event records, which are authoritative but abstract, and patient narratives, which are experience-near but unvalidated. Integrating them without conflating evidence and anecdote is especially consequential in psychiatry, where poorly contextualised information can amplify fear, nocebo responses, and non-adherence. Here we develop a provenance-aware, knowledge-graph-based multi-agent framework unifying 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and twenty years of U.S. FDA Adverse Event Reporting System records for nine antidepressants. A large-language-model entity-recognition pipeline benchmarked against physician annotations reached highest F1 scores of 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions. The two community platforms were far more concordant with each other (overlap up to a Jaccard similarity of 0.905) than with regulatory reports, indicating that patient-generated data form a partly independent safety signal. For sertraline, many adverse events appeared in community sources hundreds of days before the corresponding FDA date. A Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA vocabularies preserves provenance, keeping every claim traceable and regulatory facts distinct from patient experience. These results establish source-aware integration as a route to more auditable psychiatric medication information, with usefulness and patient benefit to be tested prospectively.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent knowledge-graph framework integrating Reddit posts, WebMD reviews, and FDA adverse-event records for nine antidepressants achieves high entity-recognition accuracy (F1 > 0.96), reveals that patient-generated data (Jaccard similarity up to 0.905) form a partly independent safety signal with earlier signals than FDA records, and preserves provenance via standardized vocabularies (ATC-N, ICD-10, MedDRA).
AI.motivation: Patients seeking online medication information face a disconnect: regulatory adverse-event records are authoritative but abstract, while patient narratives are experience-near but unvalidated. This gap is especially problematic in psychiatry, where poorly contextualized information can amplify fear, nocebo responses, and non-adherence.
AI.method: Developed a provenance-aware, knowledge-graph-based multi-agent framework integrating 466,525 Reddit posts, 60,782 WebMD reviews, and 20 years of FDA FAERS data for nine antidepressants. Used an LLM-based entity-recognition pipeline benchmarked against physician annotations. Built a Neo4j knowledge graph grounded in ATC-N, ICD-10, and MedDRA vocabularies.
AI.result: Entity recognition achieved F1 scores of 0.969 for medications and 0.973 for conditions. Community platforms showed high concordance (Jaccard similarity up to 0.905) but were substantially different from regulatory reports. For sertraline, many adverse events appeared in community sources hundreds of days before the FDA-reported date.
AI.conclusion: Source-aware integration enables auditable psychiatric medication information by keeping every claim traceable and regulatory facts distinct from patient experience; usefulness and patient benefit remain to be tested prospectively.

title: Topology-Informed Neural Networks for Flood Detection in Optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar Imagery
summary: Floods frequently impact regions around the world. Rapid and accurate flood detection is crucial for emergency response and timely mitigation of human and economic loss. The expanding availability of satellite data and advances in artificial intelligence have enhanced monitoring of environmental hazards, but many flood events remain challenging to detect because cloud cover obscures optical satellite imagery. Rambour et al. introduced the SEN12-FLOOD dataset and extracted per-image features using a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network backbone, then fed these features into a gated recurrent unit network to show that temporal information can substantially improve accuracy compared to single-image baselines. More recently, Chamatidis et al. showed that a vision transformer can achieve strong performance with popular convolutional architectures. However, these models typically function as opaque black boxes, making it difficult to interpret their decision boundaries, learned features, and internal reasoning, especially in safety-critical domains like remote sensing. In contrast, topological data analysis (TDA) provides a mathematically grounded framework for capturing global structural features of data. TDA has emerged as a powerful tool for analyzing complex imagery, especially imagery with geometrically interpretable structures, of which floods are a prime candidate. In this work, we systematically evaluate topological descriptors for flood detection using the open-source SEN12-FLOOD dataset. By extracting topological features from each image and incorporating them into neural networks, we demonstrate that topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement existing networks to yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using topological data analysis (TDA) descriptors for flood detection in satellite imagery, demonstrating that topological features carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement neural networks (CNNs, ViTs) to improve robustness and interpretability, especially under cloud cover conditions, using the SEN12-FLOOD dataset.
AI.motivation: Flood detection is critical for emergency response, but existing deep learning models (ResNet+GRU, ViTs) are opaque black boxes that are difficult to interpret, especially in safety-critical remote sensing domains. Cloud cover further complicates optical satellite imagery analysis.
AI.method: Systematically extract topological descriptors (via TDA) from satellite images in the SEN12-FLOOD dataset and incorporate them into neural network architectures (complementing CNN/Transformer backbones) for flood detection.
AI.result: Topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and, when integrated with existing networks, yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.
AI.conclusion: TDA provides a mathematically grounded, interpretable framework that complements deep learning for flood detection, enhancing robustness and model transparency in safety-critical remote sensing applications.

title: ConcoLixir: Reactive LLM Discovery Oracles for Python Concolic Testing
summary: Concolic testing combines concrete execution with symbolic constraint solving, but Python programs expose recurring limits. Library calls can cause symbolic variables to downgrade to concrete values. Regular expressions, checksums, parsers, and other semantic operations can be hard to solve, and exploration can plateau on already covered paths. We present ConcoLixir, a reactive LLM extension for Python concolic execution. The LLM acts as a discovery oracle, not a replacement for the solver or a correctness oracle. It generates initial seeds, proposes concrete inputs after solver failures, and targets uncovered code when coverage stalls. Each candidate is executed concolically, and only observed coverage and collected path constraints guide later exploration. Across synthetic, real-world, and library targets, ConcoLixir improves mean line coverage over the baseline concolic tester without an LLM oracle by 8.6, 15.1, and 17.0 percentage points. The gains are strongest near semantic barriers and library boundaries, and the full evaluation costs \$1.63 in API charges. These results show that bounded LLM discovery can complement symbolic reasoning without replacing it.

AI.tldr: ConcoLixir is a reactive LLM extension for Python concolic testing that uses the LLM as a discovery oracle (generating seeds, proposing inputs after solver failures, targeting uncovered code) without replacing the symbolic solver. It improves line coverage by 8.6-17.0 percentage points over baseline, costs $1.63, and is most effective near semantic barriers and library boundaries.
AI.motivation: Concolic testing of Python programs faces recurring limitations: library calls downgrade symbolic variables to concrete values, semantic operations (regex, checksums, parsers) are hard to solve, and coverage exploration plateaus on already covered paths.
AI.method: The LLM acts as a discovery oracle (not a solver replacement): (1) generates initial seeds, (2) proposes concrete inputs after solver failures, (3) targets uncovered code when coverage stalls. Candidates are executed concolically; only observed coverage and path constraints guide later exploration.
AI.result: Improves mean line coverage over baseline by 8.6, 15.1, and 17.0 percentage points on synthetic, real-world, and library targets respectively. Full evaluation costs $1.63 in API charges. Gains are strongest near semantic barriers and library boundaries.
AI.conclusion: Bounded LLM discovery can complement symbolic reasoning in concolic testing without replacing the underlying solver.

title: GeMoE: Gating Entropy is All You Need for Uncertainty-aware Adaptive Routing in MoE-based Large Vision-Language Models
summary: With the increase in model parameters and training data, the instruction following and generalization capabilities of Large VisionLanguage Models (LVLMs) have been significantly improved. Based on the Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture, LVLMs expand their parameter capacity while maintaining the inference cost. However, traditional MoE methods employ a Top-k static routing strategy, which fails to account for variations in the input and adaptively select the number of experts, resulting in suboptimal resource utilization. In this paper, we propose viewing token routing as an information encoding task, framing dynamic routing as a Minimum Description Length (MDL) problem in encoding By validating the connection between MDL and gating entropy in the MoE scenario, we introduce Gating Entropy-based Uncertainty-aware Adaptive Routing (GeMoE) for MoE. Unlike traditional static or heuristic-based dynamic routing methods, GeMoE explicitly models the trade-off between model complexity and performance. By using gating entropy to assess the complexity of tokens, GeMoE adaptively determines the number of experts each token should engage. On a wide range of backbones and benchmarks, our method achieves 99.5% average performance retention compared to the original static routing, while improving average expert activation sparsity by 36.5%.

AI.tldr: GeMoE introduces a Minimum Description Length (MDL) perspective to token routing in Mixture-of-Experts LVLMs, replacing static Top-k routing with gating entropy-based adaptive expert selection that achieves 99.5% performance retention while improving expert activation sparsity by 36.5%.
AI.motivation: Traditional Top-k static routing in MoE-based LVLMs fails to adaptively select the number of experts based on input variation, leading to suboptimal resource utilization.
AI.method: Frames token routing as an MDL problem; establishes a connection between MDL and gating entropy; uses gating entropy to assess token complexity and adaptively determine the number of experts per token.
AI.result: Achieves 99.5% average performance retention vs. static routing, with 36.5% improvement in average expert activation sparsity across multiple backbones and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the trade-off between model complexity and performance via gating entropy enables more efficient resource utilization in MoE-based LVLMs without sacrificing performance.

title: Accelerating Skill Assessment in Chess: A Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System
comment: Accepted at the IEEE Conference on Games (IEEE CoG) 2026
summary: Rating systems such as Elo serve as the gold standard for matchmaking in competitive chess. However, they inherently suffer from response lag due to their exclusive reliance on match outcomes, neglecting the granular quality of gameplay. Nevertheless, incorporating move-by-move information into rating adjustments presents a significant challenge given the substantial noise and the vastness of the game-state space. To address this, we propose the Drift-Diffusion-Enhanced Elo Rating System (DD-Elo), a novel skill assessment framework inspired by the drift diffusion model (DDM) from cognitive neuroscience. By modeling skill expression as a decision-making process, our model integrates move-level data to capture rapid skill fluctuations. We provide a rigorous mathematical derivation proving that DD-Elo maintains a bounded deviation from the traditional Elo system, ensuring theoretical alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DD-Elo adapts to skill changes faster than Elo. Our findings suggest that DD-Elo offers an explainable, highly responsive, and backward-compatible solution for chess rating ecosystems. The implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/Aquila-zhou1/DD-Elo .

AI.tldr: DD-Elo is a novel chess rating system that enhances traditional Elo by incorporating move-level data via a drift diffusion model, enabling faster adaptation to skill changes while maintaining theoretical alignment with Elo.
AI.motivation: Traditional Elo ratings suffer from response lag because they only use match outcomes, ignoring granular gameplay quality. Incorporating move-by-move information is challenging due to noise and the vast game-state space.
AI.method: The authors propose DD-Elo, inspired by the drift diffusion model (DDM) from cognitive neuroscience, which models skill expression as a decision-making process and integrates move-level data to capture rapid skill fluctuations. A rigorous mathematical derivation proves bounded deviation from traditional Elo.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that DD-Elo adapts to skill changes faster than traditional Elo while maintaining bounded deviation from it.
AI.conclusion: DD-Elo offers an explainable, highly responsive, and backward-compatible solution for chess rating ecosystems, with public implementation code available.

title: A General Framework for Learning Algebraic Properties from Cayley Graphs using Graph Neural Networks
summary: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework for predicting the solvability of finite groups from their Cayley graph representations was introduced in [1]. In the present work, we generalize this approach and develop a property-independent framework for learning algebraic properties of finite groups directly from Cayley graphs. As representative case studies, we consider abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability. Using a common GNN architecture and training pipeline, we investigate the extent to which algebraic structure can be recovered from graph-based representations alone. Results on a collection of finite groups drawn from several families demonstrate that the framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from their associated Cayley graphs. These findings suggest that substantial algebraic information is encoded in graph representations and can be extracted through GNNs. More broadly, the proposed framework provides a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to the study of algebraic properties of finite groups.

AI.tldr: The paper generalizes a previous GNN framework to learn multiple algebraic properties (abelianity, nilpotency, solvability) of finite groups from Cayley graphs, showing that GNNs can extract algebraic structure from graph representations.
AI.motivation: To develop a property-independent framework that can learn various algebraic properties of finite groups directly from Cayley graph representations, moving beyond the property-specific approach in prior work.
AI.method: A common GNN architecture and training pipeline is used to learn three algebraic properties (abelianity, nilpotency, solvability) from Cayley graphs of finite groups drawn from several families.
AI.result: The framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from Cayley graphs, demonstrating that algebraic information is encoded in graph representations and extractable via GNNs.
AI.conclusion: Substantial algebraic information is encoded in Cayley graphs and can be extracted through GNNs, providing a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to study algebraic properties of finite groups.

title: Quantum Mutant Equivalence via Transpilation
summary: Mutation testing evaluates test suite quality by introducing artificial faults (mutants) and checking whether tests detect (kill) them. A central challenge is the equivalent mutant problem: some mutants are syntactically different from the original program but semantically identical to it and therefore cannot be killed by any test. If left unidentified, such mutants waste testing effort and distort mutation scores. In quantum software, mutation testing is increasingly used, but the equivalent mutant problem remains unsolved. A recent study generated more than 700,000 quantum circuit mutants and found that roughly half survived the available tests, making it unclear whether these survivors reflect weak tests or semantic equivalence. We propose Transpiler-Based Equivalence (TBE), a lightweight approach that identifies equivalent quantum mutants by transpiling original and mutated circuits under the same configuration and comparing their resulting OpenQASM code. We evaluate TBE on 348,299 surviving mutants, 92,011 of which are equivalent; TBE identifies 29,536 of them (32.1%) as equivalent while achieving 100% precision and 82% accuracy.

AI.tldr: Mutation testing in quantum software suffers from the equivalent mutant problem, where mutants are syntactically different but semantically identical. The authors propose Transpiler-Based Equivalence (TBE), which identifies equivalent quantum mutants by comparing transpiled OpenQASM code of original and mutated circuits. Evaluated on 348,299 surviving mutants, TBE identifies 32.1% of equivalents with 100% precision and 82% accuracy.
AI.motivation: The equivalent mutant problem remains unsolved in quantum software mutation testing. A prior study found roughly half of over 700,000 quantum circuit mutants survived tests, making it unclear whether survivors reflect weak tests or semantic equivalence, motivating the need to identify equivalent mutants.
AI.method: TBE transpiles both original and mutated quantum circuits under the same configuration and compares their resulting OpenQASM code; if the transpiled outputs are identical, the mutant is deemed equivalent.
AI.result: Tested on 348,299 surviving mutants (92,011 equivalent). TBE identified 29,536 (32.1%) as equivalent, achieving 100% precision and 82% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: TBE is a lightweight, precise approach for identifying equivalent quantum mutants, helping to reduce wasted testing effort and improve the reliability of mutation scores in quantum software.

title: Beyond Aesthetics: Quantifying Information Loss in Turbid Scenes
summary: Visibility in underwater environments degrades rapidly under turbid conditions, yet the effects on computer-vision models remain unclear. This issue is compounded by reliance on synthetic turbidity datasets, which may misrepresent real-world information loss. To address this gap, we introduce the Turbid Underwater Baseline (TUB) dataset, comprising 1,320 images captured under extreme turbidity and over 16,000 high-confidence ground-truth segmentation masks. We additionally propose PCD, a metric derived from phase congruency maps that is invariant to contrast and aims to capture the loss of structural information in real turbidity. We show that PCD correlates strongly with the performance of instance segmentation models on both real and synthetic turbid images, whereas common metrics in the field show weak to no correlation at all. The dataset and relevant code can be found on the project page: https://vap.aau.dk/pcd

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the TUB dataset (1,320 real turbid underwater images with 16,000+ segmentation masks) and PCD metric (phase congruency-based, contrast-invariant) to measure structural information loss in turbidity, showing PCD strongly correlates with instance segmentation model performance while common metrics fail.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic turbidity datasets may misrepresent real-world information loss, and current metrics fail to correlate with computer vision model performance under turbid conditions. There is a need for real turbid data and a reliable metric.
AI.method: Collected 1,320 real underwater images under extreme turbidity with 16,000+ high-confidence ground-truth segmentation masks. Proposed PCD (Phase Congruency Degradation), a metric based on phase congruency maps that is invariant to contrast, designed to capture structural information loss in real turbidity.
AI.result: PCD shows strong correlation with instance segmentation model performance on both real and synthetic turbid images, whereas common existing metrics show weak to no correlation.
AI.conclusion: The TUB dataset and PCD metric address the gap in evaluating computer vision under real turbid underwater conditions, providing a more reliable benchmark and evaluation metric than synthetic data and existing metrics.

title: Governing Actions, Not Agents: Institutional Attestation as a Governance Model for Autonomous AI Systems
summary: Autonomous AI agents may begin to perform consequential, irreversible actions such as clinical prescribing and production software deployment. This paper observes that human institutions have governed powerful autonomous actors not by monitoring their reasoning but by requiring independently attested evidence at the point of consequential action. We formalise this institutional pattern as a computational governance model for AI agent systems. Under the proposed model, an agent retains full autonomy over planning and reasoning but holds no execution authority over designated high-risk actions. Execution is conditional on preconditions that are each independently attested by a separate authoritative source, cryptographically bound to a declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy. Decisions are recorded in a tamper-evident log amenable to independent re-verification. We present a proof-of-concept implementation and illustrate the model with examples from software deployment and clinical prescribing.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a computational governance model for AI agent systems where agents retain autonomy over planning and reasoning but cannot execute high-risk actions without independent attestation from authoritative sources, cryptographically bound to declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy.
AI.motivation: Autonomous AI agents may perform consequential, irreversible actions (e.g., clinical prescribing, software deployment), yet human institutions govern powerful actors not by monitoring reasoning but by requiring independently attested evidence at the point of action—a pattern lacking in current AI agent systems.
AI.method: Formalizes a governance model where agents have full planning autonomy but no execution authority over high-risk actions; execution requires preconditions independently attested by separate authoritative sources, cryptographically bound to declared intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy, with decisions recorded in a tamper-evident log.
AI.result: A proof-of-concept implementation and illustrative examples from software deployment and clinical prescribing demonstrate the model's feasibility.
AI.conclusion: The proposed attestation-based governance model offers a principled way to grant AI agents autonomy while maintaining institutional accountability for consequential actions.

title: Fast LeWorldModel
summary: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs), including recent LeWorldModel (LeWM), have become a promising foundation for reconstruction-free visual world models. For visual planning, however, LeWM evaluates candidate action sequences by repeatedly applying a local one-step latent transition model. This autoregressive rollout makes planning computationally expensive and exposes the predicted trajectory to accumulated latent errors as the horizon grows. We propose Fast LeWorldModel (Fast-LeWM), a fast latent world model that replaces repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction. Given the current latent and a candidate action sequence, Fast-LeWM encodes its prefixes and predicts the future latents reached after executing those prefixes in parallel. By making action prefixes the basic prediction unit, Fast-LeWM directly models action effects accumulated to different extents over multiple horizons. This prefix-level supervision forces the model to learn how states continuously evolve under different action prefixes, rather than only fitting one-step state transitions. During planning, the predictor can use the last prefix token from the encoded action sequence to evaluate the corresponding future latent without explicitly rolling through each intermediate imagined state. Across multiple tasks, Fast-LeWM improves average success over LeWM while substantially reducing planning time, achieving lower open-loop latent loss whose growth becomes significantly slower as the rollout horizon increases.

AI.tldr: Fast-LeWM replaces the autoregressive rollout of JEPA-based world models with action-prefix prediction, enabling parallel prediction of future latents. This reduces planning time and accumulated latent errors while improving task success.
AI.motivation: LeWM uses autoregressive one-step latent transitions for planning, which is computationally expensive and accumulates errors over long horizons. The authors aim to mitigate these issues.
AI.method: Fast-LeWM encodes action prefixes (subsequences of a full action sequence) and predicts the latent states reached after executing those prefixes, all in parallel. The final prefix token directly yields the future latent for planning without intermediate rollout steps.
AI.result: Fast-LeWM improves average success rate over LeWM, substantially reduces planning time, and achieves lower open-loop latent loss with significantly slower error growth as horizon increases.
AI.conclusion: Action-prefix prediction is an effective alternative to autoregressive latent rollout for visual world models, offering faster and more accurate planning.

title: Knowledge-Based Pull Requests: A Trusted Workflow for Agent-Mediated Knowledge Collaboration
summary: AI coding agents are changing the bottleneck in software collaboration: code is increasingly cheap, while understanding intent, negotiating scope, and governing long-term project responsibility remain costly. This paper proposes \emph{Knowledge-Based Pull Requests} (KPR), a trusted workflow for agent-mediated software collaboration across trust boundaries, including open source, enterprise, vendor, contractor, and customer-driven settings. In KPR, an external collaborator's local code, tests, and cleaned agent interaction trace are treated as knowledge sources rather than as the default merge candidate. Agents distill these sources into a human-confirmed knowledge package and render it into reviewer-facing forms such as design memos, risk checklists, test plans, or implementation briefs. A project-owned inner trusted coding agent then regenerates candidate code inside the receiving project's environment under repository context, engineering conventions, tests, and security policy. KPR therefore separates two decisions that traditional pull requests often collapse: whether the knowledge should enter the project, and whether a particular implementation should be merged. We contribute the KPR workflow, a candidate artifact schema, a cost-accounting view, a collaboration gateway architecture, a minimal controlled simulation pilot over seven merged public pull requests, and an evaluation agenda. The pilot shows that KPR packages can be instantiated from real PR material and stress-tested under description ablation, diff ablation, and synthetic poisoned-patch conditions. We position KPR as an empirically testable workflow: its value depends on whether auditable extraction, transformation, and project-side regeneration reduce the cost of understanding and reworking high-context external changes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Knowledge-Based Pull Requests (KPR), a workflow where external code contributions are first distilled into knowledge packages by AI agents, reviewed by humans, and then regenerated by a trusted inner agent within the project's own environment, separating the decision of accepting knowledge from accepting implementation.
AI.motivation: Traditional pull requests conflate two decisions—whether knowledge should enter the project and whether a particular implementation should be merged—leading to high costs in understanding intent, negotiating scope, and governing long-term project responsibility, especially as AI coding agents make code generation cheap.
AI.method: KPR workflow: (1) external collaborator submits code, tests, and agent interaction traces as knowledge sources; (2) agents distill these into a human-confirmed knowledge package (design memos, risk checklists, test plans, implementation briefs); (3) a project-owned inner trusted agent regenerates code inside the receiving project's environment under repository context, engineering conventions, tests, and security policy.
AI.result: A minimal controlled simulation pilot over seven merged public pull requests demonstrates that KPR packages can be instantiated from real PR material and stress-tested under description ablation, diff ablation, and synthetic poisoned-patch conditions.
AI.conclusion: KPR is positioned as an empirically testable workflow whose value depends on whether auditable extraction, transformation, and project-side regeneration reduce the cost of understanding and reworking high-context external changes.

title: Layer-Specific Prompt Fusion Discovery via Differentiable Search in Vision Foundation Models
summary: Visual prompt tuning has emerged as a parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach for adapting large-scale Vision Transformers (ViTs) to downstream tasks. As its learnable prompts are applied in input and feature spaces, prior to jointly going through attention in transformer layers, the most commonly used scheme for fusing image and prompt tokens is concatenation or addition. In this paper, we aim to study a fundamental yet essential problem in visual prompt tuning: whether a single fusion scheme tends to yield better results, and whether that would be beneficial to develop a hybrid fusion scheme. To this end, we formulate the task as a bi-level optimization problem, and solve it leveraging differentiable architecture search. In this context, the learnable prompts and their fusion schemes are jointly optimized. To enrich the search space in the architecture search, we propose two additional fusion schemes, namely, affine transformation and cross-attention, in addition to concatenation and addition. Extensive experiments on 34 datasets spanning VTAB-1k, FGVC, and HTA show consistent gains over prompt-tuning baselines. With a frozen ViT backbone, our method delivers a favorable accuracy--latency--parameter trade-off compared with VPT-Deep and recent variants. Our findings reveal that how prompts fuse with image tokens plays a significant role in visual prompt tuning, and a hybrid fusion fashion can more effectively leverage layer semantics of ViTs, contributing a novel perspective for visual prompt-tuning research.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hybrid fusion scheme for visual prompt tuning in ViTs, formulated as a bi-level optimization problem solved via differentiable architecture search, achieving consistent gains across 34 datasets by jointly optimizing learnable prompts and their fusion methods (concatenation, addition, affine transformation, cross-attention).
AI.motivation: To investigate whether a single fusion scheme (concatenation/addition) for prompt-image token integration is optimal, and whether a hybrid fusion scheme can improve visual prompt tuning performance.
AI.method: Formulates visual prompt tuning as a bi-level optimization problem solved via differentiable architecture search, jointly optimizing learnable prompts and fusion schemes (concatenation, addition, affine transformation, cross-attention).
AI.result: Consistent gains over prompt-tuning baselines across 34 datasets (VTAB-1k, FGVC, HTA), with favorable accuracy-latency-parameter trade-off compared to VPT-Deep and variants.
AI.conclusion: The fusion scheme between prompts and image tokens significantly impacts visual prompt tuning performance; a hybrid fusion fashion better leverages layer semantics of ViTs, offering a new perspective for visual prompt-tuning research.

title: COrigami: An AI Pipeline for Co-Designing Flat-Foldable Visually Recognisable Origami
summary: While generative AI has achieved remarkable success in solving problems with verifiable solutions, generating physical art that satisfies both strict geometric constraints and subjective visual aesthetics remains a challenge. This paper presents an approach to tackle these difficulties in the domain of computational origami, a mathematically rigid environment that grounds artistic design within the equations of flat foldability. We present COrigami, an end-to-end AI-driven pipeline that assists the design cycle by generating crease patterns from natural language. Our pipeline involves generating a semantic stick figure, computing a base packing, solving for a flat-foldable crease pattern, shaping the flat-folded crease pattern, and refining the generated model using reinforcement learning driven by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop. Our system acts as a highly effective collaborative assistant, generating structural starting points that human artists can further expand and shape. By integrating algorithmic optimisation with autonomous aesthetic critique, this work demonstrates how AI systems can satisfy multi-objective physical constraints to enable reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity.

AI.tldr: COrigami is an end-to-end AI pipeline that generates flat-foldable origami crease patterns from natural language descriptions, combining geometric constraint solving with aesthetic evaluation via reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Generative AI struggles with physical art that must simultaneously satisfy strict geometric constraints and subjective aesthetic criteria; computational origami provides a mathematically rigorous testbed for this challenge.
AI.method: The pipeline consists of five stages: (1) semantic stick figure generation from natural language, (2) base packing computation, (3) flat-foldable crease pattern solving, (4) shaping the flat-folded pattern, and (5) reinforcement learning refinement driven by an autonomous aesthetic evaluation loop.
AI.result: The system generates structural starting points for origami crease patterns that human artists can further develop, demonstrating successful integration of geometric constraint satisfaction with aesthetic optimization.
AI.conclusion: AI systems can satisfy multi-objective physical constraints to enable reliable, mathematically grounded co-creativity between human artists and generative models.

title: Dataset Usage Inference without Shadow Models or Held-out Data
comment: Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Synthetic & Adversarial ForEnsics (SAFE), CVPR 2026 (non-archival)
summary: How much of my data was used to train a machine learning model? Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) aims to answer this by estimating what fraction of a dataset contributed to a model's training. However, existing DUI methods rely on assumptions that rarely hold in practice: they require training expensive shadow models to imitate the target model, and they assume access to both known training samples and an in-distribution held-out set confirmed to be absent from training. These conditions make current approaches impractical for modern large models and real data ownership disputes. We introduce a practical DUI framework that removes these constraints. Our method requires neither shadow models nor real held-out data. Instead, it generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and casts DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate what share of the candidate dataset was used during training. Experiments on large image generative models show that our method reliably quantifies dataset usage, providing a practical tool for data owners to determine how much of their data was used to train a model.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a practical Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) framework that estimates how much of a dataset was used to train a model, without requiring shadow models or real held-out data. It uses synthetic non-member samples and mixture proportion estimation to quantify dataset usage, validated on large image generative models.
AI.motivation: Existing DUI methods are impractical because they require training expensive shadow models and need access to both known training samples and in-distribution held-out data confirmed absent from training. These constraints make current approaches unsuitable for modern large models and real-world data ownership disputes.
AI.method: The method generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and casts DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate the fraction of a candidate dataset used during training, eliminating the need for shadow models or real held-out data.
AI.result: Experiments on large image generative models demonstrate that the method reliably quantifies dataset usage.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a practical tool for data owners to determine how much of their data was used to train a model, overcoming key limitations of prior DUI approaches.

title: A Deterministic Control Plane for LLM Coding Agents
comment: 45 pages, 9 figures, 13 tables. Dataset and reproduction scripts: Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20780913. Ancillary files include report.json, organizations.txt, and figure-reproduction scripts
summary:
LLM coding harnesses grant agents broad file and shell access, yet the configuration layer that steers them -- rules files, agent definitions, IDE-specific markdown -- is largely unmanaged. A prevalence study of 10,008 public GitHub repositories (n=6,145 agent config files) finds that agent configurations propagate as undeclared shared components: 10.1% of tracked paths are SHA-256 exact duplicates across independent repositories (fork-adjusted, threshold-independent), with 75.5% of clone pairs crossing organisational boundaries. Two further patterns are indicative: configurations are rarely revised (58% single-commit; 0.4 vs 0.6 commits/month age-normalised against CI/CD workflows), and rarely declare permission boundaries (<1% of agent configs vs 33% of Actions workflows, n=31 true positives).
  We propose a deterministic control plane above the harness that maps one-to-one to these gaps. Rel(AI)Build treats agent definitions as a managed supply chain (SHA-256 content addressing, HMAC-stamped lockfiles, hash-chained audit logs); enforces tiered permissions and attack-derived blocklists before LLM invocation; gates feature work through a phase state machine with requirement-to-file-to-test traceability; compiles a single canonical definition to seven IDE targets; and detects prompt drift via Jaccard similarity. Conformance tests on injected violations confirm each mechanism enforces its stated invariant; developer outcomes remain future work. Governance of this layer must be deterministic and tool-agnostic -- not delegated to further LLM orchestration.

AI.tldr: Agent configuration files in LLM coding tools are largely unmanaged, with widespread duplication across repositories and minimal permission declarations. Rel(AI)Build proposes a deterministic control plane to manage these configurations as a supply chain.
AI.motivation: Agent configurations (rules files, agent definitions, IDE-specific markdown) that steer LLM coding harnesses are currently unmanaged, lacking governance, versioning, and permission boundaries — creating security and reproducibility risks.
AI.method: Prevalence study of 10,008 public GitHub repositories (n=6,145 agent config files) analyzing duplication, commit frequency, and permission declarations. Proposal of Rel(AI)Build: a deterministic control plane using SHA-256 content addressing, HMAC-stamped lockfiles, hash-chained audit logs, tiered permissions, attack blocklists, phase state machine with traceability, multi-IDE compilation, and Jaccard similarity-based prompt drift detection.
AI.result: 10.1% of tracked paths are SHA-256 exact duplicates across independent repos (75.5% cross-organizational); 58% single-commit configs; <1% of agent configs declare permissions vs 33% of Actions workflows. Conformance tests confirm each Rel(AI)Build mechanism enforces its stated invariant.
AI.conclusion: Governance of the agent configuration layer must be deterministic and tool-agnostic, not delegated to further LLM orchestration. Developer outcomes remain future work.

title: What Do Deepfake Benchmarks Measure? An Audit Using Frozen Self-Supervised Representations
summary: As deepfake generators approach perceptual indistinguishability, reliable detection becomes critical. Yet, detectors that score well on benchmarks routinely fail in the wild. A concerning feedback loop has emerged: benchmarks drive increasingly complex, engineered detectors, yet if those benchmarks do not reflect real-world deepfakes, this complexity may be solving the wrong problem entirely. This raises a prior question: what are these benchmarks actually measuring? We conduct an audit of video, image, and audio deepfake benchmarks using a deliberately simple diagnostic. If a linear probe on frozen, general-purpose self-supervised representations can approximate the performance of a bespoke detector, the benchmark is largely rewarding general modality understanding rather than forensic understanding. This has two implications: the benchmark may not reflect realistic threat models, and it raises the question of whether the bespoke detectors the probe approaches are truly learning forensic understanding. We observe, across three modalities, linear probes on general-purpose self-supervised representations closely approach the performance of bespoke detectors. We further show that generator-level difficulty is partly explained by Frechet geometry in the same representation space. Together, these results support a benchmark-audit view of deepfake detection: before high scores are read as evidence of forensic understanding, it is worth asking how much of the benchmark is already solved by general-purpose representations.

AI.tldr: The paper audits deepfake detection benchmarks across video, image, and audio modalities, finding that linear probes on general-purpose self-supervised representations closely approximate bespoke detector performance, suggesting benchmarks reward general modality understanding rather than forensic understanding.
AI.motivation: Deepfake detectors that score well on benchmarks often fail in real-world settings, creating a feedback loop where benchmarks drive increasingly complex detectors that may be solving the wrong problem. This raises the prior question of what these benchmarks are actually measuring.
AI.method: The authors conduct an audit across video, image, and audio deepfake benchmarks using a simple diagnostic: comparing a linear probe on frozen, general-purpose self-supervised representations against bespoke detectors. They also analyze generator-level difficulty through Frechet geometry in representation space.
AI.result: Linear probes on general-purpose self-supervised representations closely approach the performance of bespoke detectors across all three modalities. Generator-level difficulty is partially explained by Frechet geometry in the same representation space.
AI.conclusion: Before high benchmark scores are interpreted as evidence of forensic understanding, it is worth assessing how much of the benchmark is already solved by general-purpose representations. The paper proposes a benchmark-audit view for deepfake detection.

title: The Verification Horizon: No Silver Bullet for Coding Agent Rewards
comment: Authors are listed alphabetically by their first names
summary: A classical intuition holds that verifying a solution is easier than producing one. For today's coding agents, this intuition is being inverted: as foundation models develop stronger reasoning capabilities and engineering harnesses grow more sophisticated, generating complex candidate solutions is no longer difficult -- reliably verifying them has become the harder problem. Every verifier we can build is only a proxy for human intent, never the intent itself.
