AI.tldr: DualEval is a latent model-item calibration framework that jointly models model ability with item difficulty and sharpness, unifying static benchmarks and arena-style preference evaluation across four domains using 18 LLMs, producing reliable rankings and enabling benchmark compression and anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation relies on two complementary but often disconnected signals: static benchmarks with objective correctness labels and arena-style preference data. This disconnection limits the interpretability and efficiency of evaluation pipelines.
AI.method: DualEval is a latent model-item calibration framework that represents models and evaluation items in a shared space, jointly estimating model ability together with item difficulty and sharpness. It applies across four domains using 18 frontier LLMs, static benchmark labels, and reward-model scores validated against held-out human preferences.
AI.result: The framework produces reliable and balanced model rankings, and its learned item-level profiles support benchmark compression for sample-efficient evaluation and anomaly detection for contamination or outlier analysis.
AI.conclusion: DualEval unifies static and arena-style evaluation through joint model-item calibration, producing model rankings and item-level diagnostics that support more sample-efficient, interpretable, and auditable evaluation pipelines.

title: Position Rebinding Cache Reuse: Replay-Free Visual Revisiting for Interleaved Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Interleaved multimodal reasoning improves visual grounding by revisiting visual evidence during multi-step generation, yet existing methods typically rely on token replay, repeatedly forwarding selected visual tokens. A natural shortcut is to reuse the historical visual key-value (KV) cache directly. However, we identify a critical failure mode of this strategy: cached visual keys are already bound to their original positional context. Such stale positional binding distorts attention under later decoding contexts and can trigger severe autoregressive decoding collapse. This failure suggests that effective cache reuse requires reconstructing visual evidence under positions compatible with the current decoding state, rather than directly copying position-bound historical cache entries. To this end, we propose Position Rebinding Cache Reuse (PRCR), a cache-level framework for replay-free visual revisiting. PRCR stores raw visual KV cache together with their original spatial coordinates, then reassigns position-compatible coordinates to select entries and rebinds their keys before injecting the reconstructed cache into the active decoder cache. This design reuses historical visual evidence while preserving textual positional continuity and relative visual structure. Experiments across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks show that PRCR achieves replay-level or better performance, improving average accuracy by 5 percent and reducing visual-revisiting computation by up to tens of thousands of times.

AI.tldr: Existing visual revisiting methods either replay tokens (costly) or reuse cached visual KV pairs, but directly reusing cached KV pairs causes autoregressive collapse due to stale positional binding. PRCR solves this by storing raw visual KV cache with spatial coordinates and rebinding them to position-compatible coordinates before injection, achieving replay-level or better performance with up to tens of thousands of times computation reduction.
AI.motivation: Current interleaved multimodal reasoning methods rely on token replay (repeatedly forwarding selected visual tokens) for visual revisiting, which is computationally expensive. A natural shortcut is reusing the historical visual KV cache, but this fails because cached visual keys are bound to their original positional context, causing distorted attention and autoregressive decoding collapse under new decoding contexts.
AI.method: PRCR (Position Rebinding Cache Reuse): stores raw visual KV cache together with their original spatial coordinates, then reassigns position-compatible coordinates to select entries and rebinds their keys before injecting the reconstructed cache into the active decoder cache.
AI.result: Across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks, PRCR achieves replay-level or better performance, improving average accuracy by 5% and reducing visual-revisiting computation by up to tens of thousands of times.
AI.conclusion: Effective cache reuse requires reconstructing visual evidence under positions compatible with the current decoding state rather than directly copying position-bound historical cache entries. PRCR successfully enables replay-free visual revisiting with significant efficiency gains.

title: Humans Disengage, Reasoning Models Persist: Separating Difficulty Registration from Deliberation Allocation
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) take longer on harder problems, just as humans do. This surface similarity hides an opposite pattern within items. When an LRM gets a problem wrong, it spends more tokens than when it gets the same problem right; humans do the reverse, spending less time on the trials they get wrong. We separate two levels of deliberation: how response time tracks difficulty across items (registration), and, with item identity held fixed, whether an agent spends more on its own failures or successes (allocation). On a public matched human-LRM corpus, humans and all five thinking LRMs reproduce the known cross-item alignment (registration) but diverge within items (allocation): every LRM shows a large wrong-vs-right effect (Cohen's d = 1.47-3.13 on H-ARC) while humans show the opposite sign. The comparison stays inside each agent's own scale; we never put seconds and tokens on one axis. The dissociation holds under item fixed effects, replicates across datasets, and is absent in a non-thinking baseline. We read the human pattern as engagement versus abandonment: people stay on items they expect to solve and give up on the rest. We read the LRM pattern as length driven by uncertainty: chains grow when the model is unsure, which is exactly when it tends to fail. Both policies produce the same cross-item correlation with difficulty, so they look aligned on the measure prior work has used; the divergence shows up only once item identity is fixed. Under resource-rational metareasoning, the split is between two stopping policies that share a difficulty signal but implement opposite control; trace length captures the signal and misses the control.

AI.tldr: Large reasoning models (LRMs) and humans both spend more resources on harder problems, but they diverge within the same item: LRMs spend more tokens when wrong vs. right, while humans spend less time when wrong vs. right. This reveals a dissociation between cross-item registration and within-item allocation, reflecting different stopping policies (uncertainty-driven vs. abandonment-driven).
AI.motivation: Prior work has observed that both humans and LRMs spend more time/tokens on harder problems, suggesting surface-level alignment. However, the authors question whether this similarity extends to within-item behavior, where controlling for item identity reveals fundamentally different resource allocation patterns between humans and LRMs.
AI.method: The authors analyze a public matched human-LRM corpus (H-ARC and other datasets), separating two levels of deliberation: cross-item registration (difficulty tracking) and within-item allocation (resource difference between right vs. wrong trials). They compute within-item effect sizes (Cohen's d) for LRMs and humans, control for item fixed effects, and replicate across datasets with a non-thinking baseline.
AI.result: All five thinking LRMs and humans show the same cross-item registration (more resources on harder items). However, within the same item, every LRM shows a large wrong-vs-right effect (Cohen's d = 1.47–3.13 on H-ARC), spending more tokens on failures; humans show the opposite sign (more time on successes). The dissociation holds under item fixed effects, replicates across datasets, and is absent in non-thinking baselines.
AI.conclusion: Human and LRM resource allocation policies look aligned on cross-item measures but diverge once item identity is fixed. The human pattern reflects engagement vs. abandonment; the LRM pattern reflects uncertainty-driven chain growth. Under resource-rational metareasoning, this reflects two different stopping policies sharing a difficulty signal but implementing opposite control—trace length captures the signal but misses the control.

title: Embedding Foundation Model Predictions in Discrete-Choice Models with Structural Guarantees
summary: Tabular foundation models achieve strong accuracy on choice prediction tasks, but their predictions often violate the economic logic those tasks require: raising a price can increase predicted demand, implied willingness-to-pay estimates are frequently negative or implausible, and unavailable alternatives receive nonzero probability. We propose a two-stage adapter that takes a foundation model's predicted choice probabilities as a precomputed feature and embeds them inside a multinomial logit's utility. In Stage 1, we fit the multinomial logit's structural coefficients by maximum likelihood with sign constraints; in Stage 2, we freeze those coefficients and fit a small neural correction operating on the foundation model's predictions. We prove that this composition exactly preserves the multinomial logit's marginal rate of substitution, so analytically computable value-of-time becomes a mathematical guarantee rather than an empirical accident. Across three datasets and two foundation models, the adapter gains 6.4 percentage points (pp) of test accuracy on average over the multinomial logit and up to 12.8 pp, maintains 100% cost monotonicity, and produces values of time within the published transportation-economics range on the transportation datasets. Performance degrades gracefully under foundation-model context restriction, retaining at least 6 pp of accuracy gain even at 10% of the original foundation-model context.

AI.tldr: A two-stage adapter embeds foundation model predictions inside a multinomial logit to enforce economic constraints (cost monotonicity, plausible value-of-time), gaining up to 12.8 pp accuracy while guaranteeing theoretically sound substitution patterns.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models achieve high accuracy on choice prediction but violate basic economic logic (e.g., price increases raising demand, negative willingness-to-pay, nonzero probability for unavailable alternatives). The authors aim to reconcile predictive accuracy with economic consistency.
AI.method: Two-stage adapter: Stage 1 fits multinomial logit structural coefficients via maximum likelihood with sign constraints; Stage 2 freezes those coefficients and fits a small neural correction on the foundation model's predictions. The composition preserves the logit's marginal rate of substitution analytically.
AI.result: Across 3 datasets and 2 foundation models: 6.4 pp average test accuracy gain over multinomial logit (up to 12.8 pp), 100% cost monotonicity, value-of-time within published transportation-economics range. Graceful degradation under context restriction (≥6 pp gain at 10% context).
AI.conclusion: The adapter successfully bridges the gap between foundation model accuracy and economic consistency, providing guaranteed structural properties while maintaining strong predictive performance.

title: Temporally Consistent Label Interpolation for Robust Surgical Multi-Task Learning under Challenging Conditions
summary: Effective multi-task learning for surgical scene understanding is fundamentally hindered by annotation granularity mismatch; temporal workflow tasks such as phase recognition, step recognition and anticipation benefit from dense frame-level supervision, whereas pixel-level spatial tasks including instrument segmentation and action recognition are only sparsely annotated on selected keyframes due to prohibitive labeling costs. This supervision imbalance undermines shared representation learning and limits joint optimization across heterogeneous surgical tasks. To address this, we propose Flow-guided Annotation for Robust Operating Scenes (FAROS), a flow-guided label interpolation framework, that combines zero-shot segmentation-based mask propagation with optical flow estimation to overcome the limitations of appearance-based propagation under challenging surgical conditions such as occlusion, smoke, and motion blur, generating temporally consistent dense pseudo labels from sparse keyframe annotations. The densified instrument masks and action labels are integrated into a unified Transformer-based multi-task framework that jointly learns surgical phase recognition, step recognition, anticipation, instrument segmentation, and action recognition, enabling balanced optimization between dense temporal supervision and sparse spatial supervision. The label interpolation quality of FAROS is first validated on the DAVIS 2017 benchmark under a sparse ground-truth protocol, confirming robust propagation beyond the surgical domain. Extensive experiments on GraSP, MISAW, and AutoLaparo benchmarks further demonstrate that FAROS significantly improves cross-task representation learning and enhances holistic surgical scene understanding performance across spatio-temporal tasks.

AI.tldr: FAROS is a flow-guided label interpolation framework that uses optical flow and zero-shot mask propagation to generate dense pseudo labels from sparse keyframe annotations, enabling balanced multi-task learning for surgical scene understanding across temporal (phase/step recognition, anticipation) and spatial (instrument segmentation, action recognition) tasks.
AI.motivation: Multi-task learning for surgical scene understanding suffers from annotation granularity mismatch: temporal tasks have dense frame-level supervision while spatial tasks have only sparse keyframe annotations due to high labeling costs, which undermines shared representation learning and joint optimization.
AI.method: FAROS combines zero-shot segmentation-based mask propagation with optical flow estimation to generate temporally consistent dense pseudo labels from sparse keyframe annotations, overcoming limitations of appearance-based propagation under challenging surgical conditions (occlusion, smoke, motion blur). These densified labels are integrated into a unified Transformer-based multi-task framework.
AI.result: FAROS achieves robust propagation validated on DAVIS 2017 under sparse ground-truth protocol. Extensive experiments on GraSP, MISAW, and AutoLaparo benchmarks show significant improvements in cross-task representation learning and holistic surgical scene understanding across spatio-temporal tasks.
AI.conclusion: Flow-guided label interpolation effectively resolves annotation granularity mismatch in multi-task surgical scene learning, enabling balanced optimization between dense temporal and sparse spatial supervision, with demonstrated generalization beyond the surgical domain.

title: NeuraDock Visual Cognitive Load Agent Tutorial: A Quality-Gated Open-Source EEG Workflow for Alpha Dynamics and Real-Time Applications
summary: This tutorial paper provides a step-by-step, reproducible walkthrough of NeuraDock Agent, an open-source EEG agent focused on Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive-load analysis. The goal is practical: a reader should be able to install the agent, run EEG preprocessing and quality control, generate Alpha dynamics figures, perform within-subject Rest/Task visual cognitive-load comparison, run the public mini-dataset analyses and compare them with the reference validation summary, start an online dashboard, call the real-time API from an external application, and use the LLM interpretation layer to explain quality risks. Existing EEG toolkits provide excellent offline analysis, but assembling a real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline often requires manually bridging acquisition, custom QC, Alpha feature extraction, and a web API; this tutorial closes that offline-to-online gap. The tutorial uses a quality-gated workflow: downstream Alpha and workload metrics are computed only after preprocessing and QC gating rather than directly from raw EEG. In the included mini-dataset validation, the agent processed 18 recordings, generated 10 within-subject comparisons, observed task-related posterior Alpha suppression in 7 of 10 contrasts, estimated initial evidence of within-subject repeatability, and benchmarked local online API latency. The tutorial is intended for researchers, developers, and applied teams who want a transparent path from EEG files to real-time visual cognitive-load prototypes.

AI.tldr: This tutorial paper presents NeuraDock Agent, an open-source EEG agent that bridges offline analysis and real-time deployment for Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive-load analysis. It provides a reproducible walkthrough covering installation, preprocessing, quality gating, Alpha feature extraction, within-subject Rest/Task comparisons, mini-dataset validation, dashboard deployment, real-time API calling, and LLM-based quality interpretation.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG toolkits offer excellent offline analysis, but assembling a real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline requires manually bridging acquisition, custom QC, Alpha feature extraction, and a web API. This tutorial closes the offline-to-online gap.
AI.method: A quality-gated workflow: downstream Alpha and workload metrics are computed only after preprocessing and QC gating rather than directly from raw EEG. The agent processes recordings, generates within-subject comparisons, and includes an online dashboard, real-time API, and LLM interpretation layer.
AI.result: In mini-dataset validation, the agent processed 18 recordings, generated 10 within-subject comparisons, observed task-related posterior Alpha suppression in 7 of 10 contrasts, estimated initial within-subject repeatability evidence, and benchmarked local online API latency.
AI.conclusion: NeuraDock Agent provides a transparent, reproducible path from EEG files to real-time visual cognitive-load prototypes, intended for researchers, developers, and applied teams.

title: Optimizing CUDA like a Human: Micro-Profiling Tools as Expert Surrogates for LLM-Based GPU Kernel Optimization
summary: We present KernelPro, a closed-loop multi-agent system that automatically generates, profiles, and iteratively optimizes GPU kernel code by integrating large language model (LLM) code generation with hardware profiler feedback and pluggable bottleneck detection tools. KernelPro introduces four contributions: (1) a semantic feedback operator that encodes expert heuristics as pluggable micro-profiling tools, transforming raw hardware metrics into actionable natural language guidance; (2) a two-stage tool invocation architecture where roofline-based bottleneck classification filters which specialized analysis tools execute, combining kernel-level (ncu), instruction-level (SASS), and system-level (nsys) profiling; (3) a domain-adapted MCTS with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and search memory for cross-iteration learning; and (4) direct CuTe source-level code generation via autonomous code search over the CUTLASS/CuTe codebase. On KernelBench, KernelPro achieves geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on Levels 1/2/3, establishing state-of-the-art performance across all difficulty levels. On VeOmni's expert-optimized MoE training kernels, KernelPro achieves 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton by generating a from-scratch raw-CUDA+CuTe Hopper WGMMA kernel. Ablation studies demonstrate that each design component independently and significantly improves optimization quality: micro-profiling tools (p < 0.0001 vs raw metrics), MCTS search (26% higher geometric mean vs greedy, p = 0.004), and proactive tool orchestration (23% improvement, p = 0.035). Finally, KernelPro is the first CUDA kernel coding agent to optimize energy efficiency beyond the speed-only focus of prior systems, demonstrating an 11.6% measured energy reduction at matched speed.

AI.tldr: KernelPro is a closed-loop multi-agent system that uses LLMs, hardware profiling, and iterative optimization to automatically generate GPU kernel code, achieving state-of-the-art speedups (2.42x-5.30x on KernelBench) and being the first CUDA coding agent to also optimize energy efficiency (11.6% reduction).
AI.motivation: Existing GPU kernel optimization lacks integration between LLM code generation and hardware-level profiling feedback, resulting in suboptimal performance and no support for energy efficiency optimization.
AI.method: KernelPro combines (1) a semantic feedback operator encoding expert heuristics as pluggable micro-profiling tools, (2) a two-stage tool invocation architecture with roofline-based bottleneck classification using ncu, SASS, and nsys profiling, (3) a domain-adapted MCTS with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and search memory, and (4) direct CuTe source-level code generation via autonomous code search over CUTLASS/CuTe.
AI.result: Geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on KernelBench Levels 1/2/3; 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton on MoE training kernels; 11.6% energy reduction at matched speed. All components statistically significant in ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: KernelPro establishes that integrating LLM code generation with structured hardware profiling feedback and advanced search (MCTS) yields significant GPU kernel optimizations, and is the first system to jointly optimize for both speed and energy efficiency in automated CUDA kernel generation.

title: FracEvent: Event-Camera Simulation via Fractional-Relaxation Pixel Dynamics
summary: Event cameras asynchronously report brightness changes with microsecond-level temporal resolution, but real event data remain difficult to collect at scale because specialized sensors, careful synchronization, and task-specific annotations are required. Event-camera simulation is therefore important to event-based vision tasks. Most practical simulators build on contrast-threshold event generation, some with additional filtering, stochastic noise, or hand-tuned sensor parameters. While effective, such formulations often simplify the temporal structure produced by the lifecycle of each pixel, which can distort event timing and weaken downstream transfer. We introduce FracEvent, an event simulator that models this pixel-level lifecycle with fractional-relaxation voltage dynamics. Given a log-intensity trajectory, FracEvent drives a compact stack of relaxation modes, combines their responses into a voltage state, emits ON/OFF events by localizing threshold crossings on the continuous voltage trajectory, and updates the reference while retaining the underlying memory modes. This retained state links residual voltage response to later event timing. We evaluate FracEvent through event-stream comparison and downstream transfer on image reconstruction and optical flow estimation. Across multiple datasets, FracEvent improves the temporal structure of generated events and achieves stronger downstream-transfer results than competing simulator baselines, showing its practical value for event-camera simulation.

AI.tldr: FracEvent is a new event-camera simulator that models pixel-level voltage dynamics using fractional-relaxation processes, producing more temporally accurate events and achieving better downstream transfer on image reconstruction and optical flow compared to existing simulators.
AI.motivation: Real event data are difficult to collect at scale due to specialized hardware, synchronization needs, and annotations. Existing simulators simplify the temporal structure of pixel-level voltage dynamics, distorting event timing and weakening downstream transfer performance.
AI.method: FracEvent models pixel-level lifecycle with fractional-relaxation voltage dynamics. Given a log-intensity trajectory, it drives a compact stack of relaxation modes, combines responses into a voltage state, emits ON/OFF events by localizing threshold crossings on the continuous voltage trajectory, and updates the reference while retaining underlying memory modes.
AI.result: FracEvent improves temporal structure of generated events and achieves stronger downstream-transfer results on image reconstruction and optical flow estimation compared to competing simulator baselines across multiple datasets.
AI.conclusion: Modeling the retained voltage dynamics via fractional relaxation is practically valuable for event-camera simulation, yielding more temporally accurate events and better downstream task performance.

title: Boundary-Aware Context Grounding for A Low-Channel EEG Agent
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) can make scientific software easier to use. However, a general model does not automatically know which measurements a particular sensor can support, which algorithms are implemented in the current software, or which conclusions are justified by a computed result. These distinctions are especially important for low-channel electroencephalography (EEG), where sparse spatial coverage and variable signal quality make plausible but unsupported interpretations easy to produce. We present NeuraDock Agent, an open-source architecture that separates a deterministic local EEG engine from a hardware-aware language layer. The numerical engine parses recordings, performs quality control, executes reviewed spectral workflows, and writes machine-readable artifacts. The LLM receives only a compact, allowlisted summary and a versioned context pack. The context describes the seven-channel hardware, reviewed workflows, result fields, implementation boundaries, scientific limits, and reference cases. Raw EEG and dense per-sample arrays remain local
  We evaluate the system at three levels. First, 12 recordings produced identical structured results over ten numerical repetitions, and a complete Rest/Task run produced identical result, report, and figure hashes over three repetitions. Second, request-capture and failure-injection experiments confirmed the tested data boundary and preservation of local artifacts under HTTP, malformed-output, and connection failures. Third, a boundary-awareness benchmark tested 36 ordinary and adversarial questions under four context ablations and two LLMs, yielding 288 outputs.These results support hardware- and implementation-aware grounding as a practical mechanism for calibrating what an EEG agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses; they do not establish clinical validity or a validated absolute cognitive-load index.

AI.tldr: NeuraDock Agent is an open-source architecture that combines a deterministic local EEG engine with an LLM layer, enabling hardware-aware, implementation-aware scientific analysis for low-channel EEG while preventing unsupported interpretations through strict data boundaries and allowlisted summaries.
AI.motivation: LLMs lack inherent knowledge of sensor-specific measurements, software implementations, and scientifically justified conclusions for low-channel EEG, where sparse spatial coverage and variable signal quality make unsupported interpretations easy to produce.
AI.method: The system separates a deterministic local numerical EEG engine (parsing, quality control, spectral workflows, machine-readable artifacts) from an LLM layer that receives only a compact allowlisted summary and versioned context pack describing hardware, workflows, result fields, boundaries, limits, and reference cases. Raw EEG and dense arrays stay local.
AI.result: 1) Numerical reproducibility: 12 recordings identical over 10 repetitions; complete Rest/Task run identical over 3 repetitions. 2) Robustness: boundary and artifact preservation confirmed under HTTP, malformed-output, and connection failures. 3) Boundary-awareness benchmark: 288 outputs from 36 questions × 4 context ablations × 2 LLMs evaluated.
AI.conclusion: Hardware- and implementation-aware grounding is a practical mechanism for calibrating what an EEG agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses. The paper does not establish clinical validity or a validated absolute cognitive-load index.

title: Finding the Time to Think: Learning Planning Budgets in Real-Time RL
summary: Deliberating takes time. In real-time settings, that time is not free. Standard reinforcement learning (RL) sidesteps this as the environment waits indefinitely for the agent's decision. Instead, we study real-time RL environments where the environment progresses while waiting for the agent's action. Building on prior real-time formalizations, we introduce variable-delay real-time RL, where the agent chooses how long to deliberate at each decision point since the environment progresses. For the planning agents we use, the right delay is state-dependent, and naively planning how long to plan can paralyze the agent. We instead approach this setting by training a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets. Across real-time Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, and Speed Go, our gating policy outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines, and transfers to a real-time setup where the environment and agent run on two different GPUs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces variable-delay real-time RL where the environment progresses during agent deliberation, and proposes training a lightweight gating policy to select state-dependent planning budgets, outperforming fixed-budget and heuristic baselines across multiple real-time games and transferring to a heterogeneous GPU setup.
AI.motivation: Standard RL assumes the environment waits indefinitely for agent decisions, which is unrealistic for real-time settings. The paper aims to address the challenge of deliberation cost in time-sensitive environments where time spent planning has real consequences.
AI.method: The authors formalize variable-delay real-time RL where the agent chooses deliberation time at each decision point. They train a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets, avoiding the paralysis that comes from planning how long to plan.
AI.result: The gating policy outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines across real-time Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, and Speed Go, and transfers to a setup where the environment and agent run on separate GPUs.
AI.conclusion: Variable-delay planning with a learned gating mechanism is effective for real-time RL environments where deliberation time is costly, and the approach generalizes to heterogeneous computing setups.

title: LayersReg: A Layer-by-Layer Progressive Regressor for Reliable Intraoperative 3D/2D Registration
summary: 3D/2D registration serves as a cornerstone technique in surgical navigation. Traditional iterative optimization algorithms suffer from low efficiency and high failure rates in intraoperative settings. Deep learning-based methods reformulate registration from iterative optimization to a regression problem that maps image appearance features to spatial pose, typically achieving improved real-time performance and accuracy. However, such learnable methods are confined to memory-driven retrieval of specific pose features rather than understanding the task of image alignment itself, which limits their generalization in complex scenarios. We propose LayersReg, a pioneering regression paradigm that endows the model with 3D anatomical awareness and searches for the correct pose in a progressive, layer-by-layer manner. Inspired by the iterative pose-searching optimization criterion of classical registration, LayersReg searches for correlations between the moving and fixed images in feature space, capturing the trend of pixel flow and thereby converging iteratively toward the correct spatial pose transformation. We further design a coupling of node-wise regression with the progressive registration framework to enhance the model's perception of spatial pose changes. Experimental results demonstrate that under large offsets and multimodality conditions, LayersReg achieves high accuracy on both X-ray/CT registration (0.68°, 1.41 mm) and slice localization (0.73°, 1.55 mm) tasks, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods while meeting the intraoperative demands for precision and real-time capability.

AI.tldr: LayersReg is a deep learning-based 3D/2D registration method that mimics classical iterative optimization by progressively searching for pose correlations in feature space, achieving superior accuracy (0.68°, 1.41 mm on X-ray/CT) and real-time performance for surgical navigation.
AI.motivation: Traditional iterative optimization methods for 3D/2D registration suffer from low efficiency and high failure rates in intraoperative settings. While deep learning methods improve speed and accuracy, they are limited to memory-driven pose feature retrieval rather than truly understanding image alignment, hindering generalization in complex scenarios.
AI.method: LayersReg proposes a progressive, layer-by-layer regression paradigm that: (1) searches for correlations between moving and fixed images in feature space, capturing pixel flow trends, and (2) couples node-wise regression with the progressive framework to enhance spatial pose change perception, iteratively converging toward the correct transformation.
AI.result: Under large offsets and multimodality conditions, LayersReg achieves 0.68° and 1.41 mm on X-ray/CT registration and 0.73° and 1.55 mm on slice localization, outperforming state-of-the-art methods while meeting intraoperative precision and real-time demands.
AI.conclusion: LayersReg bridges the gap between classical iterative optimization and deep regression by endowing the model with 3D anatomical awareness through progressive pose searching, demonstrating that mimicking the iterative criterion of traditional registration in feature space yields superior generalization and accuracy for surgical navigation.

title: Radical AI Interpretability
comment: Draft of manuscript to appear as Cambridge Element in the Philosophy of Artificial Intelligence
summary: We develop a framework for interpreting AI systems as agents, drawing on the philosophical tradition of radical interpretation and the tools of mechanistic interpretability. The core question is: given the computational facts about a system, how do we solve for its beliefs, desires, and meanings? This matters increasingly for safety. We want to be able to trust the systems we deploy, whether by understanding their goals or, more modestly, by reliably detecting deception. Interpretability researchers are building tools to read beliefs and desires off a model's internals, but there is no settled account of when such a tool has succeeded. This book supplies one. We propose criteria on both representationalist and interpretationist approaches, and tie each to tests current interpretability methods can carry out. A central lesson is that these attributions cannot be made piecemeal. Beliefs, desires, and the propositional structure they presuppose are jointly constrained, and a method that fixes one while measuring the others inherits whatever distortions that introduces. This holism becomes pressing for AI systems, which may not share the interpreter's concepts. However, it also provides leverage: a system's attitudes constrain its propositional structure, that structure constrains which attitudes can be attributed, and mechanistic interpretability can help us measure both.

AI.tldr: This book develops a framework for interpreting AI systems as agents by combining radical interpretation philosophy with mechanistic interpretability, addressing how to attribute beliefs, desires, and meanings to AI systems. It proposes criteria for representationalist and interpretationist approaches, emphasizes the holistic nature of such attributions, and shows how mechanistic interpretability can help jointly constrain propositional structure and attitudes.
AI.motivation: AI safety requires understanding what AI systems believe and desire, and detecting deception. Current interpretability tools can read beliefs/desires from model internals, but there is no settled account of when such attribution has succeeded. This gap motivates the need for a principled framework.
AI.method: The book applies philosophical radical interpretation (drawing on Quine, Davidson) to AI systems, proposes criteria for both representationalist and interpretationist approaches, ties these to current mechanistic interpretability methods, and argues for a holistic constraint-based approach where beliefs, desires, and propositional structure are jointly determined.
AI.result: The framework establishes that attributions cannot be made piecemeal; holism is essential. A system's attitudes constrain its propositional structure and vice versa, providing leverage for measurements. Methods that fix one attribute while measuring others inherit distortions. Mechanistic interpretability can help measure both attitudes and propositional structure.
AI.conclusion: A principled account for interpreting AI agents is possible by combining radical interpretation with mechanistic interpretability, but it requires holistic, jointly-constrained attributions—especially critical for AI systems that may not share human concepts.

title: A Causal Foundation Model for Structure and Outcome Prediction
comment: 20 pages, 7 figures, 17 tables, 43rd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data
summary: We introduce TabPFN-CFM, a causal foundation model that can handle multiple causal problems. TabPFN-CFM predicts both causal structure and outcomes from observational data, supports queries on all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy and uses known graph structure when available to improve predictions. TabPFN-CFM is trained on synthetic datasets, and generalises to real datasets, demonstrating improved performance over both structural and outcome prediction baselines.

AI.tldr: TabPFN-CFM is a causal foundation model trained on synthetic data that predicts causal structure and outcomes from observational data, supports all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy, and outperforms baselines on real datasets.
AI.motivation: To develop a single causal foundation model that can handle multiple causal problems including structure learning and outcome prediction across all levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy, without needing separate models for different tasks.
AI.method: TabPFN-CFM is a causal foundation model trained on synthetic datasets. It uses a transformer-based prior-data fitted network (PFN) architecture that processes observational data and can optionally leverage known graph structures to improve predictions.
AI.result: The model generalizes from synthetic training data to real datasets, demonstrating improved performance over both structural prediction baselines and outcome prediction baselines across all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy.
AI.conclusion: TabPFN-CFM establishes that a single causal foundation model can effectively address multiple causal inference tasks, leveraging known structure when available, and generalizing from synthetic to real-world data.

title: Disco-LoRA: Disentangled Composition of Content, Style, and Motion for Multi-concept Video Customization
summary: Video customization based on Text-to-Video (T2V) models aims to learn specific features from reference data to generate controllable videos. While significant strides have been made in image stylization and video motion customization, simultaneously controlling multiple concepts, such as content, style, and motion, remains a major challenge. In this work, we systematically define the task of multi-concept video customization, which requires the joint control of content, style, and motion. To facilitate research in this area, we construct a comprehensive benchmark and propose Disco-LoRA, a unified framework designed to tackle this problem by disentangling and flexibly recombining different concepts in two stages: (1) We decompose the objective into two sub-tasks: Content-Style and Content-Motion. Each sub-task is addressed using our Iterative Dual-LoRA Disentanglement Framework, which effectively disentangles distinct concepts within the data. (2) We identify layer-wise weight trends as crucial for LoRA identity, while weight magnitudes dictate composability. To harmonize these scales, we propose a Z-score-based statistical regularization that aligns weight distributions, preserving layer-wise trends while minimizing interference between different LoRAs. Extensive experiments show that Disco-LoRA excels in multi-concept video customization, effectively preserving appearance, style, and motion for controllable text-to-video generation.

AI.tldr: Disco-LoRA is a unified framework for multi-concept video customization that disentangles content, style, and motion using a two-stage approach: iterative dual-LoRA disentanglement for sub-tasks and Z-score-based regularization for harmonizing LoRA weights, enabling controllable text-to-video generation.
AI.motivation: Simultaneously controlling multiple concepts (content, style, and motion) in video customization remains a major challenge despite progress in image stylization and motion customization. The paper systematically defines the multi-concept video customization task and constructs a benchmark to address this gap.
AI.method: Disco-LoRA decomposes the problem into two sub-tasks (Content-Style and Content-Motion), each solved via an Iterative Dual-LoRA Disentanglement Framework. It then uses Z-score-based statistical regularization to align weight distributions across different LoRAs, preserving layer-wise identity while minimizing interference.
AI.result: Disco-LoRA excels in multi-concept video customization, effectively preserving appearance, style, and motion for controllable text-to-video generation, as demonstrated through extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully enables joint control of content, style, and motion in video generation by disentangling concepts and harmonizing LoRA weight scales via statistical regularization.

title: PMDformer: Patch-Mean Decoupling Information Transformer for Long-term Forecasting
summary: Long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) plays a crucial role in fields such as energy management, finance, and traffic prediction. Transformer-based models have adopted patch-based strategies to capture long-range dependencies, but accurately modeling shape similarities across patches and variables remains challenging due to scale differences. To address this, we introduce patch-mean decoupling (PMD), which separates the trend and residual shape information by subtracting the mean of each patch, preserving the original structure and ensuring that the attention mechanism captures true shape similarities. Futhermore, to more effectively model long-range dependencies and capture cross-variable relationships, we propose Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) and Proximal Variable Attention (PVA). The former module reintegrates the decoupled trend from PMD while calculating attention output. And the latter focuses cross-variable attention on the most relevant, recent time segments to avoid overfitting on outdated correlations. Combining these components, we propose PMDformer, a model designed to effectively capture shape similarity in long-term forecasting scenarios. Extensive experiments indicate that PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in stability and accuracy across multiple LTSF benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/aohu1105/PMDformer.

AI.tldr: PMDformer introduces patch-mean decoupling (PMD), Trend Restoration Attention (TRA), and Proximal Variable Attention (PVA) to improve shape similarity modeling and cross-variable dependency capture in long-term time series forecasting, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based LTSF models using patch-based strategies struggle to accurately model shape similarities across patches and variables due to scale differences, motivating the need for decoupling trend and residual shape information.
AI.method: PMD (patch-mean decoupling) separates trend and residual shape by subtracting each patch's mean; TRA reintegrates the decoupled trend during attention computation; PVA focuses cross-variable attention on the most relevant recent time segments to avoid overfitting.
AI.result: PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in both stability and accuracy across multiple LTSF benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Patch-mean decoupling and the proposed attention mechanisms (TRA and PVA) effectively capture shape similarity and cross-variable relationships, advancing long-term forecasting performance.

title: Epiphany-Aware KV Cache Eviction Without the Attention Matrix
summary: As reasoning models emit chains of thought tens of thousands of tokens long, KV cache increasingly becomes a deployment bottleneck. Existing cache eviction methods rank tokens by attention weight, which is a noisy importance proxy in long reasoning traces, and prohibits the use of fused kernels in production inference by forcing the model to materialize the attention matrix. In this work, we instead score tokens with a metric we term the epiphany score: the change in the model's internal representation, read directly from the forward pass with no attention matrix and negligible extra state. Our resulting cache eviction method, EpiKV, requires no training, classifier, or custom kernel, and can be used directly in FlashAttention inference stacks unchanged -- scaling to a 16x longer feasible context than attention-based scoring. upper-mid layers negatively) and remove a positional trend with a causal rolling z-score. At a 4096-token cache EpiKV reaches 72% on MATH-500, matching the strongest attention-based baseline (ThinKV 71%, H2O 67%); a lag-normalized KV variant reaches 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens against the best of them (33%), at up to 2.8x the speed.

AI.tldr: EpiKV uses an epiphany score (change in model's internal representation from forward pass) for cache eviction, avoiding attention matrix materialization and enabling direct use with FlashAttention. It achieves up to 16x longer feasible context than attention-based scoring, matching or outperforming baselines (72% on MATH-500, 37% on AIME-2024) at up to 2.8x speedup.
AI.motivation: KV cache becomes a deployment bottleneck for reasoning models emitting long chains of thought (tens of thousands of tokens). Existing attention-weight-based eviction methods are noisy for long reasoning traces and prohibit fused kernels (e.g., FlashAttention) by forcing attention matrix materialization.
AI.method: EpiKV scores tokens using the epiphany score: the change in the model's internal representation read directly from the forward pass, requiring no attention matrix and negligible extra state. It applies causal rolling z-score normalization to remove positional trends. No training, classifier, or custom kernel needed; compatible with FlashAttention unchanged.
AI.result: At 4096-token cache: 72% on MATH-500 (vs ThinKV 71%, H2O 67%). Lag-normalized KV variant: 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens (vs best baseline 33%). Up to 2.8x speedup. Scales to 16x longer feasible context than attention-based scoring.
AI.conclusion: EpiKV provides a simple, training-free, kernel-compatible KV cache eviction method that outperforms attention-based approaches in both accuracy and speed for long-context reasoning traces.

title: DeCoFlow: Structural Decomposition of Normalizing Flows for Continual Anomaly Detection
summary: In industrial environments, new product categories arrive sequentially, requiring continual anomaly detection without access to past data. Normalizing Flows (NFs) provide exact density estimation but suffer from catastrophic forgetting as parameter updates across tasks distort the density manifold. While parameter isolation can prevent interference, it must preserve the strict invertibility and Jacobian validity of NFs. To satisfy these requirements, we exploit the inherent property that affine coupling layers maintain transformation validity regardless of subnet parameterization. Based on this, we propose DeCoFlow, which decomposes subnets into a frozen universal base and task-specific low-rank adapters to isolate updates. We further introduce Task-Specific Alignment, Auxiliary Coupling Layers, and Tail-Aware Loss to compensate for frozen-base rigidity. DeCoFlow achieves state-of-the-art image-level AUROCs of 98.40% on MVTec-AD and 93.00% on VisA, while maintaining parameter-level zero forgetting (0.00% FM under correct routing) with only 2.27M parameters per task.

AI.tldr: DeCoFlow uses Normalizing Flows with decomposed subnets (frozen base + low-rank adapters) to achieve continual anomaly detection without forgetting, reaching SOTA AUROCs of 98.40% (MVTec-AD) and 93.00% (VisA) with zero forgetting and only 2.27M parameters per task.
AI.motivation: New product categories arrive sequentially in industrial anomaly detection, requiring continual learning. Normalizing Flows provide exact density estimation but suffer catastrophic forgetting due to parameter updates distorting the density manifold, and existing parameter isolation methods struggle to preserve invertibility and Jacobian validity of NFs.
AI.method: DeCoFlow decomposes subnets into a frozen universal base and task-specific low-rank adapters to isolate updates, exploiting affine coupling layers' property of maintaining transformation validity regardless of subnet parameterization. It also introduces Task-Specific Alignment, Auxiliary Coupling Layers, and Tail-Aware Loss to address frozen-base rigidity.
AI.result: Achieves 98.40% AUROC on MVTec-AD and 93.00% on VisA, with zero forgetting (0.00% FM under correct routing) and 2.27M parameters per task.
AI.conclusion: DeCoFlow effectively enables continual anomaly detection in Normalizing Flows without catastrophic forgetting, achieving state-of-the-art performance with minimal parameter overhead.

title: Explainable Ensemble-Based Machine Learning Models for Detecting the Presence of Cirrhosis in Hepatitis C Patients
summary: Hepatitis C is a liver infection caused by a virus, which results in mild to severe inflammation of the liver. Over many years, hepatitis C gradually damages the liver, often leading to permanent scarring, known as cirrhosis. Patients sometimes have moderate or no symptoms of liver illness for decades before developing cirrhosis. Cirrhosis typically worsens to the point of liver failure. Patients with cirrhosis may also experience brain and nerve system damage, as well as gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Treatment for cirrhosis focuses on preventing further progression of the disease. Detecting cirrhosis earlier is therefore crucial for avoiding complications. Machine learning (ML) has been shown to be effective at providing precise and accurate information for use in diagnosing several diseases. Despite this, no studies have so far used ML to detect cirrhosis in patients with hepatitis C. This study obtained a dataset consisting of 28 attributes of 2038 Egyptian patients from the ML Repository of the University of California at Irvine. Four ML algorithms were trained on the dataset to diagnose cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients: a Random Forest, a Gradient Boosting Machine, an Extreme Gradient Boosting, and an Extra Trees model. The Extra Trees model outperformed the other models achieving an accuracy of 96.92%, a recall of 94.00%, a precision of 99.81%, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 96% using only 16 of the 28 features.

AI.tldr: This study applies four ML models (Random Forest, GBM, XGBoost, Extra Trees) to detect cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients using 28 attributes from 2,038 Egyptian patients. Extra Trees achieved the best performance: 96.92% accuracy, 94% recall, 99.81% precision, and 96% AUC-ROC using only 16 features.
AI.motivation: Cirrhosis from hepatitis C often goes undetected until advanced stages leading to liver failure, brain damage, and hemorrhage. Earlier detection is crucial for preventing complications, yet no prior ML study has focused on detecting cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients.
AI.method: A dataset of 28 attributes from 2,038 Egyptian hepatitis C patients (from UCI ML Repository) was used. Four ML algorithms—Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machine, Extreme Gradient Boosting, and Extra Trees—were trained to diagnose cirrhosis. Feature reduction was applied, and Extra Trees achieved top performance using 16 features.
AI.result: Extra Trees outperformed other models with 96.92% accuracy, 94.00% recall, 99.81% precision, and 96% AUC-ROC using only 16 of the 28 features.
AI.conclusion: ML, particularly Extra Trees, is effective for early cirrhosis detection in hepatitis C patients, achieving high diagnostic accuracy with a reduced feature set.

title: When Does Quality-Aware Multimodal Fusion Matter? A Leakage-Safe Diagnostic for Decision-Level Dependence
comment: Accepted to INTERSPEECH 2026. 5 pages, 1 figure, 5 tables
summary: Many multimodal systems estimate the reliability of each modality and weight their contributions to the final prediction. However, it remains unclear whether these scores influence model decisions or merely correlate with performance. We propose a simple diagnostic to test whether reliability information is used during inference. After training, the model and inputs are fixed while reliability scores are permuted across test examples. If predictions depend on these scores, performance should degrade. Experiments on StressID for stress recognition and CMU-MOSEI for sentiment analysis show that permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged despite substantial potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. In positive controls where reliability signals identify the correct modality, the same frozen fusion rules yield significant improvements, indicating that reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness.

AI.tldr: Multimodal systems using reliability scores for modality weighting may not actually leverage these scores in decision-making; permuting reliability scores across test examples does not degrade performance, indicating they are merely correlated with performance rather than causally influencing predictions.
AI.motivation: To determine whether reliability scores in multimodal fusion systems actually influence model decisions or merely correlate with performance, as prior work assumes but does not verify the causal role of these scores.
AI.method: A diagnostic test where after training, reliability scores are permuted across test examples while the model and inputs remain fixed; if predictions depend on these scores, performance should degrade. Applied to StressID and CMU-MOSEI datasets.
AI.result: Permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged, despite substantial potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. Only when reliability signals reliably predict unimodal correctness (positive controls) do frozen fusion rules yield significant improvements.
AI.conclusion: Reliability scores in existing multimodal systems may not causally influence decisions; they are correlated with but not used for inference. The proposed diagnostic reveals when reliability signals are truly actionable versus merely descriptive.

title: PhysEditWorld: A Large-Scale Dataset Toward Physics-Editable World Models
summary: Recent game world models can synthesize visually plausible, action-conditioned rollouts. However, their interaction behaviors often remain limited to exploratory or wandering trajectories, and physical dynamics are typically learned as implicit correlations from data rather than as controllable variables. This limitation hinders their applicability to authored game environments, where physical rules are deliberately designed and require explicit manipulation. We introduce PhysEditWorld, a multimodal dataset with physical parameters, with a primary focus on gravity in this initial version. At its core, PhysEditWorld is built upon a replay paradigm implemented with a UE5 replay-and-rendering pipeline. Each scenario records a normalized action trace and replays the same initial state, character controller, action sequence, and camera policy under multiple gravity configurations, enabling controlled and attributable physical variation. PhysEditWorld contains 12 cinematic UE5 scenes, over 100 hours of gameplay interactions, and more than 60 million rendered rollout frames. Each sample provides synchronized multimodal signals, including RGB, depth, normals, audio, action traces, camera trajectory, engine states, semantic annotations, and explicit gravity labels. We further conduct initial utility studies on both generative video models and world understanding models, demonstrating that PhysEditWorld enables improved gravity-faithful dynamics modeling, enhances consistency under physical edits, and provides a scalable foundation for controllable world modeling research.

AI.tldr: PhysEditWorld is a multimodal dataset of over 60 million frames from 12 UE5 scenes with explicit gravity labels and synchronized signals (RGB, depth, normals, audio, actions, etc.), built via a replay paradigm that varies gravity while keeping other factors fixed, enabling controllable physical dynamics modeling for game world models.
AI.motivation: Existing game world models fail to support authored environments because physical dynamics are learned as implicit correlations rather than explicit controllable variables, limiting their applicability to game design where physical rules like gravity must be deliberately manipulated.
AI.method: A UE5 replay-and-rendering pipeline that records normalized action traces and replays identical initial states, character controllers, action sequences, and camera policies under multiple gravity configurations, producing synchronized multimodal outputs with explicit physical parameter labels.
AI.result: 12 cinematic UE5 scenes, 100+ hours of gameplay, 60M+ rollout frames; enables improved gravity-faithful dynamics modeling, enhanced consistency under physical edits in generative video and world understanding models.
AI.conclusion: PhysEditWorld provides a scalable foundation for controllable world modeling research by decoupling physical parameters from other game dynamics through controlled replay.

title: EvoOptiGraph: Weakness-Driven Coevolution via Graph-Based Structural Generation for Optimization Modeling
summary: Automating optimization modeling from natural language with large language models (LLMs) faces two key challenges. First, training corpora lack structural diversity. Second, data generation pipelines remain static and decoupled from model learning. To address these challenges, we propose EvoOptiGraph, a novel framework where data and model co-evolve, driven by model weaknesses. EvoOptiGraph represents each mixed-integer linear program (MILP) as an attributed bipartite graph and applies validity-preserving evolutionary operators to generate structurally diverse instances. The evolved graphs are converted into solver code and natural language via deterministic compilation and verified back-translation. Training proceeds in two stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on an initial dataset, followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where graph-derived weakness signals guide the generation of new instances targeting the model's failures. This forms a closed loop that continuously updates the training distribution. Empirical results on six public datasets show that EvoOptiGraph significantly outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines in accuracy, executability, and generalization. These results demonstrate that targeted data-model coevolution is an effective strategy for improving LLMs on optimization modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: EvoOptiGraph is a framework for automated optimization modeling from natural language that co-evolves data and model via evolutionary operators on MILP graphs and reinforcement learning, significantly outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to automating optimization modeling with LLMs suffer from structurally limited training corpora and static, decoupled data generation pipelines that do not adapt to model weaknesses.
AI.method: EvoOptiGraph represents MILPs as attributed bipartite graphs, applies validity-preserving evolutionary operators to generate diverse instances, converts graphs to solver code and natural language, and trains via SFT followed by RLVR where graph-derived weakness signals guide targeted instance generation in a closed loop.
AI.result: On six public datasets, EvoOptiGraph significantly outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines in accuracy, executability, and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Targeted data-model co-evolution is an effective strategy for improving LLM performance on optimization modeling tasks.

title: Localizing RL-Induced Tool Use to a Single Crosscoder Feature
comment: Accepted as a spotlight at the ICML 2026 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
summary: Fine-tuning through RL reshapes the internal representations of language models to enable agentic behaviors such as tool use, yet the mechanistic basis of these changes remains poorly understood. While RL substantially improves structured tool-call generation, it is unclear which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features can be leveraged for retraining-free behavioral control. In this work, we show that $\textit{Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC)}$ isolate a compact set of RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling capability in $\texttt{Qwen2.5-3B}$. Across a $48$-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep, encode-decode reconstruction improves the RL model's tool correctness by $+31.1 \pm {9.7}$ pp and passively transfers tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by $+6.8 \pm 5.0$ pp which we call a $\textit{capability spillover}$. Our findings show that DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) to isolate RL-specific features that enable tool-calling in LLMs, showing that a compact feature set can be decoded to improve tool correctness by +31.1 pp and transfer capability to frozen base models (+6.8 pp), enabling retraining-free behavioral control.
AI.motivation: RL fine-tuning reshapes internal representations to enable agentic behaviors like tool use, but the mechanistic basis of these changes is poorly understood—specifically which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features can be leveraged for retraining-free behavioral control.
AI.method: The authors propose Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) to isolate a compact set of RL-specific features from Qwen2.5-3B. They conduct a 48-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep to analyze encode-decode reconstruction effects on tool-calling performance.
AI.result: DFC-based encode-decode reconstruction improves the RL model's tool correctness by +31.1 ± 9.7 percentage points and passively transfers tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by +6.8 ± 5.0 pp (capability spillover).
AI.conclusion: DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs without retraining.

title: Intracranial Aneurysm Classification and Segmentation via Tri-Axial ROI and Multi-Task Learning
summary: Intracranial aneurysms are often asymptomatic until rupture, which carries high mortality. Rupture risk assessment and treatment planning depend on both aneurysm morphology and anatomical location, yet existing automated methods remain limited to binary detection without fine-grained anatomical classification or multi-class segmentation. We present a multi-task framework that simultaneously performs multi-label classification, multi-class aneurysm segmentation, and multi-class vessel segmentation across 13 anatomical locations and four imaging modalities (CTA, MRA, T2, T1-post). Our two-stage approach combines a fast 2D tri-axial Region of Interest (ROI) extraction method with a 3D multi-task nnU-Net backbone. A dual-decoder design mitigates the extreme volume imbalance between aneurysm and vessel classes, while cross-attention pooling and modality-specific auxiliary heads improve feature learning across heterogeneous inputs. Our two-fold ensemble achieved 2nd place in the RSNA 2025 Intracranial Aneurysm Detection challenge. Code, model weights, and a 3D Slicer plugin are publicly available.

AI.tldr: A multi-task framework for simultaneous classification, segmentation of intracranial aneurysms and vessels across 13 anatomical locations and 4 imaging modalities, achieving 2nd place in the RSNA 2025 challenge.
AI.motivation: Existing automated methods are limited to binary detection of intracranial aneurysms without fine-grained anatomical classification or multi-class segmentation, which are needed for rupture risk assessment and treatment planning.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) fast 2D tri-axial ROI extraction, (2) 3D multi-task nnU-Net backbone with dual-decoder design (to handle aneurysm-vessel volume imbalance), cross-attention pooling, and modality-specific auxiliary heads. Two-fold ensemble.
AI.result: 2nd place in the RSNA 2025 Intracranial Aneurysm Detection challenge.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-task framework effectively addresses fine-grained anatomical classification and multi-class segmentation across multiple modalities, with public release of code, model weights, and a 3D Slicer plugin.

title: A Multi-Level Validation and Traceability Framework for AI-Generated Telescope Scheduling Decisions
summary: With the gradual introduction of AI into telescope scheduling, AI-based decision-making has shown advantages in handling complex multi-constraint problems. However, its outputs often suffer from inconsistent data references, reasoning errors, and non-executable decisions, limiting applicability in high-reliability observational tasks. In this work, we propose a multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework that performs systematic reliability verification of AI-generated decisions prior to execution, and enables explicit representation of the reasoning process to support traceable decision-making. The framework integrates data reference validation, logical consistency checks, and observational and instrumental constraint verification to filter and correct invalid decisions. It also introduces atomic reasoning units and their dependency relationships, representing scheduling decisions as a sequence of interconnected reasoning steps that support error localization and post hoc analysis. Experiments show that the framework improves executability and reliability of AI scheduling and reduces loss of transient opportunities. In particular, feedback correction and structured validation of reasoning steps enhance the ability to repair and block erroneous decisions, especially in complex scenarios. Compared with pure AI methods, the framework-enhanced approach maintains flexibility while substantially improving reliability and executability. These results demonstrate a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling.

AI.tldr: A multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework is proposed to verify AI-generated telescope scheduling decisions, improving reliability, executability, and error traceability through data validation, logical consistency checks, and structured reasoning units.
AI.motivation: AI-based telescope scheduling suffers from inconsistent data references, reasoning errors, and non-executable decisions, limiting its applicability in high-reliability observational tasks.
AI.method: The framework integrates data reference validation, logical consistency checks, observational and instrumental constraint verification, and atomic reasoning units with dependency relationships to represent scheduling decisions as interconnected reasoning steps.
AI.result: The framework improves executability and reliability of AI scheduling, reduces loss of transient opportunities, and enhances the ability to repair and block erroneous decisions, especially in complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework-enhanced approach maintains flexibility while substantially improving reliability and executability, demonstrating a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling.

title: Retrieval-Warmed Energy-Based Reasoning: A Five-Arm Ablation Methodology for Diffusion-as-Inference on Structured Reasoning Tasks
summary: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it is rarely clear which part of the pipeline carries the gain. We study \textbf{retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR)} -- an IRED energy-based diffusion model \cite{du2024ired} augmented with a Modern Hopfield trajectory memory -- and contribute a \textbf{five-arm ablation methodology} (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) that separates three confounded effects: class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse. The diagnostic decomposition is adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation \cite{ru2024ragchecker}. On \textbf{connectivity-2} (Erdős--Rényi all-pairs reachability), the aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches \textbf{$+35$\,pp} balanced accuracy on a fixed 1{,}000-graph validation-set diagnostic, with value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed, only per-graph alignment destroyed, while per-query random initialisation falls below cold -- per-graph alignment, not bias shift or stochasticity, dominates. Yet the \emph{deployable} cold-prediction pipeline misses the acceptance gate at stored-value quality. The same diagnostic logic, stopped at the key-quality screen, applied to \textbf{Sudoku} with a task-specific key encoder produces a clean negative at a \emph{different} component -- key quality, under the current setup. The decomposition names the first blocking component on each task. The setting -- graph reachability refined by an iterative diffusion sampler, with explainability of failure modes as the lens -- places the work within structured and spatio-temporal reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR) with a five-arm ablation methodology to isolate three confounded effects (class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, graph-aligned value reuse) in warm-started diffusion samplers. On connectivity-2 tasks, graph-aligned value reuse dominates (+35 pp balanced accuracy), while on Sudoku, key quality is the blocking component.
AI.motivation: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it is unclear which component (class-prior bias, stochastic warm-starting, or graph-aligned value reuse) carries the gain. The paper aims to decompose and isolate these confounded effects.
AI.method: A five-arm ablation methodology (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation, applied to an IRED energy-based diffusion model with Modern Hopfield trajectory memory. Tested on Erdős–Rényi connectivity-2 (all-pairs reachability) and Sudoku tasks.
AI.result: On connectivity-2, aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches +35 pp balanced accuracy, showing per-graph alignment dominates. Per-query random initialization performs worse than cold start. On Sudoku, key quality is the blocking component (negative result under current setup).
AI.conclusion: The decomposition names the first blocking component per task: graph-aligned value reuse for connectivity, key quality for Sudoku. The deployable cold-prediction pipeline misses acceptance gate at stored-value quality. The methodology provides explainability of failure modes in structured/spatio-temporal reasoning.

title: Mask to Concept: Auto-Promptable SAM3 via Efficient Test-Time Concept Embedding Search for Few-Shot Annotation
summary: Transforming foundation segmentation models from human-prompted tools into auto-promptable annotators is critical for scalable medical data annotation. Current methods commonly depend on external feature matchers or auxiliary networks to automate geometric prompting, but introducing architectural overhead and limiting performance scalability. Although SAM3 natively supports concept segmentation via reusable text prompts, its direct use in medical imaging is hindered by a lack of fine-grained clinical knowledge and the ambiguity of human-written descriptions. In this work, we propose Mask to Concept (M2C), an efficient framework that adapts SAM3 for medical few-shot annotation without external modules, parameter retraining, or manual text engineering. Using only a few labeled images, M2C enables SAM3 to automatically search for transferable visual concepts entirely within its frozen architecture: it initializes a learnable concept embedding, uses it to prompt segmentation, and updates the embedding by gradients of minimizing the concept segmentation error. We further introduce a Hybrid Uncertainty Estimation (HUE) module that calculates the prediction entropy and maps concept predictions back to the box prompts, measuring concept-geometry prompting inconsistency. Highly uncertain samples are flagged actively for human correction, and the corrected masks are then fed back to M2C to continuously search for more precise concept embeddings, forming a self-enhancing annotation loop with minimal expert effort. Experiments on medical segmentation benchmarks show that our method achieves SOTA few-shot segmentation performance and outstanding annotation efficiency, offering a practical and efficient pathway toward scalable medical image labeling. Codes are at https://github.com/Huster-Hq/M2C.

AI.tldr: M2C adapts SAM3 for medical few-shot annotation by learning transferable visual concepts within its frozen architecture, using a Hybrid Uncertainty Estimation module to actively flag uncertain samples for human correction, forming a self-enhancing annotation loop that achieves SOTA few-shot segmentation performance without external modules or retraining.
AI.motivation: Transforming foundation segmentation models from human-prompted tools into auto-promptable annotators is critical for scalable medical data annotation, but current methods rely on external feature matchers or auxiliary networks that introduce architectural overhead. SAM3 natively supports concept segmentation via text prompts but lacks fine-grained clinical knowledge and suffers from ambiguous human-written descriptions.
AI.method: M2C (Mask to Concept) adapts SAM3 by: (1) initializing a learnable concept embedding within the frozen SAM3 architecture, (2) using it to prompt segmentation and updating the embedding via gradient minimization of concept segmentation error, (3) introducing a Hybrid Uncertainty Estimation (HUE) module that calculates prediction entropy and maps concept predictions back to box prompts to detect inconsistency, and (4) flagging highly uncertain samples for human correction, feeding corrected masks back to refine concept embeddings in a self-enhancing loop.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art few-shot segmentation performance and outstanding annotation efficiency on medical segmentation benchmarks, offering a practical pathway for scalable medical image labeling.
AI.conclusion: M2C provides an efficient framework for medical few-shot annotation without external modules, parameter retraining, or manual text engineering, enabling scalable medical data annotation with minimal expert effort.

title: Content-Based Smart E-Mail Dispatcher Using Large Language Models
summary: Email communication has become an integral part of personal and professional life, but handling its vast volume is still a significant issue for large organisations. Manual perusal of emails and forwarding their contents and attachments to intended recipients using other instant messaging platforms has proved to be error-prone and time-consuming leading to losses in terms of productivity and creating undue stress. The main objective of this paper is to explore an alternative mechanism that is to automate the task of dispatching emails based on their contents to the respective WhatsApp groups of students of various semesters of programs in an engineering college, facilitating a smooth flow of information from one end to another end in an organisation. The dispatcher system is built using agents querying large language models (LLMs) to enable it to analyze the contents of emails and route them to the relevant groups of students for their information and consumption. The system harnesses the capabilities of LLMs in analysing the textual contents for decision-making. With a well-structured agent framework prompt that includes email content as input with instructions and context, the system figures out the relevant groups to which the email message is dispatched, thus providing the required information on time. The proposed system does not rely on labelled datasets and provides several benefits, including enhanced productivity and a reduction in the cognitive load associated with reading emails.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an automated email dispatching system that uses LLM-powered agents to analyze email contents and route them to relevant WhatsApp groups of students in an engineering college, eliminating the need for manual forwarding and reducing cognitive load.
AI.motivation: Manual perusal and forwarding of emails to recipients via instant messaging platforms is error-prone, time-consuming, reduces productivity, and creates undue stress in large organizations, particularly in educational settings where information needs to reach specific student groups.
AI.method: The system uses agents that query large language models (LLMs) within a structured agent framework prompt. The prompt includes email content as input along with instructions and context, enabling the LLM to analyze textual content and determine the relevant WhatsApp groups (based on semester and program) to dispatch each email to, without requiring labeled datasets.
AI.result: The system successfully automates email routing to appropriate student groups, providing timely information delivery. It achieves this without relying on labeled datasets, harnessing LLMs' natural language understanding capabilities for decision-making.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-agent-based dispatcher system offers a viable alternative to manual email forwarding, enhancing productivity and reducing cognitive load associated with email management in organizational settings like engineering colleges.

title: What Survives When You Compress a Recursive Reasoner for the Edge?
summary: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex structured tasks with only a few million parameters by repeatedly updating a latent state. Deploying these models on edge hardware requires significant compression, but unlike conventional sequence models, quantization errors compound across recursive reasoning cycles rather than across output tokens.
