As a result, standard intuitions about compression fail to apply. In this work, we ask what survives when recursive reasoners are compressed. Across a full precision sweep, three tasks, and two recursive architectures, we find that aggressive compression preserves local prediction but destroys global reasoning: cell accuracy holds while puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero under naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention alike. Token-level objectives, including quantization-aware training, cannot repair it. The collapse is architectural -- it strikes MLP-mixing recursion but not attention on the same task -- and we reverse it with per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. We also introduce carry-trajectory fidelity, the cosine similarity to the full-precision reasoning path, as a label-free signal that predicts this damage and its recovery before a task evaluation. The combined result is a deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

AI.tldr: Aggressive compression of recursive reasoning models preserves local cell predictions but destroys global reasoning ability; the collapse is architectural (strikes MLP-mixing but not attention) and can be reversed via per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. A deployment recipe is provided for edge hardware using flash-streamed embeddings, INT8, and calibrated INT4.
AI.motivation: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex structured tasks with few parameters when deployed on edge hardware, but standard quantization intuitions fail because errors compound across recursive reasoning cycles rather than output tokens. The authors aim to understand what survives when such models are aggressively compressed.
AI.method: Full precision sweep across three tasks and two recursive architectures (MLP-mixing recursion vs. attention); tested with naive INT4 pruning, distillation, linear attention, and quantization-aware training; introduced carry-trajectory fidelity (cosine similarity to full-precision reasoning path) as a label-free diagnostic signal; applied per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining.
AI.result: Aggressive compression preserves cell accuracy but collapses puzzle-exact accuracy to zero; token-level objectives cannot fix it; the collapse is architectural (affects MLP-mixing but not attention); per-channel calibrated INT4 reverses the collapse; carry-trajectory fidelity predicts damage and recovery before task evaluation.
AI.conclusion: A deployment recipe is provided: flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

title: LLM-based Models for Detecting Emerging Topics in Service Feedback
summary:
Enhancing the analysis of service feedback is essential for public sector organizations, particularly tax administrations, where trust and compliance depend on fair and effective service delivery. As feedback volumes grow, identifying emerging service quality issues and potential disparities across diverse populations becomes increasingly challenging. Traditional approaches often rely on manual review or static expert-defined indicators, limiting scalability and the ability to capture complex patterns in textual feedback.
  This paper presents a novel methodology that integrates large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human-AI collaboration to improve multilingual customer feedback analysis. The primary objective is to detect emerging service quality topics that may also reveal potential inequities in service delivery. Our framework combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight to produce accurate, computationally efficient, and context-aware analyses.
  The proposed approach was evaluated using similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers, demonstrating stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models. By incorporating a human-in-the-loop framework, the methodology reduces LLM fabrication while improving the reliability and relevance of generated insights.
  The results demonstrate the practicality of combining LLMs with human expertise to support scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations. This work contributes to the development of responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through more effective analysis of multilingual customer feedback.

AI.tldr: A human-in-the-loop framework combining fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight to analyze multilingual customer feedback for tax administrations, detecting emerging service quality issues and potential inequities with better alignment to expert judgments than baseline models.
AI.motivation: Public sector organizations (especially tax administrations) face growing volumes of multilingual service feedback; traditional manual review or static expert-defined indicators lack scalability and fail to capture complex patterns, making it difficult to identify emerging service quality issues and potential disparities across diverse populations.
AI.method: Integrates fine-tuned, quantized large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human-AI collaboration (human-in-the-loop) to analyze multilingual customer feedback. Combines computationally efficient LLMs with expert oversight to reduce fabrication and improve reliability.
AI.result: Evaluated via similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers; the proposed framework demonstrated stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models, with reduced LLM fabrication and improved reliability and relevance of generated insights.
AI.conclusion: The methodology demonstrates practicality for scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations, contributing to responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through more effective analysis of multilingual feedback.

title: Learning Probabilistic Filters with Strictly Proper Scoring Rules
summary: Bayesian filtering of partially and noisily observed dynamical systems seeks to infer the evolving conditional distribution of the state of a dynamical system, given observations, in an online fashion. This Bayesian filtering distribution is the natural object for uncertainty quantification, but it is rarely available as a supervised learning target. However, one can often use the forecast model to generate synthetic system trajectories, along with synthetic observations. We introduce the proper scoring ensemble filter (PSEF), an ensemble data assimilation method based on training an analysis map to approximate the filtering distribution using only synthetic state--observation trajectories. The analysis step is represented as a permutation-invariant, transformer-based map that takes as input a forecast ensemble and observations, producing an analysis ensemble. Training is based on strictly proper scoring rules -- with the energy score used in our implementation -- so that probabilistic accuracy is rewarded over the whole probability distribution. We prove that, under a realizability assumption, the population objective is minimized by the true Bayesian filtering distribution. We also derive the finite-ensemble empirical objective used in training and relate its single state--observation trajectory form to the population objective, using a mean-field consistency argument. Numerical experiments show that the learned filter accurately approximates challenging filtering distributions, including nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors, and achieves stronger performance in data assimilation tasks than classical methods or learning-based methods with mean-squared-error objectives. For close-to-Gaussian problems, learning a correction to the EnKF is the best approach, while for highly non-Gaussian problems an end-to-end approach that discards this inductive bias is superior.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the proper scoring ensemble filter (PSEF), a transformer-based ensemble data assimilation method that learns to approximate the Bayesian filtering distribution using synthetic trajectories and strictly proper scoring rules, outperforming classical and learning-based methods on nonlinear, non-Gaussian problems.
AI.motivation: Bayesian filtering distributions are crucial for uncertainty quantification in dynamical systems but are rarely available as supervised learning targets; the authors aim to learn the filtering distribution without direct access to it.
AI.method: PSEF uses a permutation-invariant transformer-based analysis map that takes forecast ensemble and observations as input to produce an analysis ensemble. Training uses strictly proper scoring rules (energy score) on synthetic state-observation trajectories, with theoretical guarantees under realizability and mean-field consistency.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show PSEF accurately approximates nonlinear, non-Gaussian, and multi-modal posteriors, outperforming classical methods and MSE-based learning methods. For near-Gaussian problems, learning a correction to EnKF is best; for highly non-Gaussian problems, an end-to-end approach without inductive bias is superior.
AI.conclusion: Proper scoring rule-based learning with permutation-invariant transformers enables effective ensemble filtering without direct access to the true filtering distribution, with performance advantages in challenging non-Gaussian settings.

title: Full spectrum Unlearnable Examples via Spectral Equalization
summary: Unlearnable examples (UEs) protect training data by injecting imperceptible perturbations so that models fail to extract exploitable representations. In this paper, we reveal that existing UEs exhibit a critical failure once low-pass filtering is applied, indicating that the effective perturbation signals for unlearnability concentrate predominantly in high frequencies. Hence, we argue that reliable UEs should remain effective across the full spectrum. To this end, we propose Full-spectrum Unlearnable examples via Spectral Equalization (FUSE), which aims to generate spectrum-agnostic perturbations by equalizing the contributions from different bands and enforcing cross-band consistency. Specifically, FUSE adopts a Random Spectral Masking (RSM) strategy during generator training, which randomly removes a contiguous frequency band, forcing the remaining bands to maintain unlearnability. In addition, FUSE further integrates Cross-Band Guidance (CBG), which enforces mutual consistency between high- and low-frequency components, thereby further enhancing low-frequency unlearnability and regulating high-frequency perturbations to preserve the semantic fidelity of images. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, architectures, and spectral filtering demonstrate the strong protection achieved by FUSE.

AI.tldr: Existing unlearnable examples (UEs) fail under low-pass filtering because unlearnability signals concentrate in high frequencies. The authors propose FUSE (Full-spectrum Unlearnable examples via Spectral Equalization), which uses Random Spectral Masking (RSM) and Cross-Band Guidance (CBG) to generate spectrum-agnostic perturbations that remain effective across the full frequency spectrum.
AI.motivation: Existing UEs become ineffective after low-pass filtering, as their perturbation signals are concentrated in high frequencies, making them unreliable for robust data protection.
AI.method: FUSE generates full-spectrum unlearnable perturbations via two components: (1) Random Spectral Masking (RSM), which randomly removes a contiguous frequency band during training to force remaining bands to maintain unlearnability; (2) Cross-Band Guidance (CBG), which enforces mutual consistency between high- and low-frequency components to enhance low-frequency unlearnability while preserving semantic fidelity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, architectures, and spectral filtering methods demonstrate strong protection achieved by FUSE.
AI.conclusion: Reliable UEs should remain effective across the full spectrum; FUSE achieves this via spectral equalization, producing spectrum-agnostic perturbations that resist various spectral filtering attacks.

title: Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code
comment: Accepted at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD), ICML 2026
summary: Agent safety in high-stakes domains requires formal policy enforcement, but most existing approaches either rely on probabilistic guardrails (fine-tuned classifiers, prompt-based steering) that offer no formal guarantees, or on hand-coded symbolic enforcement that does not scale to the breadth of real policy specifications. We present an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies using an LLM-based generator-critic loop. The resulting policies are written in the Cedar Policy Language. On the MedAgentBench benchmark, our autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement in prior work.

AI.tldr: An autoformalization pipeline using LLM-based generator-critic loop translates natural language agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and policy documents into formally verified Cedar policies, achieving better coverage than hand-coded symbolic enforcement on MedAgentBench.
AI.motivation: Existing agent safety approaches either rely on probabilistic guardrails (fine-tuned classifiers, prompt-based steering) that offer no formal guarantees, or hand-coded symbolic enforcement that does not scale to real policy specifications. There is a need for formal policy enforcement that is both verifiable and scalable.
AI.method: An autoformalization pipeline that uses an LLM-based generator-critic loop to translate agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies written in the Cedar Policy Language.
AI.result: On the MedAgentBench benchmark, the autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement used in prior work.
AI.conclusion: Autoformalization via LLM-based generator-critic loop can produce formally verified policies with better coverage than hand-coded symbolic enforcement, offering a scalable path to formal policy enforcement for agent safety in high-stakes domains.

title: Multipath Adaptive Gated Bottleneck Latent ODE with Raman Data Fusion for Cell Culture Process Forecasting
summary: Mammalian cell-culture processes underpin the manufacture of many biopharmaceuticals, yet keeping a run on track is hard: critical process parameters drift over days, and an off-specification trend is often confirmed too late to intervene. Early-stage, multi-day forecasts could enable timely adjustment of feeding, sampling, and control, but bioprocess forecasting is challenging because measurements are sparse and irregularly sampled, operating conditions are heterogeneous across cell lines and media, and runs with near-identical early behaviour can diverge into different futures. We propose an adaptive framework combining a Gated Bottleneck Latent Ordinary Differential Equation (GB-Latent ODE) with Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT). The GB-Latent ODE augments the stan dard Latent ODE with learnable variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck that compress high-dimensional sparse inputs, improving learning under limited data. Given a partially observed run, MP-JIT-FT retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters the local neighbourhood into candidate regimes, and fine-tunes a separate model per regime to produce multiple plausible paths, each with a reconstruction-based confidence score, not a single averaged forecast. We further fuse Raman spectroscopy data: a machine-learning soft sensor turns dense Raman spectra into pseudo-observations that enrich the sparse offline measurements for more robust training. On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs spanning 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves the best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Using local-divergence metrics, we show the multi-path gains are largest when locally similar prefixes diverge, whereas Raman fusion helps most when early dynamics are representative of later behaviour.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an adaptive framework (GB-Latent ODE + MP-JIT-FT) for early-stage, multi-day forecasting of mammalian cell-culture bioprocesses, using sparse irregular measurements and Raman spectroscopy fusion. It outperforms global Latent ODE baselines on 38 fed-batch runs across 14 conditions, with multi-path forecasts most beneficial when local trajectories diverge.
AI.motivation: Mammalian cell-culture bioprocesses are difficult to monitor and control because critical parameters drift over days, measurements are sparse and irregularly sampled, operating conditions are heterogeneous across cell lines and media, and early-similar runs can diverge into different futures. Off-specification trends are often detected too late for intervention, motivating the need for early-stage, multi-day forecasting to enable timely adjustment.
AI.method: The framework combines (1) a Gated Bottleneck Latent ODE (GB-Latent ODE) with learnable variable-wise gating and mask-aware bottleneck for sparse inputs, and (2) Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT) that retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters into regimes, fine-tunes separate models per regime, and outputs multiple plausible paths with confidence scores. Raman spectroscopy data is fused via a soft-sensor to produce pseudo-observations enriching sparse offline measurements.
AI.result: Evaluated on 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs across 14 conditions. MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves the best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Multi-path gains are largest when locally similar prefixes diverge; Raman fusion helps most when early dynamics are representative of later behaviour.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive framework with multi-path forecasting and Raman fusion effectively addresses the challenges of sparse, heterogeneous bioprocess data, enabling early detection of divergence and more robust forecasting compared to global baselines.

title: Robust Onion: Peeling Open Vocab Object Detectors Under Noise
comment: Accepted at The 19th European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV)
summary: The impact of real-world noise on Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) remains poorly understood due to their architectural complexity. We present our comprehensive analysis Robust Onion, an empirical study that uses controlled synthetic visual degradations to peel OV-ODs layer-by-layer, revealing how, why, and where robustness degrades, systematically analyzing feature collapse. Our findings reveal that models with similar vision backbones exhibit comparable robustness, driven by similar feature collapse at similar layers, while factors such as pretraining strategy, architectural nuances, and caption supervision contribute little. Robustness is primarily governed by the image domain rather than annotations, explaining the similar robustness impact on COCO and LVIS, and why datasets like ODinW-13 can give an impression of inflated robustness due to large, isolated objects. Finally, we validate our insights by improving robustness on real-world BDD100K, WiderFace, and VisDRONE via our lightweight plug-and-play NN & TK0 approach, using 96x fewer trainable parameters than end-to-end training. We also explain the prior works' robustness observations.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically analyzes the robustness of Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) to real-world noise using controlled synthetic degradations. It finds that robustness is primarily determined by the vision backbone and image domain, not by pretraining strategy or annotations. The authors propose a lightweight plug-and-play approach (NN & TK0) that improves robustness with 96x fewer parameters than end-to-end training.
AI.motivation: The impact of real-world noise on Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) is poorly understood due to their architectural complexity. The authors aim to systematically analyze how, why, and where robustness degrades in these models.
AI.method: The authors conduct a comprehensive empirical study using controlled synthetic visual degradations to "peel" OV-ODs layer-by-layer, analyzing feature collapse. They compare models with different vision backbones, pretraining strategies, architectural nuances, and caption supervision. They also propose a lightweight plug-and-play approach called NN & TK0 to improve robustness.
AI.result: Models with similar vision backbones exhibit comparable robustness driven by similar feature collapse at similar layers. Factors like pretraining strategy, architectural nuances, and caption supervision contribute little. Robustness is primarily governed by the image domain rather than annotations. The proposed NN & TK0 approach improves robustness on real-world datasets (BDD100K, WiderFace, VisDRONE) using 96x fewer trainable parameters than end-to-end training.
AI.conclusion: Robustness in OV-ODs is primarily determined by the vision backbone and image domain. The lightweight NN & TK0 approach effectively improves robustness with minimal parameters. The findings explain prior works' robustness observations and why certain datasets can give an inflated impression of robustness.

title: SKILL-DISCO: Distilling and Compiling Agent Traces into Reusable Procedural Skills
summary: Agents often repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, leading to unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces. Prior work has explored workflow reuse and executable skill induction, but it remains unclear which task scenarios admit procedural skills and how the shared procedural structure should be represented across successful traces. We study this problem in FSM-defined scenarios, where successful traces can be viewed as paths in an unknown transition graph, and formulate procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs. Based on this view, we introduce SkillDisCo, a distillation-and-compilation framework that distills reusable PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show that SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales, demonstrating the benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

AI.tldr: SkillDisCo distills reusable procedural skills (as PFSM subgraphs) from successful agent traces and compiles them into callable modules, improving success rates and reducing agent turns on ALFWorld and WebArena.
AI.motivation: Agents repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, incurring unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces, and prior work lacks clarity on which scenarios admit procedural skills and how to represent shared procedural structure across traces.
AI.method: SkillDisCo is a distillation-and-compilation framework that (1) distills reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs (PFSM) from successful traces in FSM-defined scenarios, and (2) compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills.
AI.result: On ALFWorld and WebArena, SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales.
AI.conclusion: Representing shared experience as reusable execution structures (PFSM subgraphs) benefits agent efficiency and effectiveness in FSM-defined task scenarios.

title: Theory-Scale Auto-Formalization of Logics for Computer Science
summary: Auto-formalization is critical for scalable formal verification, but existing progress largely focuses on isolated statements, while theory-scale auto-formalization, which coherently translates hundreds of interdependent definitions, lemmas, and theorems, remains open due to challenges in consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness. In this paper, we introduce LCS-Bench, a stand-alone, theory-scale benchmark based on Logics for Computer Science. LCS-Bench is built through a novel semi-automated agentic pipeline that leverages concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, sorry-filling with counter-example search, complemented by faithfulness review from human experts. The resulting artifact covers 327 textbook items, over 4,076 Lean declarations, and more than 85K lines of Lean code. The dataset supports broad evaluation through a data engine that automatically derives five tracks of evaluation benchmarks, measuring different aspects of auto-formalization and theorem-proving capabilities. We also introduce a novel evaluation protocol featuring definitional equivalence checkers, enabling more fine-grained and faithful assessment. Through extensive evaluation on 14 models, we demonstrate that (1) LCS-Bench is of high quality, consistent, and faithful; (2) the benchmark is challenging, with state-of-the-art models achieving only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks; and (3) our analysis reveals key findings regarding theory-scale auto-formalization and suggests promising directions for future work.

AI.tldr: LCS-Bench is a theory-scale auto-formalization benchmark built from Logics for Computer Science, featuring 327 textbook items, 4,076 Lean declarations, and 85K+ lines of Lean code. It includes a semi-automated pipeline, five evaluation tracks, and a definitional equivalence checker protocol. SOTA models achieve only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks, highlighting the challenge.
AI.motivation: Existing auto-formalization focuses on isolated statements; theory-scale formalization involving hundreds of interdependent definitions, lemmas, and theorems remains unsolved due to challenges in consistency, faithfulness, scalability, and correctness.
AI.method: A semi-automated agentic pipeline using concept graphs, formal signature planning, issue tracking, sorry-filling with counter-example search, and human expert review. A data engine auto-derives five evaluation tracks. A novel evaluation protocol uses definitional equivalence checkers for fine-grained assessment.
AI.result: LCS-Bench contains 327 textbook items, 4,076 Lean declarations, and 85K+ lines of Lean code. Evaluated on 14 models, SOTA achieves only 20.1% on auto-formalization tasks.
AI.conclusion: LCS-Bench is high-quality, consistent, and faithful. The benchmark is challenging and reveals key findings about theory-scale auto-formalization, suggesting promising future directions.

title: Do Image Editing Models Understand Lighting?
summary: While recent advancements in generative image editing models have achieved stunning visual fidelity, it remains an open question whether these systems possess an intrinsic knowledge of real-world lighting. Existing benchmarks typically evaluate high-level plausibility of perceptual light transport on curated internet imagery, using VLMs or human judgement, or they rely on synthetically generated datasets. In this work, we introduce the 3D-anchored Light Probe (3DLP) benchmark, for which we have captured a new high-fidelity HDR dataset of real-world lighting changes. The dataset consists of 1K image pairs of diverse indoor scenery in which light probes are physically turned on and off. To allow for a granular performance analysis, we annotated specific image regions such as cast shadows or metallic surfaces. With this data, we evaluate a range of state-of-the-art image editing models by measuring how well their light probe edits align with reality. The evaluation uses two new scores to compensate for AI-generated photographic effects, such as adjusted white balance. Our results show that the overall performance of models differs considerably, with differences slightly less pronounced for specular highlights. The best image editing models are remarkably consistent with real-world physics, however, they still leave room for improvement. We observe that image regions that receive less light from the light probe are more prone to errors for all models. Furthermore, building on their success in evaluating macroscopic lighting plausibility, we test VLMs on our task but find that they are unsuitable for pixel-level light transport analysis. We will make the benchmark, together with the real-world dataset, publicly available to encourage future research on this topic.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the 3DLP benchmark with a real-world HDR dataset of 1K image pairs capturing lighting changes from light probes being turned on/off, annotated with specific regions (cast shadows, metallic surfaces). State-of-the-art image editing models are evaluated using two new scores that compensate for AI photographic effects, revealing varied performance with specular highlights being handled best and dimly lit regions being most error-prone. VLMs are found unsuitable for pixel-level light transport analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for generative image editing models either evaluate perceptual lighting plausibility using VLMs/human judgment on curated internet imagery or rely on synthetic datasets. There is no benchmark that assesses whether these models possess intrinsic knowledge of real-world lighting physics using real-world, high-fidelity lighting change data.
AI.method: The authors captured a new high-fidelity HDR dataset of 1K image pairs of diverse indoor scenes where light probes are physically toggled on/off. Image regions were annotated (e.g., cast shadows, metallic surfaces). They evaluate state-of-the-art image editing models using two new scores designed to compensate for AI-generated photographic effects like adjusted white balance.
AI.result: Model performance varies considerably, with differences slightly less pronounced for specular highlights. Best models are consistent with real-world physics but have room for improvement. Image regions receiving less light from the probe are more error-prone across all models. VLMs are unsuitable for pixel-level light transport analysis.
AI.conclusion: The 3DLP benchmark and real-world dataset enable granular evaluation of lighting understanding in generative image editing models. Results show current models still have limitations, especially in low-light regions, and VLMs cannot replace pixel-level analysis. The benchmark will be released publicly.

title: NebulaExp-8B: An Empirical Post-Training Pipeline via Full-Scale Ablation Research
summary: Post-training alignment determines the reasoning and human preference following capabilities of large language models, yet most existing works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, which hinders community reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. This work presents NebulaExp, a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline built on Qwen3-8B-base, covering two orthogonal model branches: general instruct model and complex reasoning-specialized model. We curate a raw corpus of 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and a 200K verifiable RL candidate pool, and design an end-to-end data processing stack including response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification and diversity-aware sampling. For the Instruct branch, our three-stage optimized supervised fine-tuning approach NebulaExp-Ins-SFT improves the average benchmark score from the 55.01 baseline of Qwen3-8B-nothink to 60.99. GRPO reinforcement learning then further elevates the average score to 61.85. For the Reasoning branch, medium-difficulty GRPO RL improves average reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. To address RL's dependency on task verifiers, we systematically investigate single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD (MOPD): utilizing merely 4K instruction-following samples and outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 points on IFEval with +4.43 average overall gain; MOPD fuses four domain-specialist teachers with merely 10K samples, lifting average performance by 4.18 over the base model. This report provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs, and comprehensively dissects the capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation and general knowledge.

AI.tldr: NebulaExp presents a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline for Qwen3-8B, covering general instruct and reasoning-specialized models with 3.84M SFT samples and 200K RL candidates. The Instruct branch achieves 61.85 average score via three-stage SFT + GRPO RL, and the Reasoning branch reaches 75.17 via medium-difficulty GRPO. Multi-teacher OPD (MOPD) outperforms RL by 4.43 points on IFEval using only 10K samples.
AI.motivation: Most existing post-training works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, hindering reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. This work aims to provide a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline to address this gap.
AI.method: The pipeline includes: (1) a raw corpus of 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and 200K verifiable RL candidates; (2) an end-to-end data processing stack with response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, difficulty grading, task classification, and diversity-aware sampling; (3) three-stage optimized SFT for Instruct branch + GRPO RL; (4) medium-difficulty GRPO RL for Reasoning branch; (5) single-teacher OPD and multi-teacher MOPD as alternatives to RL verifiers.
AI.result: Instruct branch: baseline 55.01 → NebulaExp-Ins-SFT 60.99 → +GRPO RL 61.85. Reasoning branch: baseline 73.88 → 75.17. OPD with 4K samples outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 on IFEval (+4.43 avg overall); MOPD with 10K samples lifts avg performance by 4.18 over base model.
AI.conclusion: The report provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs and comprehensively dissects capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general knowledge.

title: Sample-efficient Transfer Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Reward Shaping and Policy-Ratio Reweighting Strategy
summary: Transfer learning improves policy learning efficiency by reusing knowledge from source tasks, providing a feasible paradigm for safe and efficient autonomous highway lane changing decision-making. Existing methods frequently encounter transfer mismatch induced by distribution shifts between source and target domains, leading to training oscillation and performance decline. Besides, target domain adaptation depends on exploratory interactions, which struggles to guarantee training safety in safety-critical lane changing cases. To tackle these limitations, this paper proposes a safe transfer reinforcement learning framework for autonomous highway lane changing. First, we design an adaptive teacher intervention mechanism based on instantaneous safety cost to restrain risky exploration and fade intervention strength progressively, with theoretical analysis on return bounds for mixed behavior policy. This intervention also produces dual-source samples for joint training. Second, a teacher-guided safe transfer module embeds action evaluation information of teacher policy into student learning via reward shaping to boost training safety and efficiency, with teacher guidance decaying as policy safety rises. Third, a teacher-guided weighted optimization mechanism adjusts sample weights in policy optimization using a likelihood ratio factor to stabilize transfer performance. Experiments under varied traffic densities and validations on real-world NGSIM dataset reveal that our method surpasses baseline approaches by over 52.2% in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency. Results verify the efficacy and robustness of our safety-aware transfer strategy for autonomous highway lane changing under various traffic conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a safe transfer reinforcement learning framework for autonomous highway lane changing, featuring an adaptive teacher intervention mechanism, teacher-guided safe transfer via reward shaping, and teacher-guided weighted optimization to address transfer mismatch and safety challenges. Experiments show over 52.2% improvement in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing transfer learning methods for autonomous lane changing suffer from transfer mismatch due to distribution shifts between source and target domains, causing training oscillation and performance decline. Additionally, target domain adaptation requires exploratory interactions that compromise safety in critical lane-changing scenarios.
AI.method: (1) An adaptive teacher intervention mechanism based on instantaneous safety cost to restrain risky exploration, with theoretical analysis on return bounds for mixed behavior policy; (2) A teacher-guided safe transfer module that embeds action evaluation information into student learning via reward shaping, with decaying teacher guidance; (3) A teacher-guided weighted optimization mechanism that adjusts sample weights using a likelihood ratio factor to stabilize transfer performance.
AI.result: The method surpasses baseline approaches by over 52.2% in safety and 5.0% in learning efficiency under varied traffic densities and on the real-world NGSIM dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed safety-aware transfer strategy is effective and robust for autonomous highway lane changing under various traffic conditions, addressing both transfer mismatch and safety concerns.

title: LiveEdit: Towards Real-Time Diffusion-Based Streaming Video Editing
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026, Project page: https://live-edit.github.io
summary: Streaming video editing has made rapid progress, yet practical deployment is still limited by two core issues: maintaining stable backgrounds and non-edited regions over time, and achieving the low latency required for real-time interactive scenarios. Meanwhile, recent streaming video generation methods are mostly developed for synthesis and cannot be directly applied to editing due to the strict preservation requirement and region-specific control. In this work, we present a novel streaming video editing framework that performs causal, frame-by-frame editing with strong content preservation and real-time responsiveness. Our key design is a three-stage distillation pipeline that progressively transfers editing capability from a powerful bidirectional foundation model to an efficient unidirectional streaming editor, enabling stable long-horizon edits without sacrificing visual fidelity. To further support real-time deployment, we introduce an AR-oriented mask cache that reuses region-related computation across frames, substantially reducing redundant processing and accelerating inference. Finally, we establish a dedicated benchmark for streaming video editing. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines while drastically boosting inference speed to 12.66 FPS, making it suitable for interactive and augmented reality applications.

AI.tldr: A streaming video editing framework using a three-stage distillation pipeline (bidirectional→unidirectional) and AR-oriented mask cache, achieving 12.66 FPS with state-of-the-art visual quality for real-time interactive editing.
AI.motivation: Existing streaming video editing suffers from unstable backgrounds/non-edited regions and high latency. Streaming video generation methods cannot be directly applied to editing due to strict preservation requirements and region-specific control needs.
AI.method: (1) Three-stage distillation pipeline that progressively transfers editing capability from a powerful bidirectional foundation model to an efficient unidirectional streaming editor; (2) AR-oriented mask cache that reuses region-related computation across frames to reduce redundant processing; (3) A dedicated streaming video editing benchmark.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines and drastic inference speed improvement to 12.66 FPS, suitable for interactive and AR applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the core challenges of streaming video editing—content preservation and real-time latency—enabling practical deployment in interactive and augmented reality scenarios.

title: Do Safety Guardrails Need to Reason? LeanGuard: A Fast and Light Approach for Robust Moderation
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Project page: https://ndb796.github.io/LeanGuard ; code and models: https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard
summary: In order to screen a prompt or a response, the recent guardrail methods generate a chain-of-thought (CoT) before they issue a verdict. This design follows a common belief that step-by-step reasoning improves a decision. However, CoT also makes the guard heavy and slow, because the model must generate many tokens before it decides. This may not match how guardrails are actually deployed. A guardrail sometimes should not be heavy and slow, and it often runs on-device, for example on an embodied robot. In this paper, we pose a question whether a safety guardrail really needs to reason. To answer this question, we train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, and we then remove only the reasoning while we keep everything else fixed. With this controlled same-base comparison, we show that the chain does not improve moderation accuracy. We name the resulting guard LeanGuard. A 395M label-only encoder reaches an average F1 of 82.90 $\pm$ 0.26 over public benchmarks. It matches a reasoning guard that is built on a much larger decoder, while it uses only a single forward pass over an input of at most 512 tokens. This is about a ~100x reduction in inference compute. We further show that this label-only encoder stays robust under training-label noise and retains far more recall at a strict false-positive rate than the reasoning guard, so a heavier reasoning guard is not the more robust choice either. Our finding suggests that the current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and that the necessity of CoT for moderation is still not proven. We release all source codes and models including LeanGuard at https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard.

AI.tldr: LeanGuard shows that removing chain-of-thought reasoning from safety guardrails does not hurt accuracy (average F1 82.90) while achieving ~100x reduction in inference compute using a 395M bidirectional encoder, challenging the necessity of CoT for moderation.
AI.motivation: Existing guardrail methods use chain-of-thought reasoning before issuing safety verdicts, making them heavy and slow. This is problematic for deployment scenarios like on-device embodied robots where efficiency is critical. The paper questions whether safety guardrails truly need reasoning.
AI.method: The authors train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, then remove only the reasoning component while keeping everything else fixed for a controlled comparison. The resulting model (LeanGuard) is a 395M label-only encoder that processes inputs of at most 512 tokens in a single forward pass.
AI.result: LeanGuard achieves average F1 of 82.90 ± 0.26 across public benchmarks, matching a much larger reasoning guard while using ~100x less inference compute. It also shows better robustness under training-label noise and retains higher recall at strict false-positive rates compared to the reasoning guard.
AI.conclusion: Current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and the necessity of CoT for moderation remains unproven. The findings suggest that lightweight, non-reasoning guardrails can be both more efficient and equally or more effective than their reasoning counterparts.

title: CascadeFormer: Depth-Tapered Transformers Motivated by Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry
summary: Deep Transformers are composed of uniformly stacked residual blocks, yet their deepest layers often add little value. We present two efficiency methods that exploit this asymmetry. CascadeFormer tapers width with depth to match the uneven information flow across layers, achieving comparable perplexity to a uniform baseline at the same training budget while reducing latency by 8.6% and increasing throughput by 9.4%. CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers using accumulated training gradients, with no post hoc analysis. It outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and stays competitive on downstream accuracy. To motivate these methods, we propose Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) as a structural account of why deeper layers contribute less. In Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks, the gradient at a layer is the sum of an identity path and all downstream functional paths, producing a gradient fan-in that decays linearly with depth (and quadratically under deep supervision), yielding richer gradients for early layers and sparser ones for later layers. We provide correlational and interventional evidence for GFA on models trained from scratch up to 1.2B parameters. Across Transformers and ResNets, accumulated training gradients follow the theoretical fan-in and are associated with post hoc layer importance. Two interventions point to structure rather than magnitude as the bottleneck: equalizing per-layer gradient norms does not restore late-layer value, while increasing downstream path counts via parameter-shared repetition restores and elevates it. Whether gradient magnitude proxies fan-in beyond high-rank regimes, and how these dynamics behave at the 100B+ scale, remain open questions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CascadeFormer and CascadeFlow Pruning, two efficiency methods leveraging the insight that deeper layers in Transformers contribute less. They propose Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) to explain this phenomenon, showing gradient fan-in decays linearly with depth in Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks, providing both correlational and interventional evidence up to 1.2B parameter models.
AI.motivation: Deep Transformers have uniformly stacked residual blocks, but their deepest layers often provide minimal value, creating computational inefficiency. The authors aim to exploit this asymmetry to improve efficiency without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Two methods: (1) CascadeFormer tapers width with depth to match uneven information flow; (2) CascadeFlow Pruning removes layers using accumulated training gradients. They also propose Gradient Fan-in Asymmetry (GFA) as a theoretical account showing gradient fan-in decays linearly with depth (quadratically under deep supervision) in Pre-LayerNorm residual stacks.
AI.result: CascadeFormer achieves comparable perplexity while reducing latency by 8.6% and increasing throughput by 9.4%. CascadeFlow Pruning outperforms standard heuristics on perplexity and rank-stability and stays competitive on downstream accuracy. GFA is supported by correlational and interventional evidence on models up to 1.2B parameters across Transformers and ResNets.
AI.conclusion: Structure (gradient fan-in), not gradient magnitude, is the bottleneck for late-layer contributions. Equalizing per-layer gradient norms does not restore late-layer value, but increasing downstream path counts via parameter-shared repetition does. Open questions remain about gradient magnitude as fan-in proxy beyond high-rank regimes and dynamics at 100B+ scale.

title: Depth-Semantic Alignment and Affinity-Guided Fusion for Structured Radar Point Cloud Generation
summary: Point clouds are an important carrier of three-dimensional spatial information, and their quality directly affects the performance of downstream perception tasks such as object detection and tracking. However, millimeter-wave radar point clouds are typically sparse, noisy, and structurally incomplete. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a multimodal point cloud generation method based on vision-radar fusion. The proposed method leverages image semantic information to impose structural constraints and achieve spatial alignment for radar point clouds, while incorporating a sparse completion strategy to enhance point density and recover missing structures. The generated point clouds are further evaluated in object detection and tracking tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively improves point cloud quality and enhances the detection accuracy and robustness of perception models in complex environments, providing a practical solution for multisensor point cloud generation and intelligent perception systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a vision-radar fusion method to generate high-quality point clouds by using image semantic information for structural constraints and spatial alignment, combined with sparse completion to enhance density and recover missing structures, improving downstream detection and tracking performance.
AI.motivation: Millimeter-wave radar point clouds are typically sparse, noisy, and structurally incomplete, which limits the performance of downstream perception tasks such as object detection and tracking.
AI.method: A multimodal point cloud generation method based on vision-radar fusion: leverages image semantic information to impose structural constraints and achieve spatial alignment for radar point clouds, and incorporates a sparse completion strategy to enhance point density and recover missing structures.
AI.result: The proposed method effectively improves point cloud quality and enhances detection accuracy and robustness of perception models in complex environments.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a practical solution for multisensor point cloud generation and intelligent perception systems.

title: Kalman Prototypical Networks for Few-shot Fault Detection in Combined Cycle Gas Turbines
summary: Combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGTs) play a key role in modern power generation, offering both high efficiency and reduced environmental impact. However, their complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions complicate fault detection, particularly when labeled fault data are scarce. In this paper, we introduce the Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning (FSL) framework specifically tailored for CCGT fault diagnosis. We model the evolution of class prototypes as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system to reduce episodic variance and improve robustness in embedding representation. Synthetic data sets generated with a high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of an offshore CCGT system were used, simulating both normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions. Application of the proposed framework on simulated leak fault detection tasks demonstrate that KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods such as Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations. The proposed framework significantly improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it well-suited for real-world CCGT fault detection where labeled data is limited.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN) for few-shot fault diagnosis in combined-cycle gas turbines, using synthetic data from Modelica simulations to outperform conventional few-shot learning methods in accuracy and stability.
AI.motivation: CCGTs have complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions that complicate fault detection, and labeled fault data are scarce in real-world settings.
AI.method: KPN models class prototype evolution as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system using a Kalman filter to reduce episodic variance and improve embedding robustness, trained on synthetic data from a high-fidelity Modelica simulation of an offshore CCGT.
AI.result: KPN outperforms Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations on simulated leak fault detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: KPN improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it suitable for real-world CCGT fault diagnosis with limited labeled data.

title: Can Large Language Models Reliably Code Qualitative Humanitarian Data? A Benchmark Study Against Human Expert Adjudication
summary: Data from affected populations are crucial for informing humanitarian response, but their value depends on timely and consistent interpretation of nuanced accounts of need. Humanitarian organizations often lack the staff, time, and specialist expertise required to analyze this information at scale. Large language models (LLMs) may expand this capacity, but their reliability for coding qualitative humanitarian data has not been directly established. This benchmark study compares 46 LLMs to a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts. Evaluation combined inter-rater reliability testing with Krippendorff's alpha, discrepancy analysis distinguishing correct, near-correct, and incorrect codes, and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria including discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, and non-standard communication styles. The authors find that multiple LLMs can perform deductive coding at reliability levels comparable to experienced human coders, especially when structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations are used. At the same time, aggregate reliability metrics alone are insufficient for deployment decisions. Models varied in recognizing needs expressed indirectly, needs outside predefined categories, and protection-relevant concerns such as physical safety and discrimination. These findings suggest that LLMs can materially expand humanitarian analytical capacity, but not as substitutes for human judgment. Appropriate use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, attention to theme-specific performance, and tiered oversight focused on categories where miscoding would have the greatest programmatic consequences. For sensitive humanitarian data, open-weights models deployed on self-hosted infrastructure may offer a viable path for combining analytical scalability with stronger data governance.

AI.tldr: This benchmark study evaluates 46 LLMs against a human Gold Standard on coding 150 synthetic humanitarian transcripts, finding that LLMs can achieve human-comparable reliability for deductive coding when using structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations, but aggregate metrics alone are insufficient; models vary on indirect needs, out-of-category needs, and protection concerns, and appropriate deployment requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, theme-specific oversight, and open-weights models for sensitive data.
AI.motivation: Humanitarian organizations lack staff, time, and specialist expertise to analyze qualitative accounts of need at scale, and the reliability of LLMs for coding such data has not been directly established.
AI.method: 46 LLMs were benchmarked against a human Gold Standard using 150 high-fidelity synthetic humanitarian transcripts, evaluated via Krippendorff's alpha, discrepancy analysis (correct/near-correct/incorrect), and qualitative assessment across humanitarian-specific criteria (discrimination, complex needs hierarchies, non-standard communication styles).
AI.result: Multiple LLMs achieved human-comparable reliability for deductive coding with structured prompts and reasoning-enabled configurations; however, models varied in recognizing indirectly expressed needs, needs outside predefined categories, and protection concerns (physical safety, discrimination).
AI.conclusion: LLMs can expand humanitarian analytical capacity but not as substitutes for human judgment; appropriate use requires structured codebooks, reasoning-enabled models, attention to theme-specific performance, tiered oversight, and open-weights models on self-hosted infrastructure for sensitive data.

title: Capacity-Controlled Multi-View Stylization of 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://vcc2310.github.io/SceneStyler/
summary: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provides an efficient and explicit representation for novel view synthesis, enforcing stylistic coherence across viewpoints remains challenging. Existing 3D stylization methods typically apply 2D feature-matching losses independently per rendered view, which leads to unstable style allocation, many-to-one feature reuse, and limited cross-view consistency. We propose a capacity-controlled framework for multi-view stylization of 3DGS, grounded in optimal transport. Specifically, we reformulate local style matching as a semi-balanced optimal transport problem. By introducing explicit column-capacity constraints with tunable strength, our formulation mitigates many-to-one matching and enables controllable allocation of style features. This transport-based objective provides a principled mechanism for balancing feature coverage and stylistic diversity while maintaining stable correspondences across viewpoints. To further enhance cross-view coherence, we incorporate a novel cross-view matching guidance to constrain correspondences between scene content and style patterns. In addition, we introduce several geometric regularizations to enhance the vanilla 3DGS, thereby enabling optimized Gaussian primitives to represent finer-grained textures during stylization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves multi-view stylistic consistency and produces stable, expressive 3D stylizations while preserving the core semantic structure of the scene.

AI.tldr: A capacity-controlled optimal transport framework for multi-view stylization of 3D Gaussian Splatting that uses semi-balanced optimal transport with column-capacity constraints to mitigate many-to-one feature reuse and improve cross-view stylistic consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D stylization methods apply 2D feature-matching losses independently per rendered view, leading to unstable style allocation, many-to-one feature reuse, and limited cross-view consistency.
AI.method: (1) Reformulates local style matching as a semi-balanced optimal transport problem with explicit column-capacity constraints for controllable style feature allocation. (2) Introduces cross-view matching guidance to constrain scene content and style pattern correspondences. (3) Incorporates geometric regularizations to enhance vanilla 3DGS for finer-grained texture representation.
AI.result: The approach significantly improves multi-view stylistic consistency and produces stable, expressive 3D stylizations while preserving core semantic structure.
AI.conclusion: Optimal transport with capacity control provides a principled mechanism for balancing feature coverage and stylistic diversity while maintaining stable correspondences across viewpoints in 3D stylization.

title: LithoDreamer: A Physics-Informed World Model for Multi-Stage Computational Lithography
comment: Correspondence to: Qi Sun <qisunchn \at zju \dot edu \dot cn>
summary: As semiconductor technology nodes scale, computational lithography is essential for ensuring yield and performance. However, lithography is a continuous physical process involving mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development, which existing models fail to capture. To overcome this limitation, we present LithoDreamer, the first physics-informed World Model (WM) framework for computational lithography, which formulates the ``Layout-Mask-Resist Image-After Development Image (ADI)'' pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. LithoDreamer captures feature changes between adjacent states to model stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces, in which it controls process intervention exploration and drives subsequent state transitions. To achieve interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision, we propose a contrastive variational optimization paradigm that contrasts the latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints, guiding the model to generate evolutions consistent with real lithography physics. Experiments show LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning. Our lithography dataset is publicly available at GitHub (https://github.com/7jiangyq/lithodreamer.git).

AI.tldr: LithoDreamer is a physics-informed World Model framework for computational lithography that models the layout-to-ADI pipeline as a multi-step evolution system, achieving SOTA performance via contrastive variational optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing computational lithography models fail to capture the continuous physical process (mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, development) as a unified pipeline, limiting their accuracy and interpretability.
AI.method: LithoDreamer formulates the Layout-Mask-Resist Image-ADI pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. It captures stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces for process intervention exploration and state transitions. A contrastive variational optimization paradigm contrasts latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints to guide interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in both forward evolution and inverse planning tasks. The lithography dataset is publicly released.
AI.conclusion: The physics-informed World Model approach successfully models the multi-step lithography process with interpretable interventions, outperforming existing methods that treat lithography as a monolithic or disconnected process.

title: Revisiting Action Factorization for Complex Action Spaces
comment: 53 Pages, 37 Figures, 6 Tables, Target Journal/Venue: ACM Transactions on Autonomous and Adaptive Systems TAAS
summary: Many real-world control problems involve hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces. For example, steering and signaling in autonomous driving, and aiming and firing in robotics or video-games. Despite real-world hybrid factorization and reinforcement learning framework support for complex action spaces (e.g., Gymnasium, PettingZoo, TorchRL, SeedRL, Mujoco, etc), the default environments within those frameworks often implement uniform action space configurations (LunarLander, Walker2D, Cheetah, SMAC, SUMO, Ant, Atari). Landmark hybrid-action benchmarks (RoboCup 2D HFO, SC2LE, Platform, CARLA, etc) are mostly heavyweight or archival implementations originating from papers which test one or a small number of competing factorization methods on one kind of control. This article provides a cross-sectional study of factorization methods [independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive] on each of three families of algorithms [PPO, SAC, DQN] across three action spaces [discretized, hybrid, continuous] over four lightweight environments [Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush]. Accounting for some invalid pairings such as joint-continuous, we are left with 220 configurations to analyze each method. We provide two new C++ parallel gymnasium and petting-zoo compliant environments [CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot] to isolate particular challenges such as state-dependent inter-action dependence. Finally, we introduce VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX which use a branching critic to assign credit to multi-headed PPO. These variants out-perform all other tested PPO factorizations. Our results suggest that branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance most effectively, with Auto-Regressive actions reaching the highest performance overall and native continuous SAC outperforming discrete and hybrid algorithms, albiet both at increased computational cost.

AI.tldr: Cross-sectional study evaluating 6 factorization methods across 3 algorithm families (PPO, SAC, DQN) on 3 action spaces using 4 lightweight environments, introducing VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX variants and two new environments (CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot).
AI.motivation: Real-world control problems often feature hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, but existing benchmarks are heavyweight, archival, or use uniform action spaces. There is a lack of systematic cross-sectional comparison of factorization methods across algorithms and action types.
AI.method: Evaluates 6 factorization methods (independent networks, shared encoder, VDN, QPLEX, Joint, Auto-Regressive) across 3 algorithm families (PPO, SAC, DQN) on 3 action spaces (discretized, hybrid, continuous) over 4 lightweight environments (Platform, hybrid-LunarLander, Hybrid-Shoot, CoopPush). Introduces VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX using branching critics for credit assignment in multi-headed PPO.
AI.result: 220 configurations analyzed. Branching dueling architectures balance compute and performance best; Auto-Regressive actions achieve highest overall performance; native continuous SAC outperforms discrete and hybrid algorithms; VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX outperform all other tested PPO factorizations.
AI.conclusion: VDN-PPO and PPO-MIX with branching critics are effective for hybrid action spaces. Auto-Regressive actions and continuous SAC offer high performance at increased computational cost. Two new environments (CoopPush, Hybrid-Shoot) are provided for future benchmarking.

title: ProtoKV: Streaming Video Understanding under Delayed Query with Summary-State Memory
summary: Streaming video understanding (SVU) must answer queries that arrive asynchronously while visual tokens stream continuously under strict GPU-memory and query-time latency budgets. A key challenge is delayed query: decisive cues may appear briefly, yet many subsequent updates occur before the query arrives, increasing the risk that those cues are evicted or diluted under bounded memory. We propose ProtoKV, a constant-footprint SVU memory that represents far history as a fixed-capacity summary state rather than retaining token instances. ProtoKV keeps an exact near-window KV cache and aggregates older content into a semantic-spatial prototype bank with residual statistics. At query time, each prototype is exposed through a bounded pseudo-token interface that is drop-in compatible with standard attention. Under matched budgets and comparable query-time cost, ProtoKV improves accuracy by up to 12.5 points over token-retention baselines on SVU benchmarks in the long-delay regime, with gains that grow as query delay increases.

AI.tldr: ProtoKV is a constant-footprint memory mechanism for streaming video understanding that replaces token instances with fixed-capacity prototype summaries, improving accuracy by up to 12.5 points under long delays.
AI.motivation: Streaming video understanding faces the challenge of delayed queries where critical visual cues appear briefly but may be evicted or diluted under bounded GPU-memory and latency budgets before the query arrives.
AI.method: ProtoKV maintains an exact near-window KV cache and aggregates older content into a fixed-capacity semantic-spatial prototype bank with residual statistics, exposing prototypes via bounded pseudo-token interface compatible with standard attention.
AI.result: Under matched budgets and comparable query-time cost, ProtoKV improves accuracy by up to 12.5 points over token-retention baselines on SVU benchmarks in the long-delay regime, with gains increasing as query delay grows.
AI.conclusion: ProtoKV effectively addresses the delayed query problem in streaming video understanding by using prototype-based summarization, achieving substantial accuracy improvements under constrained memory and latency.

title: A Latent ODE Approach to Spatiotemporal Modeling of Cine Cardiac MRI
summary: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR) captures rich spatiotemporal information about ventricular structure and motion, but conventional risk models use only a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases. We present a latent dynamical model that encodes bi-ventricular anatomy and full-cycle cine motion as a continuous latent trajectory, using heart-rate-aware neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) dynamics and a graph-based mesh autoencoder to reconstruct anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion. A covariate-conditioned prior defines the expected end-diastolic latent state, and a Cox proportional hazards model tests whether deviations from this prior predict incident heart failure. We studied 72,386 UK Biobank participants without baseline cardiovascular disease, including 367 incident heart failure events. In a held-out evaluation subset, adding the latent score to refitted pooled cohort equations improved the stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785, compared with 0.764 for seven established cardiac markers. Compared with non-graph and non-ODE approaches, the proposed model gave the best trade-off between reconstruction fidelity, generative realism, and downstream prognostic performance. These results suggest that continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion provides informative cardiac phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries, while external validation in more representative patient cohorts is required before clinical risk-prediction use.

AI.tldr: A latent dynamical model using neural ODE and graph-based mesh autoencoder encodes full-cycle bi-ventricular motion from CMR to predict incident heart failure, improving C-index from 0.704 to 0.785 over conventional risk models in 72,386 UK Biobank participants.
AI.motivation: Conventional cardiac risk models rely on a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases, failing to leverage the rich spatiotemporal information about ventricular structure and motion captured by CMR.
AI.method: A latent dynamical model with heart-rate-aware neural ODE dynamics for continuous latent trajectory encoding, a graph-based mesh autoencoder for 3D+t ventricular reconstruction, a covariate-conditioned prior for end-diastolic state, and a Cox proportional hazards model for risk prediction.
AI.result: On 72,386 UK Biobank participants (367 HF events), adding the latent score improved stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785, outperforming seven established cardiac markers (0.764). The model achieved best reconstruction, generative realism, and prognosis trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion yields informative cardiac phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries, though external validation in more representative cohorts is needed before clinical use.

title: SharQ: Bridging Activation Sparsity and FP4 Quantization for LLM Inference
summary: Low-bit floating-point formats and semi-structured sparsity are increasingly supported by modern accelerators, yet combining them for LLM activation compression remains challenging: activations contain input-dependent outliers that dominate block scales in FP4 quantization, and directly applying N:M sparsity masks discards moderate values, coupling sparsification loss with quantization error. We introduce SharQ, a training-free inference method that bridges activation sparsity and FP4 quantization through an online sparse--dense decomposition. For each activation tensor, SharQ generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, quantizes it to FP4, and defines a dense residual relative to the quantized sparse backbone rather than the unquantized sparse values. A sparse FP4 GEMM processes the backbone while a dense FP4 GEMM compensates for both mask-induced activation loss and sparse-path quantization error. The two paths share a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views, and a fused preparation kernel absorbs mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization into one operator. SharQ requires no calibration data, retraining, or model-specific tuning. Evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43--63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks, and generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On an RTX 5090, SharQ delivers 2.2--2.4$\times$ latency reduction over FP16 and 1.2--1.4$\times$ throughput improvement over FP8 in language model serving, and up to 1.58$\times$ speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation when combined with SageAttention. Our code is available at https://github.com/actypedef/SharQ.

AI.tldr: SharQ is a training-free inference method that combines activation sparsity (N:M) with FP4 quantization via an online sparse-dense decomposition, recovering 43-63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap and achieving 2.2-2.4× latency reduction over FP16 on RTX 5090.
AI.motivation: Low-bit FP4 formats and N:M sparsity are hardware-supported but combining them for LLM activation compression is challenging: activations have input-dependent outliers that dominate FP4 block scales, and applying sparsity masks discards moderate values, coupling sparsification loss with quantization error.
AI.method: SharQ performs online sparse-dense decomposition: (1) generates an input-adaptive N:M mask to extract an outlier-dominated sparse backbone, (2) quantizes it to FP4, (3) defines a dense residual relative to the quantized sparse backbone (not unquantized sparse values), (4) processes the backbone via sparse FP4 GEMM and compensates residuals via dense FP4 GEMM, sharing a single FP4 weight payload with path-specific scale views, with a fused preparation kernel absorbing mask generation, residual construction, and layer normalization.
AI.result: On Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, and Qwen3-VL-8B, SharQ recovers 43-63% of the NVFP4-to-FP16 accuracy gap across language and vision-language tasks, generalizes across NVFP4, HiF4, and MXFP4 formats. On RTX 5090: 2.2-2.4× latency reduction over FP16, 1.2-1.4× throughput improvement over FP8, and up to 1.58× speedup on Wan2.2-T2V-A14B video generation with SageAttention.
AI.conclusion: SharQ is a training-free, calibration-free, retraining-free method that effectively bridges activation sparsity and FP4 quantization, achieving significant speedups while recovering most of the accuracy gap, and generalizes across multiple FP4 formats and modalities.

title: Calibrated Harmonic Overlaid Implicit Neural Representations for Multi-Dimensional Data
summary: Implicit neural representation (INR) has emerged as a powerful prior for multi-dimensional data (e.g., multispectral images and videos). However, most INR methods employing periodic activation functions (e.g., Sine) predominantly rely on function composition. This mechanism introduces optimization instability as network depth increases, thereby limiting their performance. Meanwhile, these methods fail to incorporate proper physical priors to effectively alleviate spectrum bias. To address these issues, inspired by the commonalities between deep periodic networks and generalized Fourier series, we propose a novel Calibrated Harmonic Overlaid Implicit Neural Representation (CHOIR). Specifically, we utilize Coordinated Harmonic Superposition (CHS) to replace the conventional function composition used in most INRs, thereby ensuring optimization stability when scaling network depth. Furthermore, we introduce a Perceptual Spectrum Calibration (PSC) to mitigate spectrum bias. This calibration embeds the ubiquitous power-law spectrum prior of natural images and adjusts the globally fixed spectrum towards a physically plausible log-uniform distribution. Extensive experiments on various multidimensional data recovery problems demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/chorl0229/CHOIR.

AI.tldr: CHOIR is a novel implicit neural representation method that replaces function composition with Coordinated Harmonic Superposition for stable optimization in deep networks, and introduces Perceptual Spectrum Calibration with power-law spectrum priors to mitigate spectrum bias, achieving state-of-the-art results on multidimensional data recovery.
AI.motivation: Existing INR methods with periodic activation functions suffer from optimization instability as network depth increases due to reliance on function composition, and lack proper physical priors to alleviate spectrum bias.
AI.method: (1) Coordinated Harmonic Superposition (CHS): replaces conventional function composition to ensure optimization stability when scaling network depth. (2) Perceptual Spectrum Calibration (PSC): embeds power-law spectrum prior of natural images and adjusts globally fixed spectrum toward a physically plausible log-uniform distribution.
AI.result: Superior performance over state-of-the-art approaches on various multidimensional data recovery problems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CHOIR effectively addresses optimization instability and spectrum bias in deep periodic networks, demonstrating strong results on multidimensional data recovery tasks.

title: Socratic agents for autonomous scientific discovery in high-dimensional physical systems
summary: The automation of scientific discovery has reached an inflection point. While AI systems now operate instruments, optimize parameters and generate hypotheses, most remain procedural: they execute workflows fixed by human designers. True autonomous science demands epistemic autonomy--the capacity to construct, challenge and revise physical explanations in response to evidence. Here we introduce AHOIS, a multi-agent AI scientist that embeds Socratic midwifery into closed-loop experimentation. A physics-critic agent interrogates hypotheses through causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation and falsification-criteria formulation. We evaluate AHOIS on a real multimode-fibre optical platform, a high-dimensional system with complex wave transformations, indirect detection, environmental drift and multi-modal acquisition. Without prior encoding schemes, classifiers or speckle models, the system autonomously proposed and validated a random-interference encoding hypothesis, discovered task-adaptive sparse-measurement strategies, diagnosed distinct failure modes (encoding instability, fluorescence contamination and detector noise) and translated a published imaging protocol into an executable workflow on a non-original configuration. The discovered encoding yielded 16x16 measurements with effective rank 56.9 and classification accuracies of 76.97% on MNIST and 83.17% on Fashion-MNIST. Ablations show that Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration and experimental-plan validity. These results establish a route from workflow automation towards evidence-grounded, self-correcting autonomous discovery in complex physical environments.
