AI.tldr: AHOIS is a multi-agent AI scientist with Socratic midwifery that achieves epistemic autonomy—constructing, challenging, and revising physical explanations through closed-loop experimentation—demonstrated on a real multimode-fibre optical platform, where it autonomously proposed encoding hypotheses, discovered measurement strategies, diagnosed failure modes, and translated protocols, yielding 76.97% MNIST and 83.17% Fashion-MNIST accuracy with effective rank 56.9 from 16x16 measurements.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems for scientific discovery remain procedural, executing fixed workflows designed by humans. True autonomous science requires epistemic autonomy—the ability to construct, challenge, and revise physical explanations based on evidence—which existing systems lack.
AI.method: AHOIS embeds Socratic midwifery into closed-loop experimentation using a multi-agent architecture with a physics-critic agent that interrogates hypotheses through causal questioning, constraint checking, counterexample generation, and falsification-criteria formulation. Evaluation was conducted on a real multimode-fibre optical platform with complex wave transformations, indirect detection, environmental drift, and multi-modal acquisition.
AI.result: The system autonomously proposed and validated a random-interference encoding hypothesis, discovered task-adaptive sparse-measurement strategies, diagnosed encoding instability, fluorescence contamination, and detector noise, and translated a published imaging protocol. Achieved 16x16 measurements with effective rank 56.9, 76.97% MNIST accuracy, and 83.17% Fashion-MNIST accuracy. Ablations confirmed Socratic interrogation improves physical consistency, hypothesis completeness, uncertainty calibration, and experimental-plan validity.
AI.conclusion: AHOIS establishes a viable route from workflow automation toward evidence-grounded, self-correcting autonomous scientific discovery in complex physical environments.

title: Empirical Software Engineering TerraProbe: A Layered-Oracle Framework for Detecting Deceptive Fixes in LLM-Assisted Terraform
comment: 34 pages, 12 figures, 14 tables. Journal-first manuscript submitted to Empirical Software Engineering. Primary classification: cs.SE; cross-list: cs.CR
summary: Security misconfigurations in Terraform Infrastructure-as-Code are a growing risk in cloud deployments, and large language models are increasingly used as automated repair agents. Existing evaluations often treat a repair as successful when the targeted static-analysis finding disappears, without checking planning validity, behavioral change, or security intent. This paper presents TerraProbe, a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating LLM-assisted Terraform security repair. We apply TerraProbe to 288 first-pass repairs generated by gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet across 68 real-world TerraDS modules and 28 controlled injected-defect modules. The results show that targeted Checkov removal overstates repair success. Although targeted removal reaches 83.3 percent for the primary model, full-scanner cleanliness drops to 10.4 percent, Terraform planning succeeds for 39.6 percent, and plan comparison is reachable for 38.5 percent. Human adjudication further shows that 71.4 percent of plan-compared real-world repairs are deceptive fixes that pass automated checks while leaving the underlying vulnerability in place. This pattern is statistically indistinguishable across the three models, with deceptive-fix rates from 57.1 percent to 71.4 percent and pairwise Fisher exact p-values above 0.10. The paper introduces a four-dimensional taxonomy of deceptive fixes, validated with Cohen kappa of 0.78 and Krippendorff alpha of 0.76. IAM permission analysis confirms that wildcard Resource grants persist in all nine CKV2 AWS 11 deceptive-fix cases. TerraProbe contributes an evaluation methodology, a replication package, and the Multi-Layer Oracle Evaluation framework for distinguishing intent-aligned security repairs from scanner-passing false successes.

AI.tldr: TerraProbe introduces a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating LLM-assisted Terraform security repair, revealing that over 71% of repairs that pass automated checks are deceptive fixes that leave underlying vulnerabilities intact, with this pattern consistent across Gemini, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM-based Terraform security repair treat static-analysis finding removal as success, ignoring planning validity, behavioral change, and security intent—leading to inflated and misleading repair rates.
AI.method: TerraProbe uses a five-layer oracle: (1) targeted Checkov finding removal, (2) full-scanner cleanliness, (3) Terraform planning success, (4) plan comparison with original, and (5) human adjudication. Applied to 288 repairs from three LLMs over 96 modules with a four-dimensional deceptive-fix taxonomy validated by inter-rater reliability.
AI.result: Targeted removal 83.3% → full-scanner cleanliness 10.4% → planning success 39.6% → plan comparison reachable 38.5%. Human review shows 71.4% of plan-compared repairs are deceptive. IAM wildcard Resource persists in all 9 CKV2_AWS_11 deceptive-fix cases. Deceptive-fix rates across models (57.1%-71.4%) are statistically indistinguishable (p>0.10).
AI.conclusion: TerraProbe demonstrates that single-metric evaluation of LLM security repair is unreliable; multi-layer oracle evaluation is necessary to distinguish true security fixes from deceptive fixes that pass automated checks but preserve vulnerabilities.

title: Anatomy-Guided Residual Motion Diffusion for Controllable 4D Cardiac MRI Synthesis
summary: Developing robust artificial intelligence models for 4D (3D + time) medical imaging is constrained by limited annotated data, inter-device domain shifts, and privacy restrictions. To address this, we propose a 4D controllable generative framework for anatomically consistent data augmentation. A semi-supervised variational autoencoder learns a compact latent representation of anatomical volumes while jointly predicting aligned segmentation masks in a unified framework. Anatomical structure is then disentangled from temporal dynamics through a cascaded latent diffusion model (LDM). A static LDM generates subject-specific anatomy conditioned on clinical priors (diagnosis and volumes measures) and a subsequent motion LDM estimates residual latent motions, ensuring strict temporal coherence across the 4D sequence. The proposed approach was evaluated on cine cardiac MRI as a representative 4D imaging application. Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate high controllability of static anatomy (Pearson r > 0.8) and strong temporal coherence (FVD = 288.08). In cross-vendor generalization experiments, augmenting training sets with synthetic 4D sequences significantly improves downstream segmentation performance. Using nnU-Net, the proposed augmentation strategy improves the average Dice score by 1.4% and reduces the Hausdorff Distance by 3.0mm compared to training on real data alone, for the left ventricle, Dice improves by 2.8% with a 5.4mm reduction in boundary error. Overall, this framework provides a scalable and controllable solution for 4D medical image synthesis, supporting the development of more robust models with limited annotations and cross-vendor variability. Code available on https://github.com/cyiheng/4DCardiacMRISynthesis.

AI.tldr: A 4D controllable generative framework combining a semi-supervised VAE with cascaded latent diffusion models (static LDM for anatomy + motion LDM for temporal dynamics) to synthesize anatomically consistent cardiac MRI sequences for data augmentation, improving downstream segmentation (Dice +1.4%, HD -3.0mm) across vendors.
AI.motivation: Limited annotated 4D medical imaging data, inter-device domain shifts, and privacy constraints hinder robust model development.
AI.method: Semi-supervised VAE learns compact latent representations of anatomy with joint segmentation prediction; a cascaded latent diffusion model disentangles static anatomy (conditioned on clinical priors) from residual temporal motion to generate coherent 4D sequences.
AI.result: High anatomy controllability (Pearson r > 0.8), strong temporal coherence (FVD = 288.08); cross-vendor augmentation improves nnU-Net segmentation: average Dice +1.4%, HD -3.0mm; left ventricle Dice +2.8%, boundary error -5.4mm.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a scalable, controllable solution for 4D medical image synthesis, enabling robust model development under limited annotations and cross-vendor variability.

title: Scientific discovery as meta-optimization: a combinatorial optimization case study
summary: Scientific discovery is fundamentally an optimization problem, defined by a vast "state space" of theories and experiments, and an evaluation criterion based on quality, novelty, and validity. Large language models (LLMs) have enabled automated exploration of this space, but we argue that simultaneous modification of the evaluation criteria is equally important. Here, we propose formalizing research as meta-optimization, where the optimization objective itself is also being optimized. Our key contribution is "consensus objective aggregation," where LLM-generated objective functions are combined via correlation-weighted voting, yielding a stable, self-correcting evaluation criterion that evolves as understanding deepens. We apply this framework to algorithm discovery for 3-SAT problems based on digital MemComputing machines, reducing the baseline scaling with problem size $N$ from $\sim N^{2.51}$ to $\sim N^{1.33}$ and delivering a $\sim 67\times$ speedup on the largest instances tested. As a problem-agnostic framework, we hope this approach will considerably aid scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes formalizing scientific discovery as a meta-optimization problem where both the solution space and the evaluation criteria are optimized simultaneously. The key contribution is "consensus objective aggregation," which combines LLM-generated objective functions via correlation-weighted voting to create a stable, self-correcting evaluation criterion. Applied to 3-SAT algorithm discovery using digital MemComputing, it reduces scaling from ~N^2.51 to ~N^1.33, achieving ~67× speedup on largest instances.
AI.motivation: Scientific discovery is an optimization problem over a vast space of theories and experiments, but existing LLM-based approaches only explore this space without simultaneously modifying the evaluation criteria. The authors argue that optimizing the optimization objective itself is equally important for effective scientific discovery.
AI.method: The framework formalizes research as meta-optimization, where the objective function is also optimized. The core technique is "consensus objective aggregation," which uses LLMs to generate candidate objective functions and combines them via correlation-weighted voting, producing an evolving, self-correcting evaluation criterion.
AI.result: Applied to 3-SAT algorithm discovery for digital MemComputing machines, the method reduces baseline scaling from ~N^2.51 to ~N^1.33, achieving approximately 67× speedup on the largest problem instances tested.
AI.conclusion: The proposed meta-optimization framework is problem-agnostic and is expected to significantly aid scientific discovery by enabling simultaneous optimization of both the search space and the evaluation criteria.

title: Sketched Linear Contrastive Learning: Approximation, Optimization, and Statistical Scaling
summary: Scaling laws describe how learning performance varies with model size, data size, and compute. While recent theoretical work has established scaling laws for sketched linear regression, much less is understood for contrastive representation learning. In this paper, we study a sketched linear model for contrastive learning under a paired Gaussian latent-variable setup. The learner observes only sketched views of two correlated variables and trains a bilinear contrastive score by full-batch empirical gradient descent. We analyze a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition, where we derive a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term. The cross term is controlled by the bias and variance and therefore does not affect the upper-bound scaling. Our main theorem gives an explicit scaling law with respect to sketch dimension $M$, sample size $N$, and effective optimization horizon $L_{\mathrm{eff}}γ$. Compared with standard linear-regression scaling laws, the contrastive setting must learn interactions between two views, and this changes how optimization and finite-sample noise scale with model size, data, and training time. This provides a first theoretical step toward understanding scaling behavior in contrastive learning and gives guidance for balancing model size, data, and optimization compute.

AI.tldr: This paper derives scaling laws for contrastive representation learning by analyzing a sketched linear model with paired Gaussian latent variables, showing how performance scales with sketch dimension, sample size, and optimization horizon, and revealing how learning cross-view interactions alters scaling compared to standard linear regression.
AI.motivation: While scaling laws are well understood for supervised learning (e.g., linear regression), much less is known theoretically for contrastive representation learning. This paper aims to bridge that gap by providing a theoretical scaling law for contrastive learning under a tractable generative model.
AI.method: The authors study a sketched linear model for contrastive learning under a paired Gaussian latent-variable setup. The learner observes sketched views of two correlated variables and trains a bilinear contrastive score via full-batch empirical gradient descent. They analyze a Gaussian-negative quadratic contrastive surrogate under aligned power-law spectra and a contrastive source condition, deriving a risk decomposition into irreducible risk, approximation error, GD bias, GD variance, and a cross term.
AI.result: The main theorem provides an explicit scaling law with respect to sketch dimension M, sample size N, and effective optimization horizon L_eff*γ. The cross term between bias and variance is controlled and does not affect the upper-bound scaling. Compared to standard linear-regression scaling laws, the contrastive setting must learn interactions between two views, which changes how optimization and finite-sample noise scale with model size, data, and training time.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a first theoretical step toward understanding scaling behavior in contrastive learning and offers guidance for balancing model size, data, and optimization compute in practice.

title: LearniBridge: Learnable Calibration of Feature Caching for Diffusion Models Acceleration
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have driven substantial progress in image and video generation but suffer from prohibitive computational costs. Feature caching accelerates inference by reusing intermediate representations. Existing methods rely on historical features for implementation simplicity, yet suffer from severe error accumulation at high acceleration ratios. To address this limitation, we investigate the nature of the requisite feature correction. We demonstrate that the optimal calibration update is characterized by a shared low-rank subspace across diverse prompts. Guided by this structural insight, we propose LearniBridge, a learnable calibration mechanism for feature caching that bridges multiple timesteps through lightweight LoRA updates. This mechanism enables effective calibration requiring only 3-5 training samples. Extensive experiments on image and video generation show that LearniBridge achieves up to $5.87\times$, $5.75\times$, and $4.10\times$ acceleration on FLUX, HunyuanVideo, and WAN2.1, respectively. On WAN2.1, it improves VBench by 1.28% over the previous SOTA at $4.10\times$ acceleration. Our code is available at https://github.com/Iiiiiiirene/LearniBridge.

AI.tldr: LearniBridge is a learnable calibration mechanism for feature caching in Diffusion Transformers that leverages low-rank updates (LoRA) to correct accumulated errors, achieving 4-6x acceleration on image/video generation with minimal training data (3-5 samples).
AI.motivation: Existing feature caching methods for DiTs suffer from severe error accumulation at high acceleration ratios because they naively reuse historical features without proper correction. The paper aims to address this fundamental limitation by identifying the optimal calibration structure.
AI.method: The authors first demonstrate that optimal calibration updates reside in a shared low-rank subspace across diverse prompts. Based on this insight, they propose LearniBridge, which uses lightweight LoRA updates to bridge multiple timesteps as a learnable calibration mechanism for feature caching, requiring only 3-5 training samples.
AI.result: LearniBridge achieves up to 5.87×, 5.75×, and 4.10× acceleration on FLUX, HunyuanVideo, and WAN2.1 respectively. On WAN2.1 at 4.10× acceleration, it improves VBench by 1.28% over the previous SOTA.
AI.conclusion: LearniBridge effectively addresses error accumulation in feature caching for Diffusion Transformers by exploiting the low-rank structure of optimal calibration updates, enabling significant acceleration with minimal training data.

title: EGG: An Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation
summary: High-performance GPU kernels are critical for reducing the exponentially growing computational costs of large language models (LLMs), but their development heavily relies on manual tuning by domain experts. While recent advances in LLM-based approaches show promise for automating kernel generation, they still struggle to achieve both correctness and high performance. This limitation primarily arises from the lack of domain-specific optimization guidance, hindering effective exploration of the optimization space. We propose EGG, an Expert-Guided Agent Framework for Kernel Generation, which incorporates expert optimization principles to guide LLMs' decisions. Inspired by expert workflows, we decompose kernel generation into two hierarchical stages: 1) algorithmic structure design, which establishes a high-quality computational structure foundation; 2) hardware-specific tuning, which performs targeted adjustments through parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization. This staged decomposition defines explicit optimization objectives, structuring the design space to achieve progressive refinement. To this end, a stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism is designed for inter and intra-stage context management, ensuring stable optimization trajectories. Experiments on KernelBench and real-world workloads show that EGG achieves a 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.

AI.tldr: EGG is an Expert-Guided Agent Framework that uses LLMs with domain-specific optimization principles to generate high-performance GPU kernels through two hierarchical stages (algorithmic structure design and hardware-specific tuning), achieving 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch on KernelBench.
AI.motivation: High-performance GPU kernels are critical for reducing LLM computational costs, but their development requires manual tuning by experts. Existing LLM-based approaches struggle to achieve both correctness and high performance due to lack of domain-specific optimization guidance.
AI.method: EGG decomposes kernel generation into two hierarchical stages: (1) algorithmic structure design for high-quality computational foundation, and (2) hardware-specific tuning via parallel mapping, tensor tiling, and memory optimization. A stage-aware multi-agent collaboration mechanism manages inter and intra-stage context for stable optimization trajectories.
AI.result: EGG achieves a 2.13x average speedup over PyTorch on KernelBench and real-world workloads, outperforming existing agent-based and RL-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating expert optimization principles through hierarchical staged decomposition with stage-aware multi-agent collaboration effectively addresses the correctness and performance challenges in LLM-based automatic GPU kernel generation.

title: Discovering Millions of Interpretable Features with Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a powerful tool for decomposing superposed language model representations into sparse and interpretable features. However, training SAEs is computationally expensive, and available open-source SAE models remain limited. In this work, we introduce \textbf{Qwen3-Instruct SAE}, a comprehensive suite of SAEs trained on the Qwen3 instruction-tuned model family, covering Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B, and Qwen3-8B. For Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B, we train layer-wise SAEs at three key activation sites: residual streams, MLP outputs, and attention outputs. For Qwen3-8B, we train SAEs on a subset of residual stream layers. We systematically evaluate these SAEs using both activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics, revealing distinct sparsity--fidelity trade-offs across layers and components. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of Qwen3-Instruct SAE through a refusal-steering case study, showing that selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior. Our release provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned language models

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Qwen3-Instruct SAE, a comprehensive suite of sparse autoencoders trained on the Qwen3 instruction-tuned model family (1.7B, 4B, and 8B), covering multiple activation sites (residual streams, MLP outputs, attention outputs) with systematic evaluation of sparsity-fidelity trade-offs, and demonstrates utility through a refusal-steering case study.
AI.motivation: Training SAEs is computationally expensive and available open-source SAE models remain limited, motivating the need for a comprehensive, publicly available SAE suite for instruction-tuned language models.
AI.method: Train layer-wise SAEs on Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B (residual streams, MLP outputs, attention outputs) and Qwen3-8B (subset of residual stream layers). Evaluate using activation-level reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics to analyze sparsity-fidelity trade-offs.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation reveals distinct sparsity-fidelity trade-offs across layers and components. Selected SAE features can causally steer instruction-tuned Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior.
AI.conclusion: Qwen3-Instruct SAE provides a practical resource for studying sparse representations, feature-level mechanisms, and behavioral interventions in instruction-tuned language models, with demonstrated utility in refusal steering.

title: Event-based Gaze Control System for Accurate Real-time Spin Estimation in Professional Ball Games
summary: Spin plays a crucial role in many ball sports due to its effect on the trajectory of the ball. Vision-based estimation of the ball's spin during a game with conventional cameras is challenging due to the ball's small size, high speed, and fast rotation. To address these challenges, we propose an event-based active vision system that can track unmodified balls and measure their spin in real-time. The system consists of an event camera for its high temporal resolution and minimal motion blur, high-speed pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors to keep the ball in the field of view, and a low-latency focus-tunable telephoto lens to increase the spatial resolution on the ball and keep it in focus. To track the ball, we use a hybrid approach that combines 2D event-based detection for centering and 3D positions from a ball localization system for re-initialization. For high-accuracy spin estimation, we propose an offline method that performs contrast maximization on the sphere (s-CMax). This method achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on static balls across multiple sports (table tennis, baseball, tennis, and golf), with mean magnitude and axis errors of 2.1% and 4.0 degrees, respectively. We then develop a low-latency online method for table tennis as a case study in real-time applications. This method uses an uncertainty-aware convolutional neural network trained on pseudo-ground-truth spin labels from the offline approach, combined with a GPU-accelerated batch implementation of contrast maximization for refinement. We demonstrate reliable tracking and spin estimation with a three-view setup during professional table tennis matches, with high accuracy (8.8% magnitude and 6.4 degrees axis mismatch), 3 ms latency, and 750 Hz throughput.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an event-based active vision system that uses an event camera, galvanometer mirrors, and a focus-tunable lens to track and estimate spin of fast-rotating balls in real-time. It introduces s-CMax (contrast maximization on the sphere) for offline spin estimation and an uncertainty-aware CNN with GPU-accelerated refinement for online, low-latency spin estimation, achieving 750 Hz throughput and 3 ms latency in professional table tennis matches.
AI.motivation: Spin significantly affects ball trajectory in sports, but vision-based spin estimation from conventional cameras is extremely difficult due to the ball's small size, high speed, and fast rotation, which cause motion blur and tracking challenges.
AI.method: The system uses an event camera (high temporal resolution, no motion blur), pan/tilt galvanometer mirrors (active tracking), and a focus-tunable telephoto lens (high spatial resolution). Tracking combines 2D event-based detection with 3D ball localization for re-initialization. Spin estimation uses s-CMax (contrast maximization on sphere surface) for offline, and an uncertainty-aware CNN + GPU-accelerated batch s-CMax refinement for online real-time estimation.
AI.result: Offline s-CMax achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on static balls across table tennis, baseball, tennis, and golf (2.1% magnitude error, 4.0° axis error). The online system in professional table tennis matches achieves 8.8% magnitude error, 6.4° axis error, 3 ms latency, and 750 Hz throughput with a three-view setup.
AI.conclusion: The proposed event-based active vision system enables reliable real-time spin estimation for fast-rotating balls in professional sports, bridging the gap between high-accuracy offline methods and low-latency online requirements for practical deployment.

title: ResilPhase: Plug-and-Play Phase Mapping and Noise-Resilient Macro-Trajectory Extrapolation for Diffusion Acceleration
summary: The adoption of powerful diffusion models is hindered by their significant inference latency. Recent ``cache-then-forecast'' schemes alleviate this issue by accelerating DiTs using derivative-based polynomials, but they suffer from severe quality degradation at high acceleration ratios. Our analysis reveals its root cause: the discrete extrapolation performed on representations that are misaligned with the continuous diffusion trajectory and are numerically unstable. Thus, accelerated DiTs suffer from accumulated spatial errors, noisy derivative amplification, and high-order instability. We therefore reformulate accelerated inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ordinary differential equation (ODE) space. Instead of predicting intermediate features, we align forecasting with the model's Global Drift (GD), i.e., the end-to-end state evolution, thereby eliminating feature inconsistency and memory overhead. However, even this smooth macro-trajectory remains vulnerable to the derivative fallacy: its higher-order temporal derivatives are intrinsically noisy. Thus, we introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to effectively bypass derivative instability and approximation error. We further propose a bounded Phase Mapping that regularizes the extrapolation domain, suppressing oscillatory error growth. These elements collectively constitute ResilPhase, a noise-resilient acceleration framework. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo demonstrate state-of-the-art fidelity under aggressive acceleration ratios.

AI.tldr: ResilPhase accelerates diffusion model inference by reformulating cache-then-forecast as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space, using a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator and bounded Phase Mapping to overcome numerical instability and quality degradation at high acceleration ratios.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models suffer from high inference latency; existing cache-then-forecast schemes degrade severely under aggressive acceleration due to misaligned representations, accumulated spatial errors, noisy derivative amplification, and high-order instability.
AI.method: (1) Reformulate accelerated inference as stable macro-trajectory extrapolation in ODE space by aligning forecasting with the model's Global Drift (end-to-end state evolution); (2) Introduce a derivative-free barycentric Lagrange extrapolator to bypass noisy higher-order temporal derivatives; (3) Propose a bounded Phase Mapping to regularize the extrapolation domain and suppress oscillatory error growth.
AI.result: State-of-the-art fidelity on FLUX.1-dev and HunyuanVideo under aggressive acceleration ratios.
AI.conclusion: ResilPhase demonstrates that noise-resilient acceleration of diffusion models is achievable by addressing numerical instability and derivative noise through ODE-aligned macro-trajectory extrapolation and derivative-free methods.

title: From Weights to Features: SAE-Guided Activation Regularization for LLM Continual Learning
summary: Weight-space regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) are the standard approach to catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. However, those methods tend to underperform when applied to large language models. We argue that such underperformance can be partly explained by the ``polysemantic'' nature of large language models: per-weight importance estimates utilized by EWC-style regularization are too coarse and cannot isolate the knowledge that needs protection. In this paper, we propose regularizing instead in the model's activation space, using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a monosemantic feature dictionary. From the perspective of constrained optimization, we derive a new loss function that uses the SAE feature dictionary to explicitly balance stability and plasticity, and show that EWC is a special case in the one-sided weight-space penalty setting. Unlike replay-based methods that store or revisit examples from earlier tasks, our method requires no previous-task data after mask construction: current-task data is used to compute a compact SAE feature mask, and only this mask is retained for later training. Further, since the feature space has significantly lower dimensionality than the parameter space, the proposed method is more memory efficient. On the TRACE and MedCL continual learning benchmarks, the method achieves the strongest result among approaches without introducing task-specific architectural components, also surpassing traditional weight-space regularization methods like EWC. Beyond performance comparisons, we provide empirical evidence for the polysemanticity thesis: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in the SAE feature basis but indistinguishable from chance in the weight basis, and weight-space protection is nearly non-selective at the concept level.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes replacing weight-space regularization (like EWC) with activation-space regularization via pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) for continual learning in LLMs. This addresses polysemanticity issues in weight-space methods. The approach uses SAE feature masks to balance stability and plasticity, is more memory-efficient, and outperforms EWC on TRACE and MedCL benchmarks while providing empirical evidence for the polysemanticity thesis.
AI.motivation: Weight-space regularization methods like EWC underperform on large language models due to polysemanticity—per-weight importance estimates are too coarse to isolate task-relevant knowledge. The authors aim to address this limitation.
AI.method: Instead of weight-space regularization, the method regularizes in activation space using pretrained Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as monosemantic feature dictionaries. A new loss function is derived from constrained optimization using SAE feature masks. No previous-task data is needed after mask construction; only a compact SAE feature mask is retained.
AI.result: The method achieves the strongest results among approaches without task-specific architectural components on TRACE and MedCL benchmarks, surpassing EWC and other weight-space methods. It is also more memory-efficient due to lower dimensionality of feature space vs. parameter space.
AI.conclusion: Activation-space regularization via SAEs is more effective than weight-space methods for continual learning in LLMs. The polysemanticity thesis is empirically supported: task-relevant representations are linearly separable in SAE feature space but indistinguishable from chance in weight space.

title: ReasonCLIP-58M: Visually Grounded Commonsense Reasoning Supervision for CLIP
summary: CLIP and its variants are widely adopted visual backbones in multimodal systems, but their pretraining remains dominated by descriptive image-text alignment. As downstream applications increasingly demand visually grounded commonsense inference and compositional reasoning, it remains unclear whether CLIP-style encoders can support such reasoning without architectural changes. To address this, we present ReasonCLIP-58M, a continual pretraining framework that integrates large-scale reasoning supervision into CLIP-style models through our two-stage strategy, which progressively integrates reasoning signals while preserving descriptive alignment, followed by category-structured reasoning supervision. To support this framework, we construct two complementary datasets and a benchmark: ReasonLite-42M, with open-form, visually verifiable reasoning captions; ReasonPro-16M, with category-specific reasoning supervision; and RCLIP-Bench for diagnostic evaluation of visually grounded reasoning. We train a family of ReasonCLIP that improves visually grounded commonsense and compositional reasoning while also enhancing zero-shot retrieval performance. As a drop-in visual encoder for multimodal large language models such as LLaVA-NeXT, ReasonCLIP delivers consistent gains without additional inference cost, demonstrating that structured reasoning supervision enhances the expressive capacity of CLIP-style visual representations. All datasets, models, and training code are available at https://github.com/RISys-Lab/ReasonCLIP.

AI.tldr: ReasonCLIP-58M is a continual pretraining framework that integrates large-scale reasoning supervision into CLIP-style models via a two-stage strategy, using two new datasets (ReasonLite-42M and ReasonPro-16M) and the RCLIP-Bench benchmark, improving visually grounded commonsense reasoning, compositional reasoning, and zero-shot retrieval, while also enhancing MLLMs like LLaVA-NeXT without additional inference cost.
AI.motivation: CLIP-style encoders are widely used in multimodal systems but it remains unclear whether they can support visually grounded commonsense inference and compositional reasoning without architectural changes, as downstream applications increasingly demand such reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: A two-stage continual pretraining strategy: (1) integrates reasoning signals while preserving descriptive alignment using ReasonLite-42M (open-form, visually verifiable reasoning captions); (2) adds category-structured reasoning supervision using ReasonPro-16M. Constructs RCLIP-Bench for diagnostic evaluation.
AI.result: ReasonCLIP improves visually grounded commonsense and compositional reasoning, enhances zero-shot retrieval performance, and as a drop-in visual encoder for MLLMs like LLaVA-NeXT, delivers consistent gains without additional inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Structured reasoning supervision enhances the expressive capacity of CLIP-style visual representations, demonstrating that reasoning-aware pretraining benefits both standalone reasoning tasks and downstream multimodal systems.

title: Memory Depth, Not Memory Access: Selective Parametric Consolidation for Long-Running Language Agents
comment: Main paper with supplementary material included as ancillary file
summary: Long-running language agents need more than memory access. Retrieval systems can fetch past facts at query time, but they do not decide which experiences should continue to shape behavior after the working context is unloaded. We study this separate problem as memory depth: durable goal-conditioned tendencies written into a small parametric store. We introduce the loop-drift protocol, a controlled stress test in which the retrieval index remains intact while working context is unloaded and goal-conditioned behavior must persist under long-loop interference. We evaluate EVAF, a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. Across GPT-2 and TinyLlama, retrieval is strongest on shallow factual recall (short-fact accuracy 0.956--0.973), while EVAF is strongest on goal persistence and post-unload recovery (0.812--0.904) with only 2--3 parametric writes per 200 events. Mechanism controls show that selective consolidation factorizes into two controllable dimensions: selection and actuation. Matched random gates isolate selection beyond sparse writing; fixed-inner controls across GPT-2, TinyLlama, and Mistral-7B show that inner-loop write strength is model-dependent; and a Mistral-7B matched-gate inversion reveals asymmetric selection-actuation coupling under miscalibrated actuation. Public Memora event streams serve as an external diagnostic, exposing stale-memory invalidation as an unresolved boundary. Within this probe, selective parametric consolidation supplies memory depth distinct from and complementary to retrieval access.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the loop-drift protocol to test memory depth (durable goal-conditioned behavior) in long-running language agents, and proposes EVAF, a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. EVAF achieves strong goal persistence (0.812–0.904) with minimal parametric writes (2–3 per 200 events), while retrieval excels at shallow factual recall. Mechanism controls reveal selective consolidation factorizes into selection and actuation dimensions, with model-dependent inner-loop write strength and asymmetric selection-actuation coupling. Stale-memory invalidation remains an unresolved boundary.
AI.motivation: Long-running language agents need more than memory access via retrieval; they also require memory depth—durable goal-conditioned tendencies that persist after working context is unloaded. Current retrieval systems fetch past facts but do not decide which experiences should continue shaping behavior beyond the working context.
AI.method: The paper introduces the loop-drift protocol, a controlled stress test that unloads working context while keeping the retrieval index intact, testing persistent goal-conditioned behavior under long-loop interference. EVAF is proposed as a surprise- and valence-gated LoRA consolidation mechanism. Mechanism controls include matched random gates (isolating selection beyond sparse writing), fixed-inner controls (across GPT-2, TinyLlama, Mistral-7B), and matched-gate inversion (revealing asymmetric selection-actuation coupling).
AI.result: Retrieval excels on shallow factual recall (short-fact accuracy 0.956–0.973). EVAF achieves strong goal persistence and post-unload recovery (0.812–0.904) with only 2–3 parametric writes per 200 events. Selective consolidation factorizes into selection and actuation dimensions. Inner-loop write strength is model-dependent. Miscalibrated actuation causes asymmetric selection-actuation coupling. Stale-memory invalidation is an unresolved boundary.
AI.conclusion: Selective parametric consolidation supplies memory depth that is distinct from and complementary to retrieval access. The loop-drift protocol reveals that retrieval and parametric consolidation serve different roles—factual recall vs. goal persistence—and highlights stale-memory invalidation as a remaining challenge.

title: Target-Aware Bandit Allocation for Scalable Surrogate Optimization in Chemical Space
summary: Identifying high-utility candidates from massive discrete spaces under expensive evaluations is a recurring challenge across the sciences, with structure-based drug discovery as a prominent example. While surrogate-based optimization can increase sample efficiency by reducing the number of expensive evaluations, modern molecular libraries have reached billions to trillions of compounds, making full-library surrogate inference itself a major computational bottleneck. We introduce BOBa, a bandit-guided surrogate optimization framework that eliminates full-library inference by adaptively allocating computation across partitions of the action space. By treating partitions as arms in a multi-armed bandit, BOBa concentrates inference and evaluations on empirically promising partitions while maintaining principled exploration. Experiments on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries demonstrate that optimism-under-uncertainty bandits, combined with meaningful action space partitioning, are essential for effective allocation of inference and evaluations. Our findings reveal a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost, which supports practical optimization over current libraries, and establishes a viable route to ultra-large library virtual screening.

AI.tldr: BOBa is a bandit-guided surrogate optimization framework that eliminates full-library inference by adaptively allocating computation across partitions of the action space, enabling practical optimization over billion-trillion scale molecular libraries.
AI.motivation: Structure-based drug discovery faces the challenge of identifying high-utility candidates from massive discrete spaces (billions to trillions of compounds) under expensive evaluations, where full-library surrogate inference itself becomes a major computational bottleneck.
AI.method: BOBa uses a multi-armed bandit approach where partitions of the action space are treated as arms, adaptively allocating inference and evaluations to empirically promising partitions while maintaining principled exploration via optimism-under-uncertainty.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world synthesis-on-demand libraries show that optimism-under-uncertainty bandits combined with meaningful action space partitioning are essential for effective allocation; a tunable tradeoff between screening performance and surrogate inference cost is demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: BOBa supports practical optimization over current ultra-large libraries and establishes a viable route to ultra-large library virtual screening by eliminating the need for full-library surrogate inference.

title: KARLA: Knowledge-base Augmented Retrieval for Language Models
summary: We propose a new method that allows an LLM to automatically pull in factual knowledge from a knowledge base during token generation. This means that (1)~factual knowledge in the LLM output can be updated without retraining the LLM, (2)~facts in the LLM output can be traced to the knowledge base for transparency and explainability, and (3)~smaller models can achieve the same factual accuracy as larger models. Our core idea is to train the model to produce special tokens that trigger a query to the knowledge base. Our experiments show that our method improves factual grounding in both short and long-form generation, and allows factual revisions to take effect through KB edits rather than parameter updates.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes training LLMs to generate special tokens that query a knowledge base during token generation, enabling factual updates without retraining, traceable facts, and smaller models achieving larger-model accuracy.
AI.motivation: Factual knowledge in LLMs is static after training, cannot be easily updated without retraining, lacks transparency in fact provenance, and requires large model sizes for factual accuracy.
AI.method: Train the LLM to produce special tokens that trigger queries to a knowledge base during token generation, dynamically pulling factual knowledge from the KB.
AI.result: The method improves factual grounding in both short and long-form generation; factual revisions take effect through KB edits rather than parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enables updatable, traceable, and efficient factual grounding without retraining the LLM, allowing smaller models to match larger models' factual accuracy.

title: Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence
summary: Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) model continuous-time graph dynamics by parameterizing Neural ODE velocity fields with Graph Neural Networks. Their local, size-independent filters suggest a zero-shot size-transfer principle: train on a small graph and deploy on larger, similar graphs without retraining. We develop a quantitative theory for this principle on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons. We consider Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as the infinite-node limits of the forward and adjoint GNDE systems, and establish well-posedness. For an $n$-node random graph with sparsity parameter $α_n$, we prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$, up to logarithmic factors, with high probability. We also establish uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems governing hidden-state and parameter gradients. We further study discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training. Under explicit Euler discretization with $M$ steps, we show that DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, with hidden-state and local parameter-gradient discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, respectively, up to sparsity and logarithmic factors. Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, while zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes demonstrate accurate deployment of learned GNDEs on larger independently sampled graphs.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a quantitative theory for zero-shot size-transfer in Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) on sparse random graphs. It establishes convergence rates of GNDEs to their infinite-node limits (Graphon-NDEs), proves asymptotic consistency of DTO and OTD training, and validates the theory with experiments on synthetic graphons and zero-shot transfer across graphon classes.
AI.motivation: GNDEs' local, size-independent filters suggest that models trained on small graphs could be deployed on larger similar graphs without retraining (zero-shot size-transfer). The authors aim to provide a rigorous quantitative theory for this principle, which lacked theoretical foundation.
AI.method: The authors formulate Graphon-NDEs and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits of forward and adjoint GNDE systems. They prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons, with rate O((α_n n)^{-1/2}) up to logarithmic factors. They also analyze discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training under explicit Euler discretization, proving asymptotic consistency with discrepancies of O(1/M) for hidden states and O(1/M^2) for local parameter gradients.
AI.result: Theoretical convergence rates for GNDE-to-Graphon-NDE under sparsity; uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems; asymptotic consistency of DTO and OTD training. Experimental results on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, and zero-shot transfer across four graphon classes validates practical deployment.
AI.conclusion: The theory confirms that zero-shot size-transfer for GNDEs is theoretically grounded, with convergence rates depending on graph sparsity and number of nodes. DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, supporting efficient training on small graphs for deployment on larger ones.

title: Multi-modality Image Fusion under Adverse Weather: Mask-Guided Feature Restoration and Interaction
summary: Multi-modality image fusion (MMIF) enhances scene representation by exploiting complementary cues from different modalities. Adverse weather, however, causes significant image degradation, disrupting feature representation and requiring simultaneous feature restoration and cross-modal complementarity. Existing methods often struggle with effective representation learning under such conditions, limiting their practical performance. To address these challenges, we propose a mask-guided MMIF method that integrates feature restoration and interaction. We first introduce "Pseudo Ground Truth" to simplify training, promoting faster and more effective feature learning. Then, we design a mask generation mechanism based on the mapping relationship between the fused result and the source images, quantifying the relative contribution of each modality during the fusion process. By incorporating the proposed mask-guided cross-modal cross-attention mechanism, the network is encouraged to selectively attend to informative features during modality interaction, mitigating the risk of overfitting to the static distribution of the "Pseudo Ground Truth". Additionally, we propose a mask-guided learning strategy and a task-coupled degradation-aware learning strategy to balance feature restoration and interaction. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method surpasses state-of-the-art approaches in visual quality, quantitative metrics, and downstream tasks. The source code is available at https://github.com/ixilai/AMG-Fuse.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a mask-guided multi-modality image fusion method (AMG-Fuse) that integrates feature restoration and cross-modal interaction to handle adverse weather conditions. It uses Pseudo Ground Truth for efficient training, a mask generation mechanism to quantify modality contributions, and mask-guided cross-attention to balance feature restoration and interaction, achieving SOTA performance on synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing MMIF methods struggle under adverse weather conditions because image degradation disrupts feature representation, and prior approaches fail to effectively balance simultaneous feature restoration and cross-modal complementarity learning.
AI.method: 1) Introduces "Pseudo Ground Truth" to simplify training; 2) Designs a mask generation mechanism based on the mapping relationship between fused results and source images to quantify each modality's relative contribution; 3) Proposes a mask-guided cross-modal cross-attention mechanism for selective feature attention during modality interaction; 4) Develops a mask-guided learning strategy and task-coupled degradation-aware learning strategy to balance feature restoration and interaction.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on both synthetic and real-world datasets in terms of visual quality, quantitative metrics, and downstream task performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed mask-guided approach effectively addresses the challenge of representation learning under adverse weather by jointly restoring features and modeling cross-modal complementarity, with code publicly available.

title: Computational Analysis of Heart Rate Variability in Healthy Adults
summary: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis is a key indicator of cardiac physiological state and aids in disease diagnosis. However, research on HRV parameters in healthy individuals remains limited, and no gold standard exists. This study evaluates HRV indices in 40 healthy adults (20 men, 20 women, aged 30-50) to improve HRV's clinical utility. Using computational methods for signal processing and data analysis, time, frequency, and nonlinear indices were analyzed to address five questions: (1) normality, (2) stability, (3) correlation, (4) reproducibility, and (5) consistency. Key findings: (1) Time-domain and nonlinear indices, particularly global and LF (low frequency), follow normal distributions, with gender differences noted. (2) Most indices are stable except HF (high frequency)-related ones. (3) High correlations in HF-related indices suggest redundancy, indicating only one is necessary in studies. (4) Comparisons with the Fantasia database revealed less than 10% error for most indices, except SD2 and SDNN in women (greater than 15%). (5) Time-domain and nonlinear indices show low inter-study variability, while frequency-domain indices exhibit high variability, limiting cross-study comparisons. The selected indices-ApEn and IRRR (global variability), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF)-are best suited for accurately representing HRV components and enhancing its clinical and research relevance.

AI.tldr: This study analyzed HRV indices in 40 healthy adults (20 men, 20 women, aged 30-50) across five criteria: normality, stability, correlation, reproducibility, and consistency. Key findings include normal distributions for time-domain/nonlinear indices, gender differences, redundancy in HF-related indices, acceptable reproducibility versus Fantasia database, and low inter-study variability for time-domain/nonlinear indices. Recommended indices for clinical/research use are ApEn and IRRR (global), HRVi and SD2 (LF), and MADRR or rMSSD (HF).
AI.motivation: HRV analysis is important for cardiac health assessment and disease diagnosis, but research on HRV parameters in healthy individuals is limited and no gold standard exists. This study aims to fill this gap and improve HRV's clinical utility by systematically evaluating multiple HRV indices.
AI.method: Computational methods for signal processing and data analysis were applied to analyze time-domain, frequency-domain, and nonlinear HRV indices from 40 healthy adults (20 men, 20 women, aged 30-50). Five aspects were evaluated: normality, stability, correlation, reproducibility (compared against Fantasia database), and consistency (inter-study variability).
AI.result: (1) Time-domain and nonlinear indices follow normal distributions with gender differences; (2) Most indices are stable except HF-related ones; (3) High correlations among HF-related indices suggest redundancy; (4) Less than 10% error vs. Fantasia database for most indices except SD2 and SDNN in women (>15%); (5) Time-domain and nonlinear indices have low inter-study variability, frequency-domain indices have high variability. Recommended indices: ApEn/IRRR (global), HRVi/SD2 (LF), MADRR/rMSSD (HF).
AI.conclusion: The selected indices (ApEn, IRRR, HRVi, SD2, MADRR, rMSSD) are best suited for accurately representing HRV components and enhancing its clinical and research relevance.

title: PersistentKV: Page-Aware Decode Scheduling for Long-Context LLM Serving on Commodity GPUs
summary: Autoregressive large language model (LLM) serving is increasingly limited by key-value (KV) cache movement rather than dense matrix multiplication. Modern paged-attention systems reduce KV-cache fragmentation and mature kernels such as FlashInfer provide highly optimized native-paged decode attention. However, the best single-kernel implementation is not always the best serving schedule: low-active long-context decode can under-utilize commodity GPUs, while mixed sequence lengths introduce a tension between many exact-length launches and coarse padded batches. We present PersistentKV, a native block-table decode attention engine and page-aware scheduling study for grouped-query attention (GQA). PersistentKV maps work by KV-head group, is designed to reuse K,V tiles across grouped query heads, supports native page tables, and adds a compact workqueue schedule that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. On an RTX 3060 with FP16, page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128, and identical correctness tolerance against FlashInfer, a calibrated adaptive policy selects FlashInfer for small active batches, PersistentKV sequence splitting for B1 long-context steps, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling for B8 long-context steps. With thresholds and split counts fixed on calibration traces, one held-out trace seed improves synchronized wall throughput by 1.063-1.265x on B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads and by 1.399x on a B1 bucketed trace. On the B4 bimodal boundary case, the policy avoids the PersistentKV regression by selecting FlashInfer. These results identify a concrete systems niche for adaptive page-aware decode scheduling and show that work assignment, not only attention math, is a decisive serving-system variable.

AI.tldr: PersistentKV introduces a page-aware, KV-head-group-based decode attention engine with adaptive scheduling that outperforms FlashInfer on long-context, low-active, and mixed-length workloads by up to 1.399x, while falling back to FlashInfer when it would regress.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive LLM serving is increasingly bottlenecked by KV-cache movement rather than compute. Existing paged-attention systems (e.g., FlashInfer) have highly optimized single-kernel implementations, but the best kernel is not always the best serving schedule: low-active long-context decode under-utilizes GPUs, and mixed sequence lengths create a tension between exact-length launches and coarse padded batches.
AI.method: PersistentKV is a native block-table decode attention engine that (1) maps work by KV-head group, (2) reuses K,V tiles across grouped query heads, (3) supports native page tables, and (4) adds a compact workqueue schedule that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. A calibrated adaptive policy selects between FlashInfer, PersistentKV sequence splitting, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling depending on active batch size and context length.
AI.result: On an RTX 3060 (FP16, page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128), the adaptive policy improves synchronized wall throughput by 1.063-1.265x on B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads, by 1.399x on a B1 bucketed trace, and avoids regression on the B4 bimodal boundary case by selecting FlashInfer.
AI.conclusion: Work assignment—not only attention math—is a decisive variable in LLM serving systems. Adaptive page-aware decode scheduling fills a concrete systems niche beyond what single best-kernel implementations can achieve.

title: Learning Adversarial Augmentation Policies for Robust Garlic Seedling Detection
summary: Accurate seedling detection during early growth stages is essential for timely replanting and effective crop management in precision agriculture. However, existing studies are mostly evaluated under relatively stable imaging conditions, such as UAV imagery or greenhouse environments, leaving robust detection under severe and spatially heterogeneous illumination in ground-based outdoor monitoring insufficiently explored. In addition, many illumination-robust detection methods rely on additional enhancement or feature-extraction modules, which increase inference-time overhead and are not tailored to seedling detection and downstream missing seedling localization. To address these gaps, we construct a new garlic seedling dataset captured using a ground-based monitoring platform under real outdoor field conditions with highly variable illumination. We further propose an illumination-robust seedling detection framework based on adversarial augmentation policy learning. The proposed method jointly optimizes a stochastic augmentation policy agent and an object detector, enabling the detector to learn robust representations under challenging visual conditions. A structural penalty is introduced to prevent unrealistic distortions while encouraging challenging augmentations during training. Extensive experiments show that the proposed approach achieves an AP$_{50}$ of 91.6%, improving the baseline by 0.9 percentage points and outperforming the previous best-performing method by 0.2 percentage points. For downstream missing seedling localization, it achieves 75.0% precision and a 67.0% F1-score, improving the baseline by 4.8 and 2.0 percentage points, respectively. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework for practical ground-based agricultural monitoring under complex outdoor lighting conditions without additional inference-time computational overhead.

AI.tldr: Proposes an illumination-robust garlic seedling detection framework using adversarial augmentation policy learning, achieving 91.6% AP₅₀ and improved missing seedling localization under challenging outdoor field conditions without extra inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing seedling detection methods are mostly evaluated under stable imaging conditions (UAV, greenhouse), leaving robust detection under severe and spatially heterogeneous illumination in ground-based outdoor monitoring insufficiently explored. Many illumination-robust methods rely on additional enhancement modules that increase inference-time overhead and are not tailored to seedling detection and downstream missing seedling localization.
AI.method: Constructs a garlic seedling dataset from ground-based monitoring under real outdoor variable illumination. Proposes a framework that jointly optimizes a stochastic augmentation policy agent and an object detector using adversarial augmentation policy learning, with a structural penalty to prevent unrealistic distortions while encouraging challenging augmentations during training.
AI.result: Achieves AP₅₀ of 91.6% (0.9% improvement over baseline, 0.2% over previous best). For missing seedling localization: 75.0% precision and 67.0% F1-score (improvements of 4.8 and 2.0 percentage points over baseline). No additional inference-time computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is effective for practical ground-based agricultural monitoring under complex outdoor lighting conditions without extra inference cost.

title: The Capability Frontier: Benchmarks Miss 82% of Model Performance
summary: Existing benchmarks typically report accuracy for a single model on a single run. This systematically understates real-world LLM capabilities, particularly under heterogeneous data distributions: (i) different models get different questions correct according to their specializations, and (ii) given a budget, multiple generations can be sampled and selectively retained. To quantify this gap, we introduce the Capability Frontier: a Pareto frontier over a set of models that characterizes the best achievable performance at each cost level under optimal selection across models and generations (i.e., via an oracle). Our construction corrects for two opposing biases: underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from taking maxima over noisy samples. We study 21 LLMs across 16 widely used benchmarks spanning coding, reasoning, medicine, factuality, instruction following, and agentic tasks, comparing Capability Frontier performance at matched cost to each benchmark's top-performing model. Correcting for single-model evaluation yields a 54% error rate reduction; additionally correcting for single runs yields an 82% improvement, with SOTA accuracy matched at 85% cost reduction. Complementing these empirical results, we use controlled probabilistic simulations to show that higher query topic entropy produces a near-monotonic increase in the performance gap between oracle routing and the best single model. Our findings suggest collective LLM capabilities are substantially underestimated, with implications for evaluation and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings.

AI.tldr: Existing benchmarks underestimate LLM capabilities due to single-model, single-run evaluation. The authors introduce the Capability Frontier - a Pareto frontier measuring best achievable performance across models and generations at each cost level. Testing 21 LLMs across 16 benchmarks shows 54% error reduction correcting single-model bias and 82% improvement correcting single-run bias, with SOTA accuracy matched at 85% cost reduction.
AI.motivation: Current benchmark evaluations systematically understate real-world LLM capabilities because they report accuracy for a single model on a single run, ignoring that (i) different models specialize on different question types, and (ii) under a budget, multiple generations can be sampled and selectively retained. This creates a gap between reported and achievable performance.
AI.method: The Capability Frontier is a Pareto frontier over a set of models characterizing best achievable performance at each cost level under optimal oracle selection across models and generations. It corrects for underestimation from single-model evaluation and overestimation from taking maxima over noisy samples. Controlled probabilistic simulations were also used to study the effect of query topic entropy.
AI.result: Correcting single-model evaluation yields 54% error rate reduction; additionally correcting single runs yields 82% improvement. SOTA accuracy matched at 85% cost reduction. Higher query topic entropy produces near-monotonic increase in the performance gap between oracle routing and best single model.
AI.conclusion: Collective LLM capabilities are substantially underestimated under current evaluation practices, with important implications for both evaluation methodology and deployment in data-heterogeneous, multi-domain settings.

title: Algorithmic Foundations of Deep Learning: Complexity-Theoretic Rates and a Characterization of Universal Approximation
summary:
Feedforward neural network (NN) expressivity is typically studied by emulating optimal basis-expansion schemes. While powerful, this perspective is incomplete: it primarily captures complexity through regularity, and therefore does not distinguish intuitively simple and complicated objects with comparable regularity, such as the square-root function and a typical Brownian path.
  The guiding message is that neural networks should be viewed not only as flexible basis functions, but also as models of computation. If a function is computable by a real-valued circuit over a prescribed elementary gate language, then it can be computed to comparable accuracy by an NN with explicit depth, width, and non-zero-parameter bounds controlled by the depth, width, gate count, and gate structure. Thus, neural-network complexity is not governed by regularity alone, but also by algorithmic complexity. We then show that any definable NN model satisfying a natural parallelization condition, allowing possibly multivariate non-linearities such as attention or layer normalization, is a universal approximator if and only if it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
  The scope of our theory is illustrated by deducing universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions, minimax-optimal approximation guarantees for Besov classes, logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and by showing that NNs can emulate numerical algorithms such as Newton-Raphson root finding and power iteration without architecture-specific arguments. Its precision is illustrated by shortest-path computation on $k$-vertex graphs: compiling the tropical dynamic-programming circuit yields NNs with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving in 1/ε over the generic $O(ε^{-c k^2})$ Lipschitz-approximation scale, for a constant c>0.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that neural network expressivity should be viewed through the lens of algorithmic computation (circuit complexity) rather than solely regularity/smoothness, showing that NNs can emulate any function computable by a real-valued circuit over a gate language, with bounds controlled by circuit structure, and that universal approximation holds under a parallelization condition iff a non-affine nonlinearity is present. The theory is demonstrated on continuous functions, Besov classes, holomorphic functions, numerical algorithms, and shortest-path graphs, where the NN approach achieves O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters versus O(ε^{-c k^2}) Lipschitz-based scaling.
AI.motivation: Standard NN expressivity theory based on regularity (e.g., Besov, Sobolev classes) fails to distinguish intuitively simple vs. complicated objects with comparable regularity, such as the square-root function vs. a Brownian path. The authors argue for a computational lens to capture algorithmic complexity.
AI.method: The paper models NNs as real-valued circuits over a prescribed elementary gate language. It proves that any function computable by such a circuit can be approximated by an NN with explicit depth, width, and parameter bounds derived from the circuit's structure. It also proves that any definable NN model satisfying a parallelization condition is a universal approximator iff it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
AI.result: The theory yields universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions, minimax-optimal approximation for Besov classes, logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and emulation of numerical algorithms (Newton-Raphson, power iteration) without architecture-specific arguments. For shortest-path on k-vertex graphs, the NN achieves O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving over the O(ε^{-c k^2}) Lipschitz-approximation scale.
AI.conclusion: NN complexity is governed not only by regularity but also by algorithmic (circuit) complexity, providing a richer and more precise framework for understanding expressivity and approximation guarantees across diverse function classes and computational tasks.

title: Identifying the Unknown: Prompt-Free Open Vocabulary Anomaly Recognition for Robot-Object Interaction
comment: International Conference on Artificial Neural Networks 2026
summary: Robots operating in real-world environments must in general be able to recognize previously unseen objects. As robotic systems move toward open-world autonomy, there is a growing, yet largely unmet, need for open vocabulary object detectors that are prompt-free and efficient enough for continuous deployment. We present AnomNOVIC, a two-stage known-workspace framework that combines a masked autoencoder (MAE) trained for anomaly detection, with NOVIC, a powerful real-time prompt-free open vocabulary image classifier. The MAE produces generic object-agnostic bounding boxes, allowing NOVIC to classify salient image regions without requiring a predefined candidate class list. We evaluate AnomNOVIC against strong open vocabulary baselines in a tabletop robot-object environment featuring the NICOL humanoid robot, reaching 47.1% AP / 57.5% AP50 for prompt-free recognition, and 59.0% AP / 72.5% AP50 if class candidates are provided. Across additional datasets, including an in-the-wild test set with 48 unique objects, AnomNOVIC reaches up to 82.6% prompt-free detection and classification accuracy. These results significantly surpass all tested open vocabulary baselines, including YOLO-World-v2, OWLv2, and YOLOE.

AI.tldr: AnomNOVIC is a two-stage, prompt-free open vocabulary object detector combining a masked autoencoder for anomaly detection (bounding box proposal) with NOVIC (real-time open vocabulary classifier). It achieves state-of-the-art results on tabletop robot environments (47.1% AP / 57.5% AP50 prompt-free; 59.0% AP / 72.5% AP50 with class candidates) and up to 82.6% accuracy on in-the-wild data, outperforming YOLO-World-v2, OWLv2, and YOLOE.
AI.motivation: Robots operating in open-world environments need to recognize novel objects without predefined class lists. Existing open vocabulary detectors require prompts (text queries) or are computationally inefficient for continuous deployment. This work addresses the unmet need for prompt-free, efficient, real-time open vocabulary object detection in robotics.
AI.method: Two-stage known-workspace framework: (1) a masked autoencoder (MAE) trained for anomaly detection generates generic, object-agnostic bounding boxes; (2) NOVIC, a real-time prompt-free open vocabulary image classifier, classifies each salient region without requiring a predefined candidate class list.
AI.result: In a tabletop robot environment (NICOL humanoid robot): 47.1% AP / 57.5% AP50 prompt-free, 59.0% AP / 72.5% AP50 with class candidates. On in-the-wild dataset (48 unique objects): up to 82.6% prompt-free accuracy. Outperforms YOLO-World-v2, OWLv2, and YOLOE.
AI.conclusion: AnomNOVIC significantly advances open vocabulary object detection for robotics by removing the need for text prompts while maintaining real-time performance. It sets a new state-of-the-art for prompt-free open vocabulary detection across both controlled and in-the-wild settings.

title: HyperDFlash: MHC-Aligned Block Speculative Decoding with Gated Residual Reduction
summary: We present HyperDFlash, a block-parallel speculative decoding framework tailored to the novel multi-hyper-connection (MHC) architecture proposed by DeepSeek-V4. Despite the strong initial-token drafting performance of the native Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) module in DeepSeek-V4, its draft accuracy degrades sharply at later positions, as error accumulation from unverified intermediate tokens harms acceptance rates. Although the original DFlash method supports efficient one-pass block drafting, it cannot be seamlessly adapted to the MHC paradigm, since the multi-path residual stream of DeepSeek-V4 induces feature misalignment with conventional drafting designs. To resolve this mismatch, we propose two model-aligned optimizations for MHC residual streams. First, we adopt pre-collapse residual states as the exclusive conditioning signal, preserving multi-path structural information and aligning the drafter with the native prediction pathway of the target model. Second, we replace the heavy generic linear compressor with a lightweight gated residual reducer, whose parameters are inherited from the built-in hyper-connection head. This design yields input-aware path aggregation with three orders of magnitude fewer parameters while maintaining architectural alignment. We further enhance training via a targeted KL distillation loss applied to the LM-head, which regularizes predictions against the full target probability distribution and improves draft quality at early training stages. Experiments across math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational benchmarks show that HyperDFlash consistently outperforms both the native MTP baseline and vanilla DFlash adaptation. It achieves substantial gains in average accepted draft length and decoding speedup, validating the effectiveness of MHC alignment, gated reduction, and targeted distillation for high-performance speculative decoding.

AI.tldr: HyperDFlash is a block-parallel speculative decoding framework for DeepSeek-V4's multi-hyper-connection architecture, using pre-collapse residual states, a gated residual reducer, and targeted KL distillation to improve draft accuracy and decoding speed over native MTP and vanilla DFlash baselines.
AI.motivation: DeepSeek-V4's native MTP module suffers from error accumulation and degraded draft accuracy at later positions; the original DFlash method cannot be adapted to the MHC architecture due to multi-path residual stream feature misalignment.
AI.method: (1) Pre-collapse residual states as conditioning signals for multi-path alignment; (2) Gated residual reducer (inherited from hyper-connection head) replacing heavy linear compressor for lightweight path aggregation; (3) Targeted KL distillation loss on LM-head for regularization.
AI.result: Outperforms native MTP and vanilla DFlash on math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational benchmarks; achieves gains in average accepted draft length and decoding speedup.
AI.conclusion: MHC alignment, gated reduction, and targeted distillation are effective for high-performance speculative decoding in DeepSeek-V4.

title: Liquid Fusion of Heterogeneous Representations Towards General Salient Object Detection
summary: General Salient Object Detection (SOD) aims to identify and segment visually interesting objects from uni-modality or multi-modality scenes, recently advanced by cutting-edge State Space Models (SSMs). However, a critical limitation of current approaches is their neglect of the inherent spectral biases exhibited by different neural network paradigms. By digging to the dataset-level spectral analysis of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and SSMs, their semantic representations are inherently complementary based on their complementary frequency preferences. Inspired by this, we harmonize heterogeneous representations from SSMs and CNNs to bridge their spectral biases for general salient object detection. To this end, inspired by the dynamic information propagation of Liquid Neural Networks (LNNs), we introduce a liquid fusion to dynamically integrates features from two backbones, including VMamba and ConvNeXt, referred to Liquid Fusion Network (LFNet). Concretely, by treating the continuous VMamba features and ConvNeXt features as evolving states and exogenous stimulus, respectively, LFNet employs a dynamic gating mechanism for content-aware feature aggregation. Crucially, this state-stimulus paradigm enables to scale to multi-modal cues, resulting in flexibility in general SOD. Besides, a Saliency-Guided Upsampling (SGU) operator to propagate the features to the shallow layer, which leverages a spectral-spatial co-design to suppress upsampling artifacts while preserving semantics. Extensive experiments across five diverse tasks (RGB, RGB-D, RGB-T, VSOD, and VDT) demonstrate that LFNet achieves state-of-the-art performance, offering a superior trade-off between detection accuracy and model efficiency. Code has been released at https://github.com/cke520/LFNet.

AI.tldr: LFNet harmonizes heterogeneous representations from SSMs (VMamba) and CNNs (ConvNeXt) using a liquid fusion mechanism inspired by Liquid Neural Networks, along with a Saliency-Guided Upsampling operator, achieving state-of-the-art performance across five general SOD tasks (RGB, RGB-D, RGB-T, VSOD, VDT).
AI.motivation: Current SSM-based SOD methods neglect the inherent spectral biases of different neural network paradigms (CNNs vs SSMs). By analyzing dataset-level spectral properties, the authors identify that CNNs and SSMs have complementary frequency preferences, motivating the need to harmonize their heterogeneous representations for general SOD.
AI.method: LFNet integrates two backbones: VMamba (SSM) and ConvNeXt (CNN). A liquid fusion mechanism, inspired by Liquid Neural Networks, treats continuous VMamba features as evolving states and ConvNeXt features as exogenous stimulus, using a dynamic gating mechanism for content-aware feature aggregation. A Saliency-Guided Upsampling (SGU) operator leverages spectral-spatial co-design to propagate features to shallow layers while suppressing artifacts.
