AI.method: BINEVAL uses a meta-prompt to generate fine-grained atomic binary questions from evaluation criteria. An LLM answers these questions independently per output, yielding transparent question-level feedback and calibrated multi-dimensional scores aggregated from binary verdicts.
AI.result: Matches or outperforms strong baselines (UniEval, G-Eval) on SummEval, Topical-Chat, QAGS; achieves especially strong results on factual consistency (QAGS); better matches human score distributions and avoids ceiling effects; supports iterative prompt optimization under self-update and cross-model settings.
AI.conclusion: BINEVAL is a task-agnostic, training-free, interpretable evaluation framework combining strong empirical correlation with human judgments, practical diagnostic transparency, and prompt optimization utility.

title: Heavy-Ball Q-Learning with Residual Weighting Correction
summary: This paper proposes a corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method for reinforcement learning (RL) and establishes its convergence. It also identifies conditions under which the method is theoretically guaranteed to converge faster than standard Q-learning. The same construction is then extended to Q-learning with linear function approximation, where analogous convergence and acceleration statements are derived. The analysis is based on a switched linear system (SLS) representation of Q-learning algorithms and on the joint spectral radius (JSR) of the associated switching families. This SLS viewpoint is not commonly used in standard analyses of Q-learning, and it provides a complementary framework and new insight into how heavy-ball momentum can accelerate Q-learning.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method with proven convergence and acceleration over standard Q-learning, extended to linear function approximation, analyzed via switched linear system and joint spectral radius frameworks.
AI.motivation: To improve convergence speed of Q-learning by incorporating heavy-ball momentum while ensuring theoretical guarantees, and to provide a complementary analytical framework beyond traditional stochastic approximation methods.
AI.method: Corrected heavy-ball momentum is integrated into Q-learning (tabular and with linear function approximation). Convergence analysis uses a switched linear system (SLS) representation and joint spectral radius (JSR) of switching families to characterize the dynamics and acceleration conditions.
AI.result: The corrected heavy-ball Q-learning converges under identified conditions, and provably accelerates faster than standard Q-learning. These results extend to the linear function approximation setting with analogous guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The SLS/JSR analytical framework offers new insights into how heavy-ball momentum accelerates Q-learning, serving as a complementary tool to conventional analysis methods.

title: PanoImager: Geometry-Guided Novel View Synthesis and Reconstruction from Sparse Panoramic Views
summary: Panoramic sensing offers wide field-of-view coverage, yet 3D reconstruction from sparse panoramas remains challenging under rotation-dominant, weak-parallax motion. In such regimes, SfM/SLAM initialization is often ill-conditioned and unreliable. We present PanoImager, an SfM-free framework that combines feed-forward pose/depth priors, geometry-conditioned diffusion view completion, and depth-guided 3DGS optimization. Given only a few panoramic images, PanoImager decomposes them into local perspective views, synthesizes auxiliary observations to enrich sparse evidence, and stabilizes Gaussian optimization for improved cross-view consistency. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show improved stability under extreme sparsity, suggesting PanoImager as an offline/background component for map refinement when SfM/SLAM fails to initialize.

AI.tldr: PanoImager is an SfM-free framework for 3D reconstruction from sparse panoramic images under rotation-dominant, weak-parallax motion. It uses feed-forward pose/depth priors, diffusion-based view completion, and depth-guided 3DGS optimization to handle ill-conditioned SfM/SLAM initialization.
AI.motivation: Panoramic sensing provides wide FoV, but 3D reconstruction from sparse panoramas under rotation-dominant, weak-parallax motion is challenging because SfM/SLAM initialization becomes ill-conditioned and unreliable.
AI.method: PanoImager decomposes panoramic images into local perspective views, synthesizes auxiliary observations via geometry-conditioned diffusion view completion, and stabilizes Gaussian optimization with depth guidance for improved cross-view consistency.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show improved stability under extreme sparsity.
AI.conclusion: PanoImager serves as an effective offline/background component for map refinement when SfM/SLAM fails to initialize under rotation-dominant, weak-parallax conditions.

title: EO-WM: A Physically Informed World Model for Probabilistic Earth Observation Forecasting
summary: Earth Observation (EO) forecasting aims to predict future Earth surface dynamics from satellite observations under changing meteorological conditions. In this paper, we view this task as a partially observed, weather-driven world modeling problem, in which weather acts as a conditioning signal, while forecasting remains uncertain due to sparse observations and unobserved land-surface states. However, existing methods do not fully capture this setting: deterministic models collapse uncertainty into a single future prediction, while diffusion-based methods typically treat weather variables as undifferentiated conditioning signals, and existing benchmarks focus mainly on reconstruction accuracy rather than whether forecasts respond correctly to changed weather forcing.We introduce EO-WM, a video diffusion transformer for multispectral EO forecasting. EO-WM incorporates a physically informed conditioning framework that represents meteorological forcing through a climatological baseline, weather anomalies, and cumulative physical stress signals. Specifically, it separates baseline and anomaly through distinct conditioning pathways, and accumulates anomalous forcing over time to capture sustained heat and drought stress. To evaluate weather-response behavior beyond standard metrics, we introduce two diagnostic benchmarks: an Extreme Summer Benchmark for severity-aware prediction of vegetation degradation under extreme weather, and a Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark for testing response fidelity under changed weather forcing. Experiments show that EO-WM reduces the error in predicted Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) decline amplitude by a relative 5.63% and improves directional hit rate by a relative 7.80%, while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics. The benchmarks and model will be made open-source at https://github.com/Luo-Z13/EO-WM.

AI.tldr: EO-WM is a video diffusion transformer for Earth Observation forecasting that uses physically informed conditioning (climatological baseline, weather anomalies, cumulative stress signals) to improve weather-response behavior, demonstrated by reduced NDVI decline error and improved hit rate on two new diagnostic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing EO forecasting methods fail to fully capture the partially observed, weather-driven world modeling problem: deterministic models collapse uncertainty, diffusion models treat weather as undifferentiated signals, and benchmarks focus on reconstruction accuracy rather than correct weather-response behavior.
AI.method: Proposes EO-WM, a video diffusion transformer with physically informed conditioning that separates climatological baseline from weather anomalies via distinct pathways and accumulates anomalous forcing over time to capture sustained heat/drought stress. Also introduces two diagnostic benchmarks: Extreme Summer Benchmark and Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark.
AI.result: EO-WM reduces NDVI decline amplitude error by 5.63% and improves directional hit rate by 7.80% relative to baselines, while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics.
AI.conclusion: Physically informed conditioning in diffusion models improves weather-response fidelity in EO forecasting; new benchmarks enable evaluation beyond standard reconstruction accuracy.

title: Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network with Inverse Propensity Score under Unobserved Confounding
summary: Uplift modeling, crucial for estimating individual treatment effects (ITE), faces dual challenges: flexibly leveraging inter-group similarity to enhance discriminative power and debiasing under unobserved confounding scenarios. In this paper, we propose the Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network (CHAUN) and Robust Adversarial Inverse Propensity Score (RA-IPS) method to address these limitations. CHAUN employs shared feature embeddings and cross-head attention mechanisms to dynamically integrate treatment-specific and control-specific representations, enhancing inter-group correlation modeling. Theoretically, we prove that access to the true propensity scores ensures ITE identifiability even with unobserved confounders. For practical scenarios lacking true propensity scores, RA-IPS adversarially optimizes propensity weights within constrained uncertainty sets to mitigate bias from unobserved variables. Experiments on public datasets (CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA) and a production e-commerce dataset demonstrate CHAUN's superiority over state-of-the-art uplift models, achieving relative improvements of up to 25.6% in QINI scores. RA-IPS further enhances robustness, outperforming standard IPS by 5.4% under unobserved confounding. The results validate the effectiveness of our proposed methods in real-world causal inference tasks.

AI.tldr: Proposes CHAUN (Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network) for inter-group correlation modeling and RA-IPS (Robust Adversarial Inverse Propensity Score) for debiasing under unobserved confounding, achieving up to 25.6% QINI improvement on real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Uplift modeling faces two key challenges: (1) flexibly leveraging inter-group similarity to improve discriminative power, and (2) debiasing under unobserved confounding scenarios where standard methods fail.
AI.method: CHAUN uses shared feature embeddings with cross-head attention to dynamically integrate treatment/control representations. RA-IPS adversarially optimizes propensity weights within constrained uncertainty sets to mitigate unobserved confounding bias. Theoretically proves ITE identifiability given true propensity scores.
AI.result: CHAUN outperforms SOTA uplift models with up to 25.6% relative QINI improvement on CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA, and a production e-commerce dataset. RA-IPS improves robustness by 5.4% over standard IPS under unobserved confounding.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods effectively address both inter-group similarity modeling and unobserved confounding in uplift estimation, validated on multiple real-world causal inference tasks.

title: Pseudo-Text-Conditioned 3D Grounding DINO for Organ Localization in Abdominal CT
summary: Reliable organ localization in abdominal CT can provide spatial priors for downstream trauma analysis. We propose CT-3GDINO, a lightweight 3D detector that adapts a Grounding-DINO-style query-based architecture to fixed organ localization using frozen pseudo-text class tokens instead of a real text encoder. The model combines a Swin3D visual backbone, bidirectional feature enhancement, pseudo-text-guided query selection, and a cross-modality decoder to predict normalized 3D boxes for liver, spleen, left kidney, right kidney, and bowel. We train and evaluate on 193 matched RSNA/RATIC CT volumes with segmentation-derived boxes. The best multi-scale model, trained from scratch, achieves 0.5830 overall top-1 class-wise mAP over 3D IoU thresholds from 0.1 to 0.7, outperforming fixed- and trainable-backbone classification-pretrained variants with 0.5570 and 0.4657 mAP. Performance is strong for coarse localization, with 0.9649 AP at IoU 0.1, but remains limited for strict box alignment, with 0.1552 AP at IoU 0.7. These results establish CT-3GDINO as an open-source baseline for pseudo-text-conditioned 3D organ localization and motivate future work on localization-aware pretraining, richer multimodal conditioning, and injury-focused detection.

AI.tldr: CT-3GDINO is a lightweight 3D detector for abdominal organ localization that adapts Grounding-DINO-style query-based architecture using frozen pseudo-text class tokens. Trained on 193 CT volumes, it achieves 0.5830 mAP, with strong coarse localization (0.9649 AP at IoU 0.1) but limited fine alignment (0.1552 AP at IoU 0.7), establishing a baseline for pseudo-text-conditioned 3D organ detection.
AI.motivation: Reliable organ localization in abdominal CT can provide spatial priors for downstream trauma analysis, motivating the need for an effective 3D detection approach.
AI.method: Proposes CT-3GDINO, which adapts Grounding-DINO-style query-based architecture to 3D by using frozen pseudo-text class tokens (instead of a real text encoder), a Swin3D visual backbone, bidirectional feature enhancement, pseudo-text-guided query selection, and a cross-modality decoder to predict normalized 3D boxes for five abdominal organs.
AI.result: Best multi-scale model achieves 0.5830 overall top-1 class-wise mAP over 3D IoU thresholds 0.1-0.7, outperforming fixed- and trainable-backbone classification-pretrained variants (0.5570 and 0.4657 mAP). Strong coarse localization (0.9649 AP at IoU 0.1) but limited fine box alignment (0.1552 AP at IoU 0.7).
AI.conclusion: CT-3GDINO establishes an open-source baseline for pseudo-text-conditioned 3D organ localization and motivates future work on localization-aware pretraining, richer multimodal conditioning, and injury-focused detection.

title: Simulation-based inference for rapid Bayesian parameter estimation in epidemiological models: a comparison with MCMC
summary: Mechanistic epidemiological models are widely used to support infectious disease forecasting and public-health decision making. Bayesian calibration of such models is commonly performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), which can become computationally expensive for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and repeated near-real-time analyses. Here, we investigate simulation-based inference (SBI) using neural posterior estimation as a scalable alternative for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 intensive care unit (ICU) occupancy data from Germany during 2020. We compared SBI and MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using both 31-day inference windows and a substantially more challenging 201-day reconstruction problem involving multiple transmission change points. Posterior agreement was evaluated quantitatively using Wasserstein distances and Kullback-Leibler divergences together with posterior predictive checks. Across the 31-day windows, SBI recovered posterior distributions in strong agreement with MCMC while accurately reproducing observed ICU trajectories. In the 201-day setting, SBI preserved the dominant posterior structure despite increased uncertainty. SBI, by combining CPU and GPU resources, substantially reduced computational runtime compared with MCMC, which was restricted to running on CPUs. Whereas MCMC required approximately 1000 seconds for the 31-day inference problems, SBI achieved comparable posterior and predictive performance in approximately 60-70 seconds on a single GPU. For the 201-day inference problem, SBI required an average of 157 seconds, while the MCMC runs took over 19,000 seconds. Our results demonstrate that SBI provides a rapid and computationally efficient framework for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, supporting repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper compares simulation-based inference (SBI) using neural posterior estimation against traditional MCMC for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 ICU data from Germany. SBI achieves comparable posterior accuracy to MCMC while reducing computational runtime from ~1000s to ~60-70s for 31-day windows and from ~19,000s to ~157s for a 201-day reconstruction problem.
AI.motivation: Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models using MCMC is computationally expensive, especially for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and repeated near-real-time analyses. There is a need for a scalable, faster alternative that maintains calibration accuracy.
AI.method: The authors applied simulation-based inference (SBI) with neural posterior estimation to calibrate a SECIR model using COVID-19 ICU occupancy data from Germany. They compared SBI against MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using 31-day inference windows and a 201-day reconstruction problem with multiple transmission change points. Posterior agreement was evaluated via Wasserstein distances, KL divergences, and posterior predictive checks.
AI.result: SBI recovered posterior distributions in strong agreement with MCMC for 31-day windows and preserved dominant posterior structure for the 201-day setting. SBI reduced runtime from ~1000s (MCMC) to ~60-70s (SBI) for 31-day windows, and from ~19,000s (MCMC) to ~157s (SBI) for the 201-day problem.
AI.conclusion: SBI provides a rapid and computationally efficient framework for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, enabling repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis without sacrificing posterior accuracy.

title: Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) for aerodynamic prediction: a comparison with MLPs and GNNs
summary: Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) have recently been introduced as a (deep) neural network architecture whose trainable parameters adapt the activation functions, instead of the coefficients of the affine transformations at the core of traditional architectures such as deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs). This architecture builds on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem, which endows it with universal approximation properties. While the advent of KANs has been received with excitement, there is a current debate about the possible KAN supremacy over deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) for classic fields such as symbolic regression, generic-purpose machine learning, natural language processing or computer vision. Here we assess the performance of KANs --and its nuanced comparison against MLPs and graph neural networks (GNNs)-- in the realm of fluid dynamics surrogate modelling. To that aim, we consider the task of predicting the surface pressure distribution over subsonic and transonic airfoils, a canonical task in aerodynamics. Our results show that KAN models show good performance in predicting the whole pressure coefficients and is able to interpolate across Mach numbers and angles of attack, however its performance is comparable --marginally inferior-- to a suitably trained MLP, where best performance is achieved by a GNN at the expense or requiring lengthier training. While the optimal KAN model have typically much lower complexity than MLP and GNN --hence resulting in faster training--, we find that KANs suffer from training instabilities, and their performance is highly dependent on a proper hyperparameter optimisation.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) for fluid dynamics surrogate modeling (airfoil pressure prediction) and finds they perform comparably but marginally inferior to MLPs, with GNNs performing best. KANs have lower complexity but suffer from training instabilities and hyperparameter sensitivity.
AI.motivation: To assess whether KANs, a novel neural network architecture with universal approximation properties, can outperform or match traditional MLPs and GNNs in fluid dynamics surrogate modeling, specifically for predicting surface pressure distribution over airfoils.
AI.method: The authors compare KAN, MLP, and GNN models on the task of predicting surface pressure coefficients over subsonic and transonic airfoils, evaluating interpolation across Mach numbers and angles of attack.
AI.result: KANs show good performance comparable to MLPs (marginally inferior) but worse than GNNs. KANs have lower model complexity and faster training but suffer from training instabilities and high sensitivity to hyperparameter optimization.
AI.conclusion: KANs do not demonstrate supremacy over MLPs in this domain; they offer some efficiency advantages but are hindered by instability issues. GNNs achieve the best predictive performance at the cost of longer training.

title: SubdivAR: Autoregressive Next-Scale Prediction for Neural Mesh Subdivision
summary: Mesh subdivision is a fundamental operation for converting coarse, editable meshes into high-resolution surfaces, with broad applications in digital asset creation. Classical rule-based schemes rely on fixed local refinement rules and often produce over-smoothed surfaces. Recent neural subdivision methods improve detail synthesis, but remain constrained by local modeling and exhibit limited generalizability. We present SubdivAR, a neural mesh subdivision framework based on our proposed Mesh Autoregressive Representation (MAR). MAR arranges meshes at different subdivision levels into an ordered scale sequence, reformulating subdivision as autoregressive next-scale prediction. To support this formulation, we introduce a Hybrid Topology-Aware Transformer that combines global semantic attention with topology-constrained local feature aggregation. SubdivAR adopts a next-scale coordinate prediction paradigm, regressing vertex offsets at each refinement stage to preserve subdivision topology while recovering fine-grained geometric details. To enable reliable learning, we construct FII-40K, a curated dataset of nearly 40,000 high-quality meshes with multi-level subdivision supervision. Experiments show that SubdivAR outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, reducing Hausdorff Distance and Chamfer Distance by 18.8% and 14.2%, respectively, and demonstrates strong robustness on complex open-surface geometries.

AI.tldr: SubdivAR proposes a neural mesh subdivision framework using Mesh Autoregressive Representation (MAR), which reformulates subdivision as autoregressive next-scale prediction via a Hybrid Topology-Aware Transformer, achieving state-of-the-art surface quality with 18.8% and 14.2% reduction in Hausdorff and Chamfer distances on the curated FII-40K dataset.
AI.motivation: Classical rule-based subdivision schemes produce over-smoothed surfaces, while existing neural methods are constrained by local modeling and limited generalizability. The authors aim to overcome these limitations by reformulating subdivision as a global, autoregressive prediction problem.
AI.method: SubdivAR introduces Mesh Autoregressive Representation (MAR) to arrange meshes across subdivision levels into an ordered scale sequence, enabling autoregressive next-scale prediction. A Hybrid Topology-Aware Transformer is designed, combining global semantic attention with topology-constrained local feature aggregation. The framework predicts vertex offsets at each refinement stage to preserve subdivision topology while recovering fine details. A curated dataset FII-40K (~40K high-quality meshes with multi-level supervision) is constructed for training.
AI.result: SubdivAR outperforms state-of-the-art baselines with 18.8% reduction in Hausdorff Distance and 14.2% reduction in Chamfer Distance. It demonstrates strong robustness on complex open-surface geometries.
AI.conclusion: The autoregressive next-scale prediction paradigm combined with topology-aware attention effectively addresses limitations of previous rule-based and neural subdivision methods, achieving superior surface detail recovery and generalization.

title: Prompt Injection in Automated Résumé Screening with Large Language Models: Single and Multi-Injection Settings
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to screen and rank job applicants, creating incentives for candidates to strategically manipulate algorithmic hiring systems. We study prompt injection in automated résumé screening, defined as subtle self-promotional text that introduces no new qualifications but is designed to influence LLM evaluations. Using controlled experiments, we show that prompt injection reliably improves applicant rankings when résumé quality is homogeneous and few candidates inject. However, its effectiveness rapidly diminishes as more candidates inject, collapsing when manipulation becomes widespread. When candidate quality is heterogeneous, prompt injection is less effective on average, but can occasionally allow lower-quality candidates to outrank higher-quality ones, raising fairness concerns. Overall, LLM-based screening is most vulnerable when manipulation is rare and candidate quality differences are small. Code and resources are publicly available at: https://github.com/preetb1199/Prompt_Injection_ACL26

AI.tldr: This paper investigates prompt injection in LLM-based résumé screening, showing it works when manipulation is rare and candidate quality is homogeneous, but collapses under widespread use and raises fairness concerns when quality is heterogeneous.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for automated job applicant screening, candidates may strategically manipulate these systems. The paper aims to understand the effectiveness and fairness implications of prompt injection in résumé evaluation.
AI.method: Controlled experiments studying prompt injection (subtle self-promotional text designed to influence LLM evaluations) under varying conditions of résumé quality homogeneity and prevalence of manipulation among candidates.
AI.result: Prompt injection reliably improves rankings when résumé quality is homogeneous and few candidates inject, but effectiveness collapses as more candidates adopt the strategy. Under heterogeneous quality, injection is less effective on average but can let lower-quality candidates outrank higher-quality ones.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based screening is most vulnerable when manipulation is rare and candidate quality differences are small. The findings highlight fairness concerns in algorithmic hiring systems.

title: fTNN: a tensor neural network for fractional PDEs
summary: We develop the fTNN, a deterministic tensor neural network subspace method for problems involving the fractional Laplacian on bounded domains, taking the fractional Poisson equation and time-dependent fractional advection-diffusion equation as typical representatives. The work employs a geometry-adapted integration split featuring a spatially dependent near-field radius, which decomposes the fractional Laplacian into three contributions: a singular near field, a regular interior far field, and an analytical exterior far field. Then the singular radial integrals are treated by Gauss-Jacobi quadrature, the regular radial integrals by Gauss quadrature, and the angular variables by deterministic angular quadrature, yielding a fully deterministic integration framework of the fractional Laplacian operator. To accurately resolve low-regularity solutions and the associated loss functional, we construct boundary-singularity-aware trial functions enriched with explicit boundary features, and propose two strategies for automatically selecting the leading exponent and evaluating the loss function from the singularity structure induced by the fractional operator, or jointly by the fractional operator and the source term. For time-dependent fractional PDEs, we design a spatiotemporally separable neural network that factorizes the time-space residual into a sum of low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals, and we integrate this representation with an alternating neural network subspace optimization strategy for efficient training. Numerical experiments show that the proposed framework attains high accuracy on the tested benchmarks and improves substantially over existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces fTNN, a deterministic tensor neural network subspace method for fractional Laplacian problems on bounded domains, featuring geometry-adapted integration, boundary-singularity-aware trial functions, and spatiotemporally separable networks for time-dependent problems, achieving high accuracy and outperforming fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines.
AI.motivation: To develop an accurate and efficient deterministic method for solving fractional PDEs (fractional Poisson and advection-diffusion equations) on bounded domains, overcoming limitations of existing methods like fPINN and Monte Carlo approaches, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.
AI.method: The fTNN method employs: (1) a geometry-adapted integration split with spatially dependent near-field radius decomposing the fractional Laplacian into singular near field, regular interior far field, and analytical exterior far field; (2) Gauss-Jacobi quadrature for singular radial integrals, Gauss quadrature for regular radial integrals, and deterministic angular quadrature; (3) boundary-singularity-aware trial functions enriched with explicit boundary features with automatic selection of the leading exponent; (4) spatiotemporally separable neural networks for time-dependent PDEs that factorize residuals into low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals, integrated with alternating neural network subspace optimization.
AI.result: The proposed fTNN framework attains high accuracy on tested benchmarks and substantially outperforms existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, especially for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.
AI.conclusion: The fully deterministic integration framework combined with singularity-aware trial functions and spatiotemporal factorization provides a powerful and accurate approach for solving fractional PDEs on bounded domains, significantly advancing over stochastic and PINN-based alternatives.

title: TMP: Tree-structured Mixed-policy Pruning for Large-scale Image Generation and Editing
summary: Modern image generation model rapidly grows their sizes to meet high-fidelity image synthesis. However, they gradually become unaffordable for their enormous parameter consumption and computation budget that lead to massive resources requirement and gpu memory footprint. In this paper, we propose TMP, the first Tree-structured Mixed-policy Pruning framework that generalizes prevalent image tasks (T2I and TI2I) and architectures (Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and Diffusion transformer (DiT)). It could be applied to the step-distilled models and contribute as the last stage. We perform experiments upon current open-sourced SOTA HunyuanImage-3.0 instruct and a popular efficient model Z-Image turbo. The proposed pruning framework manages to compress HunyuanImage 3.0 from 80B to 20B parameters at 75% reduction ratio, sacrificing limited generation quality. We also optimize to enable the inference of the pruned 20B version of HunyuanImage 3.0 on a single 24GB 4090 GPU by engineering skills. The inference script and model weight have been integrated into the existing HunyuanImage3.0 open-source github and huggingface repository. Besides, we prove the efficacy of TMP by compressing Z-Image turbo from 6B to 4B (33% reduction) with negligible degradation.

AI.tldr: TMP is the first tree-structured mixed-policy pruning framework for compressing large image generation models (T2I and TI2I) across MoE and DiT architectures, achieving up to 75% parameter reduction (80B→20B) with limited quality loss, and enabling inference on a single 24GB GPU.
AI.motivation: Modern image generation models are growing too large, requiring massive computational resources and GPU memory, making them unaffordable for practical deployment.
AI.method: Proposes TMP, a Tree-structured Mixed-policy Pruning framework that generalizes across T2I/TI2I tasks and MoE/DiT architectures, applicable to step-distilled models as a final-stage compression method.
AI.result: Compressed HunyuanImage 3.0 from 80B to 20B (75% reduction) with limited quality loss, enabled 20B inference on a single 24GB 4090 GPU; compressed Z-Image turbo from 6B to 4B (33% reduction) with negligible degradation.
AI.conclusion: TMP is an effective and generalizable pruning framework for large image generation models, offering significant compression ratios while maintaining generation quality and enabling practical deployment on consumer hardware.

title: When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models
summary:
Multi-model LLM systems such as routing, voting, cascades, fusion, and mixture-of-agents are used to beat single-model accuracy. We show that their gain is capped by a quantity the field rarely reports. For any policy whose output is one member model answer, accuracy cannot exceed one minus beta, where beta is the rate at which every model is wrong on the same query. In contrast, the usual diagnostic, average pairwise error correlation rho, cannot identify beta: error laws with identical marginals and pairwise correlations can have different all-wrong rates. A Clopper-Pearson bound on beta gives a finite-sample certificate on the largest gain any router, vote, or cascade could deliver before training a router.
  Across 67 models from 21 providers, a tetrachoric-calibrated single-factor model still underprices the all-wrong tail: on open-ended mathematics, observed beta is 0.052 versus 0.023 under the full 67-model Gaussian copula, about 2.5 times underpricing, with 90 percent CI 1.7 to 3.4 and k equals 17. The effect recurs on execution-graded code, where beta is 0.079. Re-asking the same GPQA-Diamond questions in free-response rather than multiple-choice form reopens the tail, with beta 0.127 and a five-judge panel with kappa 0.73 to 0.92, locating co-failure in answer format rather than subject. At matched quality, low-rho heterogeneous ensembles beat high-rho Self-MoA, but on checkable tasks in our pool, combining models rarely beats the single best model without a strong query-level routing signal. Gains come from models failing on different questions, not from adding more models.

AI.tldr: Multi-model LLM systems (routing, voting, cascades, fusion, MoA) have accuracy gains capped by the "all-wrong rate" β (fraction of queries where every model is wrong). Standard correlation ρ cannot identify β. Empirical results show β is systematically underestimated by Gaussian copula models. Combining models rarely beats the single best model without strong query-level routing; gains come from models failing on different questions, not from adding more models.
AI.motivation: To identify the fundamental ceiling on multi-model LLM system accuracy—the all-wrong rate β—and show that standard diagnostics (pairwise error correlation ρ) are insufficient for detecting it, leading to over-optimism about multi-model gains.
AI.method: Theoretical derivation of accuracy cap (1 - β) for any policy outputting one member answer. Finite-sample Clopper-Pearson certificate. Empirical analysis across 67 models from 21 providers using tetrachoric-calibrated single-factor model, Gaussian copula, free-response reformulation of GPQA-Diamond, and comparison of low-ρ heterogeneous ensembles vs. high-ρ Self-MoA.
AI.result: Observed β = 0.052 on open-ended math vs. 0.023 under 67-model Gaussian copula (~2.5× underpricing, 90% CI [1.7, 3.4], k=17). On execution-graded code, β = 0.079. Free-response GPQA-Diamond yields β = 0.127. Low-ρ heterogeneous ensembles beat high-ρ Self-MoA at matched quality, but combining models rarely beats the single best model without strong query-level routing.
AI.conclusion: Multi-model system gains are fundamentally bounded by β, not ρ. The all-wrong tail is systematically underestimated by standard copula models. Gains arise from models failing on different questions, not from adding more models; strong query-level routing signals are required to surpass the single best model.

title: Stochastic Gradient Optimization with Model-Assisted Sampling
summary: This work addresses the problem of variance in stochastic gradient estimation for machine learning optimization. Deep learning relies on mini-batch methods such as stochastic gradient descent, which approximate full gradients but introduce noise, creating trade-offs between convergence stability, speed, and generalization. Existing methods, including variance reduction techniques (e.g., SVRG and SAG) and adaptive optimizers, aim to mitigate gradient noise but may introduce additional computational overhead. We propose a model-assisted sampling framework that interprets mini-batch gradients through survey sampling theory, treating the dataset as a fixed finite population and gradients as sample-based estimates. Our aim is to bridge machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory by combining their perspectives on sample-based estimation and variance reduction. By incorporating auxiliary gradient-prediction models, we construct more efficient gradient estimators, with uniform sampling arising as a special case when no auxiliary information is used. Our approach integrates easily with existing optimizers, improving efficiency without altering their dynamics. Empirical results on synthetic and six benchmark datasets show performance gains in 71-86% of the experiments, particularly for medium-sized input spaces in our benchmarks. Notably, with momentum-based optimizers such as AdamW, the proposed estimator achieves clearly better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to baseline estimator.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a model-assisted sampling framework inspired by survey sampling theory to reduce gradient variance in stochastic gradient estimation for deep learning, achieving better generalization with fewer training epochs compared to baseline optimizers like AdamW.
AI.motivation: Variance in mini-batch gradient estimation introduces noise that creates trade-offs between convergence stability, speed, and generalization. Existing methods like variance reduction techniques (SVRG, SAG) and adaptive optimizers mitigate this but may add computational overhead.
AI.method: A model-assisted sampling framework that interprets mini-batch gradients through survey sampling theory, treating the dataset as a fixed finite population. Auxiliary gradient-prediction models are used to construct more efficient gradient estimators, with uniform sampling as a special case.
AI.result: Performance gains in 71-86% of experiments across synthetic and six benchmark datasets, especially for medium-sized input spaces. With momentum-based optimizers like AdamW, the proposed estimator achieves better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to baseline.
AI.conclusion: The approach bridges machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory, integrates easily with existing optimizers without altering their dynamics, and improves efficiency by reducing gradient variance through auxiliary information.

title: FlameVQA: A Physically-Grounded UAV Wildfire VQA Benchmark with Radiometric Thermal Supervision
summary: Wildfire monitoring from UAVs requires reliable reasoning over complex aerial scenes, where smoke, scale variation, and occlusions often limit RGB-only interpretation. We introduce FlameVQA, a multiple-choice visual question answering benchmark for UAV-based wildfire intelligence built on FLAME 3, leveraging paired RGB imagery and radiometric thermal TIFFs for temperature-grounded, safety-critical reasoning. FlameVQA includes 34 multiple-choice questions per image spanning six operational capability groups, covering tasks such as detection, localization, distribution/coverage estimation, cross-modal reasoning, and flight planning. To ensure label reliability, we combine MLLM-assisted annotation with deterministic thermal rules and cross-question consistency checks, followed by human auditing. We also evaluate representative MLLMs on FlameVQA to provide baselines for future work. Results show strong performance when explicit cross-modal cues are available, but notable failures on presence detection under heavy smoke and on coverage estimation. These findings suggest that current MLLMs require domain-specific adaptation to better support disaster and wildfire monitoring. The dataset and benchmark code are open-source at github.com/mobiiin/WildFire_VQA

AI.tldr: FlameVQA is a multiple-choice VQA benchmark for UAV-based wildfire monitoring, built on FLAME 3 with paired RGB and thermal TIFF data. It includes 34 questions per image across 6 operational capability groups, uses MLLM-assisted and deterministic thermal annotation with human auditing, and reveals that current MLLMs struggle with smoke-heavy detection and coverage estimation, requiring domain-specific adaptation.
AI.motivation: Wildfire monitoring from UAVs requires reliable reasoning over complex aerial scenes where smoke, scale variation, and occlusions limit RGB-only interpretation, motivating a benchmark that integrates thermal data for temperature-grounded, safety-critical reasoning.
AI.method: FlameVQA is constructed from the FLAME 3 dataset with paired RGB and radiometric thermal TIFFs. It contains 34 multiple-choice questions per image across 6 operational capability groups (detection, localization, distribution/coverage estimation, cross-modal reasoning, flight planning). Label reliability is ensured via a pipeline combining MLLM-assisted annotation, deterministic thermal rules, cross-question consistency checks, and human auditing.
AI.result: MLLMs show strong performance when explicit cross-modal cues are available, but exhibit notable failures on presence detection under heavy smoke and on coverage estimation.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs require domain-specific adaptation to better support disaster and wildfire monitoring tasks.

title: Language-Based Digital Twins for Elderly Cognitive Assistance
comment: Accepted and published in the Proceedings of the ACM International Conference on PErvasive Technologies Related to Assistive Environments (PETRA 2026). The final published version is available through the ACM Digital Library
summary: Digital twins have emerged as a promising paradigm for personalized healthcare, enabling modeling of individual behavior and health trajectories. In cognitive health, early detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) remains challenging, where language and conversational patterns serve as non-invasive biomarkers. In this work, we propose a language-based digital twin framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to mimic the conversational behavior of elderly individuals by incorporating stylometric cues and contextual metadata. To evaluate fidelity and cognitive consistency, we introduce a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) that jointly measures reconstruction quality and predicts cognitive scores. Experiments on the I-CONECT dataset show that the digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics and achieves reconstruction and MoCA prediction errors comparable to real data, while outperforming baseline GPT-generated responses. These results highlight the potential of language-based digital twins as a scalable and non-invasive approach for personalized and continuous cognitive health monitoring.

AI.tldr: A language-based digital twin framework using LLMs with stylometric cues and contextual metadata to mimic elderly conversational behavior, evaluated via a multi-head cVAE for reconstruction quality and cognitive score prediction, achieving high fidelity on the I-CONECT dataset.
AI.motivation: Early detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) is challenging; language and conversational patterns serve as non-invasive biomarkers, motivating the need for a personalized, scalable digital twin approach for continuous cognitive health monitoring.
AI.method: Proposes a language-based digital twin using LLMs enhanced with stylometric cues and contextual metadata; introduces a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) to jointly measure reconstruction fidelity and predict cognitive scores (e.g., MoCA).
AI.result: On the I-CONECT dataset, the digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics, achieves reconstruction and MoCA prediction errors comparable to real data, and outperforms baseline GPT-generated responses.
AI.conclusion: Language-based digital twins are a scalable, non-invasive approach for personalized and continuous cognitive health monitoring, with potential for early MCI detection.

title: RecallRisk-BERT: A Multi-Task Framework for Post-Report Medical Device Recall Triage
summary: Medical device recalls are a critical regulatory mechanism for protecting patient safety. The growing volume of FDA recall records presents challenges in post-report recall triage, severity assessment, and root-cause interpretation. Existing studies mostly address recall occurrence prediction or root-cause analysis separately, while joint modeling of recall severity and root-cause categories has received limited attention. We develop an automated recall triage framework using 54,165 FDA medical device recall records from openFDA, covering the period from 2002 to October 2025. We first evaluate classical machine learning and boosting-based models for recall severity and root-cause category prediction. We then develop RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model that combines PubMedBERT-based textual representations of recall narratives with embedding-based representations of structured categorical features, including product code, regulation number, and medical specialty. The model simultaneously predicts recall severity (Class I/II/III) and a consolidated root-cause category (9 classes). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, macro-averaged precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. In single-task severity prediction, our LightGBM-based text--tabular configuration achieved the strongest performance, with an accuracy of 0.963, macro-F1 of 0.856, and ROC-AUC of 0.974. In the multi-task setting, RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed the single-task PubMedBERT baseline. Model-derived risk rankings were strongly consistent with observed root-cause severity patterns (rho = 0.983, p = 1.936e-6). These findings indicate that text--tabular learning can support scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and model-based root-cause risk analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model combining PubMedBERT textual representations with structured categorical features to jointly predict recall severity (Class I/II/III) and root-cause categories (9 classes) from 54,165 FDA medical device recall records (2002–2025). LightGBM with text–tabular features achieved 0.963 accuracy and 0.974 ROC-AUC in single-task severity prediction; RecallRisk-BERT outperformed single-task PubMedBERT in multi-task setting with strong risk ranking consistency (rho=0.983).
AI.motivation: Existing studies address recall occurrence prediction or root-cause analysis separately, while joint modeling of recall severity and root-cause categories has received limited attention. The growing volume of FDA recall records presents challenges in post-report recall triage, severity assessment, and root-cause interpretation.
AI.method: Developed RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model combining PubMedBERT-based textual representations of recall narratives with embedding-based representations of structured categorical features (product code, regulation number, medical specialty). Also evaluated classical ML and boosting-based models (e.g., LightGBM with text–tabular configuration). Multi-task setting jointly predicts recall severity (Class I/II/III) and consolidated root-cause category (9 classes).
AI.result: LightGBM-based text–tabular configuration achieved accuracy of 0.963, macro-F1 of 0.856, and ROC-AUC of 0.974 in single-task severity prediction. RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed single-task PubMedBERT baseline in multi-task setting. Model-derived risk rankings were strongly consistent with observed root-cause severity patterns (rho=0.983, p=1.936e-6).
AI.conclusion: Text–tabular learning can support scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and model-based root-cause risk analysis.

title: Safe Autoregressive Image Generation with Iterative Self-Improving Codebooks
comment: 10 pages including references, 8 figures, accepted for publication at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Unlike diffusion-based models that operate in continuous latent spaces, autoregressive unified multimodal models produce images by sequentially predicting discretized visual tokens. These tokens are derived from a codebook that maps embeddings to quantized visual patterns. The language-like architecture enables unified multimodal models to effectively capture text conditional information for generation, making them promising for text-to-image tasks. This also raises an interesting question: how safe are the images generated in such an autoregressive way? In this work, we propose iterative self-improving codebooks for safe autoregressive generation. We leverage the understanding and judgment capabilities of the unified multimodal model itself to identify unsafe generated images without human annotation. Subsequently, the inherent representations in the codebook are fixed to eliminate harmful mappings. Our method comprises two steps: first, we use the unified model to identify unsafe generations and construct corresponding harmful and safe image-text pairs. These pairs are used to construct the Harmful Space and guide updates to the codebook, thereby eliminating harmful outputs. Second, we perform adaptive fine-tuning on the codebook within the harmless space using safe image-text pairs to ensure the quality of generated images. These two steps are repeated until no further improvement is observed, producing a safety-enhanced model codebook. Without additional external feedback, the safety of models is improved iteratively.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an iterative self-improving codebook method for safe autoregressive image generation in unified multimodal models. It uses the model's own understanding to identify unsafe images, constructs harmful/safe image-text pairs to eliminate harmful mappings in the codebook, and adaptively fine-tunes the codebook for quality preservation, iteratively improving safety without external feedback.
AI.motivation: To address safety concerns in autoregressive unified multimodal models for text-to-image generation, specifically regarding how to identify and eliminate unsafe generated images without relying on human annotation.
AI.method: Two-step iterative process: (1) Use the unified model's own understanding and judgment to identify unsafe generated images, construct harmful and safe image-text pairs, build a Harmful Space, and update the codebook to eliminate harmful mappings. (2) Perform adaptive fine-tuning on the codebook within the harmless space using safe image-text pairs to maintain generation quality. Steps repeat until no further improvement.
AI.result: The safety of autoregressive unified multimodal models is improved iteratively without requiring additional external feedback or human annotation, producing a safety-enhanced model codebook.
AI.conclusion: The proposed iterative self-improving codebook approach effectively enhances the safety of autoregressive image generation in unified multimodal models while preserving generation quality, all without external supervision.

title: Automating Potential-based Reward Shaping with Vision Language Model Guidance
summary: Sparse rewards are inherently challenging for reinforcement learning agents as they lack intermediate feedback to guide exploration and to correctly attribute the sparse success rewards to relevant parts of the trajectory. Naive reward shaping can induce reward hacking, yielding policies that exploit auxiliary signals instead of solving the intended task. Potential-based reward shaping (PBRS) guarantees preservation of the optimal policy set, but requires the definition of a heuristic potential function over the state space. In this work, we introduce the VLM-guided PBRS framework VLM-PBRS that learns the potential function directly from vision language model (VLM) feedback. We query a lightweight VLM to obtain preferences over image pairs and train a model of the potential function using these preferences. As this approach is based on potential-based reward shaping, it preserves the original optimal policies, and removes the need for expert-designed reward shaping terms. Because large VLMs are prohibitively expensive to invoke repeatedly during policy learning, we employ smaller, more computationally efficient VLMs. Although the resulting preference labels are less accurate, empirical evidence shows that the preference labels can still be used to accelerate learning. We validate our method empirically in the Meta-World and Franka Kitchen environments and highlight the connection between VLM preference label accuracy and sample efficiency improvements. Our contributions are threefold: (1) the first application of VLM preference-based learning to synthesize a potential function for PBRS, (2) a principled, low-cost solution that leverages small VLMs, and (3) extensive empirical demonstration of improved sample efficiency and robustness to reward hacking.

AI.tldr: VLM-PBRS uses preferences from small vision-language models to learn a potential function for potential-based reward shaping, improving sample efficiency in sparse-reward RL while preserving optimal policy guarantees and reducing reward hacking.
AI.motivation: Sparse rewards in RL lack intermediate feedback, making exploration and credit assignment difficult. Existing reward shaping methods either risk reward hacking (naive shaping) or require handcrafted potential functions (PBRS), limiting scalability.
AI.method: The framework queries a lightweight VLM for pairwise preferences over image trajectories, trains a potential function model from these preferences, and uses it for PBRS. Small VLMs are used for computational efficiency, despite lower label accuracy.
AI.result: Validated in Meta-World and Franka Kitchen environments. Empirical results show improved sample efficiency and robustness to reward hacking, with a demonstrated connection between VLM preference label accuracy and learning acceleration.
AI.conclusion: VLM-PBRS provides a principled, low-cost solution for sparse-reward RL by replacing expert-designed reward shaping terms with VLM-learned potential functions, preserving optimal policies while improving efficiency and robustness.

title: HarmVideoBench: Benchmarking Harmful Video Understanding in Large Multimodal Models
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have recently shown immense potential in automated content moderation, sparking growing interest in developing harmful-video benchmarks. However, we identify two primary limitations in existing works: 1) The multi-layered characteristics of harmful videos are overlooked. Existing benchmarks predominantly formulate evaluation as a binary classification task, failing to capture implicit or deep contextual harms. 2) Explanatory rationales are completely absent. Current frameworks measure exclusively whether a model flags a video correctly rather than explaining why, turning evaluation into a black box where models can succeed through superficial shortcuts. To address these problems, we present HarmVideoBench, a multi-layered diagnostic benchmark comprising 1,379 videos paired with 4,137 multiple-choice questions. HarmVideoBench benchmarks three hierarchical dimensions: Observable Evidence, Clip-Internal Meaning, and Beyond-Clip Reasoning, aiming to evaluate models' deep understanding beyond surface cues with carefully balanced and curated samples. We evaluate 19 leading models on HarmVideoBench to assess their multidimensional understanding of harmful videos. Moreover, we introduce BCR, a benchmark-aligned method that predicts reasoning boundaries and dynamically retrieves context only when needed. Experimental results show that BCR substantially improves the base model's performance in harmful video understanding, raising the macro average from 61.7 percent to a state-of-the-art 84.4 percent.

AI.tldr: HarmVideoBench is a multi-layered diagnostic benchmark with 1,379 harmful videos and 4,137 multiple-choice questions that evaluates LVLMs across three hierarchical dimensions (Observable Evidence, Clip-Internal Meaning, Beyond-Clip Reasoning), accompanied by BCR, a method that improves harmful video understanding accuracy from 61.7% to 84.4% by predicting reasoning boundaries and dynamically retrieving context.
AI.motivation: Existing harmful-video benchmarks for LVLMs have two limitations: they treat evaluation as binary classification (missing implicit/deep contextual harms) and lack explanatory rationales, turning model evaluation into a black box where models can succeed via superficial shortcuts.
AI.method: The authors construct HarmVideoBench with 1,379 videos and 4,137 multiple-choice questions organized into three hierarchical dimensions: Observable Evidence (surface-level cues), Clip-Internal Meaning (context within the clip), and Beyond-Clip Reasoning (external knowledge inference). They also propose BCR (Benchmark-aligned Context Retrieval), which predicts reasoning boundaries and dynamically retrieves additional context only when needed.
AI.result: Evaluation of 19 LVLMs shows that BCR substantially improves base model performance on harmful video understanding, raising macro average accuracy from 61.7% to 84.4%, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: HarmVideoBench provides a more comprehensive evaluation framework for harmful video understanding, and BCR demonstrates that dynamic context retrieval guided by reasoning boundaries can significantly enhance LVLM performance on this task.

title: Explaining Temporal Graph Neural Networks via Feature-induced Information Flow
summary: Event-based Temporal Graph Neural Networks (ETGNNs) have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of applications, including social network analysis, epidemic tracing, recommender systems, and political event forecasting. However, their increasing complexity poses significant challenges for explainability. Existing explanation methods focus only on a subset of the information flow within ETGNNs, typically tracing contributions from the event-related embeddings to the output. Consequently, they overlook the important pathways through event-induced variables, which mediate interactions between nodes and thereby play a central role in capturing long-range temporal dependencies. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel attribution method that analyzes the \emph{entire} information flow through all event-associated variables. Our method is built upon the recent Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework, which enables explicit quantification of information flow originating from event embeddings as well as information flow passing through event-induced variables. It also ensures comparability of latent variables across layers, and supports higher-order analysis of interactions between events. To handle the architectural complexity of ETGNNs, we extend the NRM framework with a modular decomposition procedure that facilitates the systematic construction of relevance structure for complex neural architectures. We evaluate our approach on two synthetic datasets for epidemic tracing and social dynamics, as well as a real-world dataset of political event networks. Our qualitative and quantitative experiments show that our method consistently outperforms existing explanation approaches while producing more human-interpretable explanations.

AI.tldr: A novel attribution method for Event-based Temporal Graph Neural Networks (ETGNNs) that captures the entire information flow through all event-associated variables, including overlooked event-induced pathways mediating long-range temporal dependencies, built on an extended Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework with modular decomposition.
AI.motivation: Existing explanation methods for ETGNNs focus only on a subset of information flow (event-related embeddings to output), overlooking event-induced variables that mediate node interactions and capture long-range temporal dependencies.
AI.method: Built upon the Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework, extended with a modular decomposition procedure for systematic construction of relevance structures, enabling quantification of information flow from both event embeddings and event-induced variables, comparability of latent variables across layers, and higher-order interaction analysis.
AI.result: Outperforms existing explanation approaches on synthetic datasets (epidemic tracing, social dynamics) and a real-world political event network dataset, producing more human-interpretable explanations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method addresses a critical limitation in ETGNN explainability by covering the entire information flow through all event-associated variables, achieving superior performance and interpretability.

title: LISA: Likelihood Score Alignment for Visual-condition Controllable Generation
summary: The prevalent dual-branch paradigm, i.e., training a side network to encode visual conditions and fusing its intermediate-layer features to a frozen pretrained main network, has shown remarkable success in visual-condition controllable generation. Despite its widespread adoption, the role of the side branch and its training efficiency remain underexplored. In this paper, we first revisit this mainstream paradigm through the lens of score-based generative modeling: 1) The main network preserves visual perceptual quality by providing a prior unconditional score. 2) The side network steers conditional control by implicitly contributing a likelihood score. Guided by this perspective, we propose LIkelihood Score Alignment (LISA), an effective regularization method that explicitly aligns the intermediate feature of the side network with an approximated likelihood score. Specifically, we first hook features from a designated layer of the side network and project them into the score latent space by a lightweight decoder. Then, we construct an approximated likelihood score target and calculate the distance between the decoder's output and this target as an additional regularization loss. Finally, we jointly optimize the side network and decoder with both standard diffusion loss and our regularization loss. Experiments across various image/video tasks, architectures, and diffusion/flow models demonstrated that LISA can not only consistently accelerate the training convergence and improve final synthetic results, but also encourage the side network's features to be more disentangled for conditional modeling with negligible additional training cost and zero extra inference cost.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes LISA (LIkelihood Score Alignment), a regularization method that aligns side network features with likelihood scores to accelerate training and improve results in dual-branch controllable generation, without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: The dual-branch paradigm (side network + frozen pretrained main network) is widely used in visual-condition controllable generation, but the role of the side branch and its training efficiency remain underexplored.
AI.method: LISA revisits the dual-branch paradigm through score-based generative modeling: the main network provides a prior unconditional score, while the side network contributes a likelihood score. The method hooks features from a designated side network layer, projects them via a lightweight decoder into score latent space, constructs an approximated likelihood score target, computes a regularization loss as the distance between decoder output and target, and jointly optimizes the side network and decoder with both standard diffusion loss and the regularization loss.
AI.result: Experiments across various image/video tasks, architectures, and diffusion/flow models show LISA consistently accelerates training convergence, improves final synthetic results, encourages more disentangled side network features for conditional modeling, with negligible additional training cost and zero extra inference cost.
AI.conclusion: LISA is an effective regularization method that explicitly aligns intermediate features of the side network with an approximated likelihood score, improving efficiency and quality of dual-branch controllable generation without incurring inference overhead.

title: Graph Neural Networks Applications Across Domains: All Insights You Need
summary: Graph neural networks have moved from a niche representation-learning technique to the default model class wherever data carry relational structure. The interesting question is no longer whether message passing helps on a given dataset, but where graph structure earns its computational cost and where it does not. This survey organises the field around a single design space, derives the spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, and connects expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy with explicit statements of what current architectures can and cannot separate. Against that methodological backbone we examine twelve application domains, among them recommendation and social networks, knowledge graphs and language-model integration, drug discovery and molecular property learning, healthcare and neuroscience, computer vision, traffic and urban computing, power and renewable-energy systems, wireless and sixth-generation networks, fraud and cybersecurity, industrial prognostics, materials science, and climate modelling. For each domain we specify the graph-construction choices and their costs, identify which architecture families dominate and why, and separate reported gains from artefacts of weak baselines or favourable splits. A cross-domain comparison exposes recurring patterns: heterophily and scale undercut the same models almost everywhere, temporal graphs remain harder than their static counterparts, and the architectures that top public leaderboards are seldom the ones that reach deployment. We treat over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability not as a closing checklist but as the constraints that decide adoption.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey organizing graph neural networks around a unified design space, connecting spectral and spatial formulations from first principles, and evaluating twelve application domains to reveal where graph structure earns its computational cost.
AI.motivation: To move beyond the question of whether message passing helps on a given dataset, and instead determine where graph structure earns its computational cost and where it does not, by systematically examining trade-offs across diverse application domains.
AI.method: Organizes the field around a single design space, derives spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, connects expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy, and examines twelve application domains through consistent criteria: graph-construction choices and costs, dominant architecture families and why, and separating genuine gains from weak baseline artefacts.
AI.result: Identifies recurring cross-domain patterns: heterophily and scale undercut the same models across domains, temporal graphs remain harder than static ones, and leaderboard-topping architectures seldom reach deployment. Treats over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability as adoption-deciding constraints rather than a closing checklist.
AI.conclusion: The survey provides a structured framework for practitioners to decide when graph structure earns its cost, revealing that architectural choices are highly domain-dependent and that practical deployment constraints often outweigh benchmark performance.

title: SatSplatDiff: Geometry-preserving generative refinement for high-fidelity satellite Gaussian Splatting
summary: Gaussian Splatting has been recently explored for satellite 3D reconstruction, demonstrating flexibility and efficiency in representing radiometrically diverse satellite scenes. However, the limited top viewpoint of satellite imagery results in insufficient supervision on building facades, leaving surface holes and degraded visual fidelity. Generative refinement, which leverages pretrained generative priors to iteratively refine and update the rendered images used as supervision targets, has recently been investigated to improve the visual fidelity of Gaussian-rendered images. However, since these models refine each view independently, the resulting images can generate hallucinations and break photo-consistency, leading to geometric degradation. To address these limitations, we propose SatSplatDiff, which aims to minimize geometric degradation prevalent in generative refinement. Building on photogrammetric DSM initialization and 2DGS-based shadow casting established in our prior work SatSplat, we first introduce monocular depth supervision and multi-scale geometric refinement to establish a geometrically accurate and well-regularized surface representation. We then apply shadow-guided generative refinement, where geometrically calculated shadow maps guide the Gaussians to maintain consistency with the underlying geometry, improving visual fidelity while reducing geometric degradation. Extensive evaluations on the IARPA2016 and DFC2019 datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, reducing geometric MAE by up to 18% and improving visual fidelity (FID-CLIP) by 28-45% over existing baselines. Our method delivers up to 5x resolution enhancement with minimal hallucination and sensor-consistent appearance, demonstrating seamless cross-tile consistency and strong scalability for large-scale reconstruction. Source code is available at https://github.com/GDAOSU/SatSplatDiff

AI.tldr: SatSplatDiff improves satellite 3D reconstruction by combining geometrically accurate Gaussian splatting (via DSM initialization, monocular depth supervision, multi-scale refinement) with shadow-guided generative refinement that maintains photo-consistency, reducing geometric MAE by 18% and improving visual fidelity by 28-45% on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing Gaussian Splatting for satellite 3D reconstruction suffers from limited top viewpoints causing insufficient building facade supervision and surface holes. Generative refinement applied independently per view introduces hallucinations and breaks photo-consistency, leading to geometric degradation.
AI.method: Building on SatSplat (DSM initialization + 2DGS shadow casting), SatSplatDiff introduces: (1) monocular depth supervision and multi-scale geometric refinement for accurate surface representation; (2) shadow-guided generative refinement where geometrically computed shadow maps enforce consistency between rendered images and underlying geometry.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on IARPA2016 and DFC2019: geometric MAE reduced up to 18%, visual fidelity (FID-CLIP) improved by 28-45%, up to 5x resolution enhancement with minimal hallucination and sensor-consistent appearance.
AI.conclusion: SatSplatDiff effectively minimizes geometric degradation from generative refinement by integrating geometric priors and shadow-guided consistency, achieving high-quality, scalable satellite 3D reconstruction with cross-tile consistency.

title: The Geometry of Updates: Fisher Alignment at Vocabulary Scale
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026), PMLR 306. 64 pages total (main paper plus appendix), 4 figures, 29 tables
summary: Training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies arises in scientific string domains such as SMILES, protein, and genomic sequences, where candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. This creates an activation-dark regime: representation-similarity metrics can be uninformative without assumptions about label-conditioned error geometry, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale. We show that, in a shared-output head setting, representation metrics (e.g., CKA) are non-identifiable for transfer; models can share identical representations yet have orthogonal head updates. The key identity is that head Fisher alignment is exactly a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, exposing activation, error, and coupling factors rather than requiring a materialized Fisher matrix. FisherSketch estimates this cosine directly in a single streaming pass, making K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical with a 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and a 192 KB per-task streaming state, small enough to store next to a model hash, but encoding transfer-relevant update structure. Beyond source selection, the same signatures and marginals provide a diagnostic instrument for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling; shared-parameter and internal-layer validations, together with Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments, show that FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks.

AI.tldr: FisherSketch enables training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies by efficiently estimating head Fisher alignment via a single streaming pass, requiring only 16-192 KB per task, and remains informative when activation-based metrics (e.g., CKA) are non-identifiable.
AI.motivation: Training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies is needed in scientific string domains (SMILES, protein, genomic sequences), where candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. Existing representation-similarity metrics (e.g., CKA) are uninformative in this activation-dark regime, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale.
AI.method: FisherSketch estimates the cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, which exactly equals head Fisher alignment. This avoids materializing the Fisher matrix; instead, it computes the alignment directly via a single streaming pass, producing a compact task signature (16 KB for m=4096) and streaming state (192 KB).
AI.result: FisherSketch makes K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical with minimal storage (16 KB task signature, 192 KB streaming state). Shared-parameter and internal-layer validations, plus Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments, demonstrate that FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity (CKA) cannot distinguish tasks.
AI.conclusion: FisherSketch provides an efficient, scalable method for source selection in shared-vocabulary LLM families. Its signatures and marginals also serve as a diagnostic tool for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling, proving effective even when activation-based metrics fail.

title: CORTEX: A Structured Reasoning Benchmark for Trustworthy 3D Chest CT MLLMs
summary: Reasoning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has shown strong promise in medical imaging. However, this reasoning is usually free-form text judged only by its final answer, making it hard to interpret and verify, especially in 3D radiology, where a diagnosis should be traceable to evidence in the scan. Existing chest CT question-answering datasets compound this by reducing expert radiology reports to answer-only pairs, dropping the reasoning that links findings to conclusions and omitting the patient history clinicians rely on. As a result, reasoning-capable 3D chest CT MLLMs remain out of reach, as neither the structured supervision needed to train them nor the protocol needed to verify their reasoning yet exists. We introduce CORTEX (Clinically Organized Reasoning and sTructured EXplanation), a structured reasoning benchmark for 3D chest CT. For each question, CORTEX restores the missing reasoning as a four-stage diagnostic trace mirroring a radiologist's workflow: task understanding, visual observation, diagnostic reasoning, and answer synthesis. We generate these traces using frontier large language models with broad medical and general-domain knowledge, then filter and verify them with a stage-level evaluation protocol combining automated rubric scoring with expert radiologist review. Crucially, both the reasoning structure and evaluation rubrics are designed in close collaboration with clinicians. Built on CT-RATE, a large, publicly available chest CT dataset without reasoning annotations, CORTEX comprises 76,177 validated reasoning traces across open-ended VQA, closed-ended VQA, and report generation, providing both the structured supervision and the stage-level evaluation protocol needed to build and evaluate trustworthy reasoning models for 3D chest CT. Our dataset and evaluation code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: CORTEX is a structured reasoning benchmark for 3D chest CT that introduces a four-stage diagnostic trace (task understanding, visual observation, diagnostic reasoning, answer synthesis) to provide both structured supervision and stage-level evaluation for multimodal LLMs, comprising 76,177 validated reasoning traces across VQA and report generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM reasoning in medical imaging lacks interpretability and verifiability, especially in 3D radiology; current chest CT QA datasets drop reasoning traces and patient history, making it impossible to train or verify reasoning-capable 3D models.
AI.method: CORTEX constructs a four-stage diagnostic trace (task understanding, visual observation, diagnostic reasoning, answer synthesis) using frontier LLMs with medical knowledge, then filters/verifies traces via a stage-level evaluation protocol combining automated rubric scoring and expert radiologist review, all designed in collaboration with clinicians.
AI.result: 76,177 validated reasoning traces across open-ended VQA, closed-ended VQA, and report generation tasks, built on the CT-RATE dataset.
AI.conclusion: CORTEX provides both the structured supervision and stage-level evaluation protocol necessary to build and evaluate trustworthy reasoning models for 3D chest CT, addressing a critical gap in interpretable medical MLLM reasoning.

title: Effective Covariance Dynamics in Solvable High-Dimensional GANs
summary: We study a solvable high-dimensional model of generative adversarial network (GAN) training in which a linear generator learns a low-dimensional subspace from data with structured latent covariance. Prior solvable GAN analyses assume unconditional signals with diagonal latent covariance; we extend the multi-feature discriminator setting to class-dependent, correlated, and non-zero-mean latent structure. For the quadratic energy discriminator, all such heterogeneity enters the dynamics through a probability-weighted effective second moment.
